complexity_delta.note: Asymptotics in N are unchanged (trial division dominates), but dependence on M changes from linear to effectively constant because sum_e e is tiny. For typical constraints (M up to ~1e5, sum_e <= ~60), the factorial-table approach does much more work than needed, while the direct combination approach performs only a few big-integer operations.

trigger_signals: Only a single test case; no reuse of factorial tables across queries.
The binomial coefficients required have the special form C(M + e - 1, e) where e (exponent) is very small.
The number of distinct prime factors of N is small, so the total number of binomial evaluations is tiny.
Profile or reasoning shows the O(M) factorial precomputation dominates runtime and memory, while combination evaluation itself is not the real bottleneck.
transformation_steps: Eliminated global factorial and inverse-factorial arrays and their O(M) precomputation.
Replaced nCk via factorial/inverse-factorial with a direct multiplicative formula that loops only over k = exponent(e) for each prime.
Deferred modular reduction until after combining all prime-factor contributions, reducing the number of modular operations.
Retained simple trial-division factorization, which is already asymptotically optimal for these constraints in Python.

bottlenecks.detail: Building factorial and inverse-factorial arrays up to M + max_exponent is O(M) work and memory for a single test case, even though only a handful of binomial coefficients with very small k (the prime exponents) are needed.

Every factorial step is a modular multiplication, and one modular exponentiation is used for computing the inverse factorial at the top. These are relatively heavy compared to a few small-k combinations.
Two arrays of size O(M + max_exponent) (factorial and inverse factorial) are stored, which is unnecessary for a single-query scenario with very small k.

from.time: O(N + sqrt(M)) with N implicitly tied to factorial precomputation size.
from.space: O(N) for factorial and inverse tables.

complexity_delta.note: The key improvement is removing all dependence on N from precomputation, making the solution viable for N up to ~1e9; time and space now depend only on M via sqrt(M) and log(M).

trigger_signals: Problem constraints specify N (first argument in combinations) up to 1e9 or similarly large, making factorial precomputation up to N impossible.
Memory usage projections show factorial/inverse arrays of size N+O(1) would exceed available memory.
Time complexity analysis of precomputation O(N) is incompatible with strict time limits (e.g., 1–2 seconds) for large N.
The number of required nCr evaluations is very small (O(log M)), and k (exponent) is tiny, indicating that per-query multiplicative computation is cheap and precomputation is unnecessary.
Existing solution works only when hardcoded MAXN (e.g., 2e5) covers all test cases; failing or wrong answers appear when N exceeds this bound.
transformation_steps: Remove global factorial / inverse / inverse-factorial precomputation arrays that scale up to MAXN ≈ N.
Replace nCr computation via precomputed tables with a direct multiplicative formula for C(N+e-1, e) = Π_{j=1}^{e} (N+e-j)/j using Python big integers.

Delay modulo operations until after each full combination value is computed, multiplying into the global answer and reducing modulo 1e9+7 there; this keeps intermediate logic simple and avoids needing modular inverses.
Keep the prime factorization step as simple trial division up to sqrt(M), using a while-based loop that shrinks M as factors are divided out, maintaining O(sqrt(M)) complexity.
Rely on the small size of each exponent e (sum of exponents ≤ log_2 M) to ensure the multiplicative combination computation is fast.

bottlenecks.detail: Factorial and inverse precomputation is O(MAXN). If N is as large as 1e9 (as per typical problem constraints), this becomes infeasible in Python (and in general) and dwarfs the sqrt(M) factorization cost.
Storing three integer arrays (factorial, inverse, inverse-factorial) of size MAXN+1 requires O(MAXN) memory. For N up to 1e9, this is impossible; even N ~ 1e7 is already too large in Python.

Code hardcodes MAXN = 2*10^5+10. This only works if all needed n in nCr(n,k) are <= 2e5. For the actual problem, n = N + exponent - 1 can be up to ~1e9, so the code becomes incorrect for large N. To fix correctness, MAXN must scale with N, which triggers the time and memory bottlenecks above.

Precomputing full factorial tables up to MAXN is overkill when only O(log M) many combinations with small k (exponents <= ~30) are needed. This wastes time relative to the actual number of combinatorial evaluations performed.

complexity_delta.from: O(sqrt(M) + N + log M) time, O(N) space
complexity_delta.to: O(sqrt(M) + log M) time, O(#primes) space
complexity_delta.note: Because the combinatorial workload is intrinsically O(log M), any O(N) preprocessing (factorial tables) is asymptotically and practically wasteful when N can be large.

trigger_signals: Observed runtime dominated by loops up to N (building factorials) even though only a handful of binomial coefficients are required.
Profiling or simple logging would show most time spent in factorial precomputation rather than in factorization or final multiplications.
Problem constraints and math analysis reveal that the number of distinct primes and their exponents is very small compared to N.
Memory usage grows linearly with N due to factorial arrays, which is unnecessary for an answer depending only on a few exponents.
transformation_steps: Retain the trial-division factorization logic (func_1 / func_2’s core idea): factor M once into prime exponents.
Remove global factorial and inverse-factorial arrays and all O(N) precomputation loops.
Replace table-based binomial computation with a per-call multiplicative nCr implementation that iterates only over k = exponent, exploiting k ≪ N.
Apply the modulus only to the final binomial result (and the accumulated product), instead of per-step modular multiplications inside large O(N) loops.
Keep storage minimal: track only prime exponents in a dictionary rather than arrays sized by N.

bottlenecks.detail: The code precomputes factorials and inverse factorials up to N + max_exp even though it only needs O(#primes) binomial coefficients, each with small k = exponent ≤ O(log M). This turns an inherently tiny combinatorial workload into an O(N) operation.

Using global factorial tables for nCr is beneficial when many binomial coefficients with large n, k are needed. Here, the number of combinations equals the number of distinct prime factors of M (usually very small), and each k is small. Per-call multiplicative nCr is cheaper than building tables up to N.

Allocating two arrays of length N + max_exp (var_8 and var_9) costs O(N) memory. For large N (e.g., 1e5 or 1e6), this increases memory footprint and cache pressure, slowing execution compared to the O(1) working memory of the fast version.

The call var_9[var_4] = func_3(var_8[var_4], mod=var_12) does not match func_3’s signature (second parameter is named var_12, not mod). In real execution this would raise a TypeError, but even ignoring this, the overall structure is still slower than the fast version.

complexity_delta.from: O(N + sqrt(M)) time, O(N) space
complexity_delta.to: O(sqrt(M)) time, O(#prime_factors) space
complexity_delta.note: Precomputing factorials and modular inverses up to N+100 is removed; binomial coefficients are computed per-use with a multiplicative formula. Improvement is significant when N is large (e.g., N ≫ sqrt(M)) and there is only a single query.

trigger_signals: Presence of a loop precomputing factorials/inverses up to `var_2 + 100` for a single (N, M) input suggests unnecessary O(N) overhead.

Use of factorial-based modular combinations where the required combination parameters (exponents) are very small compared to N indicates that a direct multiplicative formula or on-demand computation would be cheaper.
The fact that only a handful of combinations are computed (one per distinct prime factor of M) signals that investing in large precomputation may be wasteful.
Factorization result is small (few prime factors of M <= 1e9), so using Counter or simple counting on a list is sufficient and avoids more complex structures.
Memory footprint N-sized tables (var_6, var_7, var_9) for a problem that only needs O(#prime_factors) information is a classic sign that the approach can be made more space efficient.
transformation_steps: Prime factorization representation change: Instead of returning (prime, exponent) pairs, the fast version returns a flat list of prime factors (with repetition) and then uses collections.Counter to recover exponents. This simplifies factorization storage and lets Counter handle exponent counting.

Combination computation strategy change: Replaced precomputed factorial/inverse factorial tables plus O(1) modular nCk with a direct multiplicative formula: - Compute numerator as product(n, n−1, ..., n−k+1). - Compute denominator as product(1, 2, ..., k). - Return numerator // denominator, then apply modulo after multiplying into the global product. This removes O(N) setup and uses only O(k) operations per combination.

Precomputation removal: Deleted arrays var_6, var_7, var_9 and the loop `for var_8 in range(2, var_2 + 100 + 1)` that prepared factorials and modular inverses, substantially reducing both time and memory usage for large N.

Control-flow simplification for special case M=1: The fast version checks `if var_4 == 1: print(1); exit()` before factorization, handling the trivial case without invoking any heavier logic; func_2’s `if var_6 == 1: return [1]` is effectively redundant but not harmful because func_2 will not be called with 1 in the main flow.

Arithmetic and modular handling: The slow version keeps all arithmetic under modulo 1e9+7 via precomputed factorials/inverses, keeping integers small. The fast version performs exact integer arithmetic in func_1 and only applies modulo when accumulating into the result. This trades some big-integer cost for the removal of O(N) precomputation.

bottlenecks.detail: Loop `for var_8 in range(2, var_2 + 100 + 1)` builds factorial and inverse tables of size Θ(N). For a single query and small exponents, this O(N) precompute dominates when N is large compared to sqrt(M).
Building var_9 as a modular inverse table is O(N) and stored in memory, even though only a handful of combinations are needed.

Factorization via `for var_8 in range(2, int(-(-(var_11**0.5) // 1)) + 1)` is O(sqrt(M)). This is acceptable for M up to around 1e9, but still a nontrivial term compared to the very light work done after factorization.

complexity_delta.note: Given N up to ~1e9 and M up to ~1e9, the original approach is dominated by O(N) precomputation and O(N) memory, which is infeasible. The optimized approach eliminates dependence on N in both time and space, relying only on small exponents from factorization of M.

trigger_signals: Detection that array sizes depend on N (var_4 = N + max_exponent) where N can be as large as 1e9, implying impossible memory and runtime requirements.
Recognition that precomputation of factorials is only beneficial when many nCr queries are needed or when N is moderately sized, neither of which holds here.
Awareness that the sum of exponents in the prime factorization of M is bounded by O(log M), making small-k combinatorics feasible via direct multiplication.
Typical constraints and editorial knowledge for this pattern (Factorization problem) indicating the intended solution avoids O(N)-sized arrays.
transformation_steps: Change prime factorization output: instead of listing each prime factor with multiplicity, return (prime, exponent) pairs so that exponents e are directly available.
Observe that for M <= 1e9, each exponent e in the factorization is at most about 30, meaning combinations C(e + N - 1, e) involve a very small k.

Replace global factorial and inverse factorial precomputation (O(N) time and space) with a local multiplicative nCr implementation that computes C(n, k) via a short loop in O(k) time with integer arithmetic.
Remove modular inverse computations and factorial tables entirely; instead, compute exact binomial coefficients as integers and apply the modulo only when combining into the final answer.

Keep the trial-division factorization as-is in terms of asymptotics, but adjust it to accumulate exponents (var_6) for each prime and append (prime, exponent) rather than duplicating primes per factor.

bottlenecks.detail: Two full linear passes to build factorial and inverse factorial arrays of length N + max_exponent lead to O(N) modular multiplications, which is infeasible for N as large as 1e9.
Allocating arrays var_9 and var_10 of size N + max_exponent (potentially ~1e9 elements) far exceeds memory limits and can cause memory errors or severe paging.

The nCr implementation is designed for large N and K with many queries (precompute factorials), but the actual problem has very small K (exponents e <= ~30) and only a handful of combinations, making the precomputation strategy unnecessary and overly expensive.

complexity_delta.note: Asymptotically both are dominated by sqrt(M) for the given constraints, but removing the O(2e5) factorial/inverse precomputation significantly reduces constant factors and memory usage. The new combination routine is O(e) where exponents e are at most ~30 for M ≤ 1e9, so the overhead is negligible.

trigger_signals: The binomial coefficient is only ever called with parameters of the form (N-1+e, e) where e is derived from the exponent of a prime factor of M, and M is relatively small (≈1e9), implying e is always small.
The code performs a large fixed-size factorial precomputation (2e5) even though it only needs a handful of combinations for a single test case.
Memory footprint (arrays of size 2e5) is disproportionate to the real combinatorial work required (a few small-e combinations).
Problem constraints indicate many operations on modest-sized integers but not a high volume of binomial queries; optimizing per-query speed via precomputation is unnecessary.
transformation_steps: Removed global precomputation of factorials, modular inverses, and inverse factorials up to a fixed bound (2*10^5).

Replaced the O(1)-per-query modular binomial using precomputed factorials with a direct integer binomial computation func_1(a, b) based on multiplicative products of length b (where b is the exponent e of a prime factor).
Simplified the prime factorization routine to a standard while-loop with condition var_6 * var_6 <= var_8, eliminating floating-point sqrt and ceiling operations.
Changed the representation of factorization output: instead of (prime, exponent) pairs, func_2 now returns a flat list of prime factors and uses collections.Counter to obtain exponents.
Applied the modulo 1e9+7 only to the final product accumulation, relying on Python big integers for intermediate binomial computations.

bottlenecks.detail: Factorials, modular inverses, and inverse factorials are computed up to a hard-coded limit 2*10^5 even though only a handful of combinations with small extra exponent e are needed.
The upper limit 2*10^5 is independent of the input and may be much larger than N + max_exponent(M), causing needless O(2e5) modular multiplications.
Prime factorization uses int(-(-(var_11**0.5) // 1)) to get ceil(sqrt(n)), introducing floating-point sqrt and ceiling logic instead of a simple integer while-loop bound.

complexity_delta.from: O(N + sqrt(M) + k log MOD) time, O(N) space
complexity_delta.to: O(sqrt(M) + log M) time, O(log M) space
complexity_delta.note: The N-dependent factorial precomputation is removed; combination computation cost now depends only on small exponents of prime factors, not on N. For typical constraints (N up to 1e4, M up to 1e9), the sqrt(M) term dominates and the new method removes the unnecessary O(N) work and O(N) memory.

trigger_signals: Presence of a factorial precomputation up to N where N can be large, even though each required combination depends only on small parameters (like exponents of prime factors).
Use of modular inverses (pow(x, MOD-2, MOD)) for every binomial coefficient when the number or size of combinations is small enough for direct integer arithmetic.
Memory footprint dominated by large factorial tables (lists sized O(N)) when only a handful of combinations are needed.
Performance profiling or timeouts showing significant time spent in factorial precomputation or pow with exponent MOD-2.
transformation_steps: Remove global factorial precomputation (func_3) and the factorial array var_8; avoid storing all factorials up to N+100.

Replace modular-inverse-based binomial computation with a direct multiplicative combination function func_1 that computes C(n, k) using integer arithmetic: multiply descending n..n-k+1 and divide by 1..k, using k = min(k, n-k) to keep loops short.
Keep prime factorization via trial division but store exponents in a dictionary mapping prime -> exponent (func_2 in fast_code), simplifying later iteration over exponents.
Compute the final answer as a product over primes of C(exponent + N - 1, exponent), then take the modulo once at the end of the accumulation (or occasionally, as needed).

bottlenecks.detail: Factorial list var_8 is precomputed up to N + 100 regardless of how small the combination parameters are, incurring O(N) time and memory even though the total exponents of prime factors of M are small (<= ~30 for M <= 1e9).

Each binomial coefficient uses modular inverses computed via pow(x, MOD-2, MOD), which entails O(log MOD) modular multiplications; although individually fast, this is still more expensive than necessary for tiny k when using integer arithmetic without repeated modular inverses.
Use of global arrays (var_8, var_7, var_10) and separate helper calls introduces indirection; not a big-O issue, but can add constant-factor overhead versus a more localized approach.

complexity_delta.from: O(L + sqrt(M)) time, O(L) space with L = 2*10^5 due to factorial/inverse precomputation.
complexity_delta.to: O(sqrt(M) + log M) time, O(log M) space, dominated by trial division and small combinational products.
complexity_delta.note: For single-query problems with few combination evaluations, removing the O(L) precomputation substantially reduces runtime and memory. If many queries or larger N were involved, the original precomputed factorial approach could be preferable.

trigger_signals: Presence of a large fixed-range loop (2 to 2*10^5) doing modular arithmetic before any input-dependent heavy computation.
Very small number of actual binomial-coefficient queries (bounded by the number of distinct prime factors of M).
Single test case with no reuse of precomputed factorial/inverse data.
Factorial precomputation limit tightly set to 2*10^5, hinting that it was chosen for safety rather than an exact requirement.
transformation_steps: Removed global factorial, inverse factorial, and inverse arrays (`var_6`, `var_7`, `var_9`) and their O(L) precomputation loop.
Replaced O(1) table-based `nCk` computation with `func_2`, which computes C(n, k) directly via a multiplicative formula using integer arithmetic and then takes modulo at the end.
Kept trial-division prime factorization but reimplemented as `func_1` that returns a dict {prime: exponent} instead of a list of pairs.
Adjusted the combination parameters to use C(exponent + N - 1, exponent) instead of C(exponent + N - 1, N - 1); these are mathematically equivalent but better match the new `func_2` interface.
Reduced space usage by storing only the prime factor map and a few scalars, instead of large auxiliary arrays.

bottlenecks.detail: Precomputes factorials and modular inverse factorials up to 2*10^5 even though only O(log M) binomial coefficients are needed for a single test case.
Stores three arrays (factorial, inverse factorial, modular inverses) of length ~2e5, which increases memory usage and cache footprint unnecessarily.

Linear-time modular inverse sieve `var_9.append((-var_9[var_10 % var_8] * (var_10 // var_8)) % var_10)` adds expensive modular arithmetic per iteration, dominating runtime compared to the small amount of actual combinatorial work.

complexity_delta.from: O(P + sqrt(V)) time, O(P) space with P = 2*10^5 for factorial and inverse tables in the slow version.
complexity_delta.to: O(sqrt(V)) time, O(log V) space in the fast version, since combinations are computed on-the-fly with only small O(k) loops per prime exponent.
complexity_delta.note: As P is a fixed large constant chosen as a safety upper bound rather than a tight requirement, removing the O(P) precomputation significantly reduces runtime and memory traffic for the actual input sizes used.

trigger_signals: The number of combination evaluations equals the number of distinct prime factors of var_1, which is very small (O(log var_1)), so heavy precomputation for many possible nCk queries is unnecessary.
The precomputation bound for factorials (2*10^5) is far larger than any actual n involved in combinations for typical inputs, indicating wasted work.
Running-time profiling or intuition shows a large fixed startup cost regardless of var_1’s size, pointing to precomputation as the dominant cost.
Memory use includes multiple large arrays despite the logical problem needing only a few integer values per prime factor, suggesting over-allocation.
transformation_steps: Remove global factorial / inverse / inverse-factorial precomputation loops (arrays var_6, var_7, var_9) which were sized up to 2*10^5.

Replace table-based combination computation func_1(n, k, mod) that uses precomputed factorials and inverse factorials with a direct multiplicative implementation func_1(n, k) that computes C(n, k) by multiplying n*(n-1)*... and dividing by k! in small loops of length k.

Keep trial-division prime factorization but simplify its output representation: the fast version returns the multiset of prime factors and then compresses it via Counter to obtain exponents, instead of constructing a list of [prime, exponent] pairs during factorization.

Defer modular reduction until after the aggregate product of combinations is computed, exploiting Python's big integers; this avoids per-operation modular overhead but relies on the fact that the total bit-length remains manageable for these constraints.

bottlenecks.detail: The factorial and modular-inverse tables (var_6, var_7, var_9) are built up to 2*10^5 even though only O(log var_1) combinations are computed and each combination’s n = var_2 + exponent - 1 might be far below that bound. This adds a fixed ~2*10^5-iteration cost on every run.
Three arrays of size ~2*10^5 are allocated and filled, which is non-trivial memory traffic compared to the actual problem size (only a handful of primes and small exponents).
Each step in the precomputation involves modular multiplication and accessing arrays; the constant factor is relatively high and dominates runtime when var_1 is not extremely large.

complexity_delta.from: O(L + sqrt(M)) time, O(L) space with L = 2*10^5 due to factorial and inverse-factorial tables irrespective of actual input needs.
complexity_delta.to: O(sqrt(M) + log M * log MOD) time, O(1) space, dominated by prime factorization; combination computation cost depends only on small prime exponents.
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotically both are dominated by sqrt(M), but for given constraints the new version removes a fixed O(2*10^5) setup cost and large auxiliary arrays, which can be noticeable in Python for a single test case or small inputs.

trigger_signals: Single-query problem: factorial precomputation up to a fixed large L is often a red flag when the number of combination evaluations is tiny.
Prime exponents are small: sum(e_i) is O(log M), so using an O(e_i) multiplicative formula for each combination is attractive.
Observed that the factorial upper bound (2*10^5) is chosen from worst-case N, not from the actual per-instance needs, leading to wasted work.
Memory footprint and startup time: large global tables are built before any useful work, which matters in high-level languages with bigger constant factors.
transformation_steps: Eliminated global factorial and inverse-factorial precomputation (arrays var_10 and var_12) and the associated O(L) setup.

Replaced table-based nCr with an incremental multiplicative computation: for each exponent e, compute C(N-1+e, e) via a short loop over range(e), multiplying by (N-1 + e - k) and by the modular inverse of (k+1).
Changed state representation from a dict mapping primes to exponents to a generator that yields exponents in sequence, simplifying memory usage and iteration.

Adjusted prime factorization to the pattern that first extracts factor 2, then iterates odd candidates only, modestly reducing the number of trial divisions compared with the naive 2..sqrt(M) loop over all integers.
Kept the final aggregation logic as a simple product over prime exponents, but implemented in a streaming manner without needing random access to precomputed combinatorial tables.

bottlenecks.detail: Always performs O(2*10^5) modular multiplications to build factorial and inverse-factorial tables, even though only a handful of binomial coefficients (one per distinct prime factor of M) are needed.
Factorization loop iterates var_11 from 2 up to sqrt(M), testing all integers (including even numbers > 2), which is slightly less efficient than skipping evens after handling factor 2.
Stores two arrays of size 2*10^5 for factorials and inverse factorials, which is safe but not strictly necessary given the small number of nCr evaluations required.

to.time: O(√m + (Σe) · log MOD) ≈ O(√m + log m · log MOD)

complexity_delta.note: The dominant cost shifts from linear in n (factorial precomputation and storage) to depending only on the logarithmic sum of prime exponents of m. This is especially beneficial when n is large while m remains reasonably small (e.g., ≤ 1e9).

trigger_signals: The presence of large arrays sized as n+50 and loops that touch each element even though the number of required binomial coefficients is small.
Constraints or problem context suggesting n can be significantly larger than log m, making O(n) precomputation disproportionate to the actual combinatorial work needed.
Use of factorial-based binomial computation in a context where only combinations with small second argument e are needed, indicating a mismatch between implementation cost and mathematical structure.
Potential memory pressure or timeouts when scaling n upward, detectable via profiling or from problem constraints.
transformation_steps: Reformulate the combinatorial expression: recognize that the answer can be written as a product over prime exponents e of combinations C(n + e - 1, e) (stars and bars formulation).
Replace global factorial and inverse-factorial precomputation with an incremental formula for C(n + e - 1, e): C(n + e - 1, e) = product_{k=0..e-1} (n + e - 1 - k) / (k + 1).
Implement the division in modular arithmetic via Fermat's little theorem: for each term divide by (k + 1) using pow(k + 1, MOD-2, MOD) to get the modular inverse.
Turn the prime factorization into a generator function (func_1) that yields the exponent for each candidate factor, and iterate over these exponents directly, skipping work when the exponent is zero.
Eliminate the O(n) arrays (factorial and inverse-factorial tables) and their construction loops, reducing memory from O(n) to O(1) and avoiding linear-time precomputation.

bottlenecks.detail: Two full linear passes over arrays of size ~n+50 to compute factorials and inverse factorials under modulo. For large n this becomes the main cost even though only O(log m) binomial coefficients are actually needed.
Stores two arrays of length ~n+50 (factorials and inverse factorials). If n is large (e.g., up to 1e7 or more), this is memory-heavy and can cause TLE/MLE or cache inefficiency.

To compute a product over a handful of combinations C(n + e_i - 1, e_i), the code precomputes factorials for all values up to n+50, even though only binomials with small e_i (sum e_i = O(log m)) are required.

Prime factorization via trial division up to int(sqrt(m)) is O(√m). While usually acceptable for m up to 1e9, this component becomes noticeable if m is near the upper bound; it is, however, not the primary bottleneck compared to O(n) factorial precomputation when n is large.

complexity_delta.from: O(sqrt(M) + N) time, O(N) space
complexity_delta.to: O(sqrt(M) + sum(e_i)) time, O(sum(e_i)) space
complexity_delta.note: Since sum(e_i) is typically O(log M) and independent of N, the modified approach removes the linear dependence on N, which dominates for large N.

trigger_signals: Observation that the number of required binomial coefficients is equal to the number of distinct prime factors of M, which is very small (typically <= 9 for M up to 1e9).
Recognition that factorial precomputation up to N+50 is O(N) and dominates running time when N is large but sum of exponents is small.
Realization that direct combinatorial computation using big integers is feasible because k (each exponent) is small, making the product loops short and the big-int sizes moderate.
transformation_steps: Replace exponent-counting factorization with a factor-list factorization (func_2) and use collections.Counter to derive exponents; complexity remains O(sqrt(M)) but logic is modularized.
Remove global factorial and inverse factorial precomputation arrays sized O(N).
Introduce func_1(n, k) that computes binomial coefficients directly using products over k terms in numerator and denominator with Python big integers, and returns the exact integer C(n, k).
In func_3, for each prime exponent e, call func_1(N + e - 1, e), then multiply the result into the running answer modulo 1e9+7, avoiding modular inverse/table lookups entirely.
Short-circuit the special case M == 1 (no prime factors) to immediately output 1, saving unnecessary work.

bottlenecks.detail: Factorials and inverse factorials are precomputed up to N+50 even though the number of binomial coefficients required is equal only to the number of distinct prime factors of M (typically <= 9 for M <= 1e9). This introduces an O(N) cost regardless of how small the exponents are.
Two arrays of size N+50 are maintained (factorial and inverse factorial), each storing Python ints modulo 1e9+7, which is unnecessary when only a handful of combinations are needed.
Every step of factorial / inverse factorial precomputation uses modular multiplication, which is relatively costly compared to a small number of big-int multiplications when exponents are small.

to.time: O(sqrt(M) + sum_e e) ≈ O(sqrt(M) + log M)

complexity_delta.note: The optimized approach removes the O(N) factorial and inverse-factorial precomputation and instead computes each required binomial coefficient directly with cost proportional to the small exponent e of each prime factor of M.

trigger_signals: Presence of large arrays sized by N (var_7 and var_6 of length N+50) where the actual computation only requires combinatorial values for very small k (prime exponents).

Loops that run over the entire [0..N] range solely to build factorial and inverse-factorial tables, even though the number of required nCk evaluations is proportional to the number of distinct primes in M (tiny).
Theoretical constraint that exponents of primes in M are bounded by log_p(M), which is small for typical problem constraints (e.g., <= 30 for 2^30 ~ 1e9).
Independent evidence from the math formulation that only combinations of the form C(N+e-1, e) are needed, suggesting a multiplicative direct-computation approach.

Observation that N does not appear inside any loop of the optimized code except as parameters to the combinatorial function, indicating possible over-dependence on N in the original precomputation strategy.
transformation_steps: Recognize that the combinatorial term needed for each prime factor p^e of M is C(N + e - 1, e), where e is small (bounded by the exponent of p in M).
Observe that the slow implementation precomputes factorials up to N+50 to evaluate these binomial coefficients via n! / (k!(n-k)!) under modulo, incurring an O(N) cost in both time and memory.

Replace global factorial array precomputation with a local combinatorial function (func_1) that computes C(n, k) using a multiplicative formula on integer ranges of length k, with no dependency on N beyond using it as the n parameter.
Retain the same prime factorization logic (trial division up to sqrt(M)) but store exponents via a Counter, reducing repeated work and making the per-prime exponent access O(1).
Apply modulo only to the final aggregated product, letting Python's big integers handle the intermediate exact binomial coefficients (which remain modest in size because k is small).

bottlenecks.detail: Factorial and inverse-factorial arrays of size (N + 50) are precomputed even though only a handful of binomial coefficients with small k (prime exponents) are needed. This introduces an O(N) time and space dependency that is not intrinsic to the mathematical task.

The largest k used in nCk is the maximum exponent of a prime factor of M, which is at most about 30 for typical constraints (M <= 1e9). Computing all factorials up to N+50 is wasteful when nCk can be calculated directly using a multiplicative formula with cost depending on k, not on N.

Two integer lists of size O(N) (factorials and inverse factorials) are stored simultaneously. For large N, this increases memory use and may cause cache inefficiency or memory limit issues on tighter platforms.

Each factorial and inverse-factorial entry is computed with both multiplication and modulo operations. While O(N), the constant factor is relatively high because modulo is comparatively expensive and applied in every iteration.

complexity_delta.to: O(√N + Σ e_i) time and O(#primes(N)) space, independent of M except for a constant number of big-int multiplications per exponent, which is easily fast enough.
complexity_delta.note: The key improvement is to avoid any data structure whose size scales with M, and instead use short per-factor loops leveraging that exponents of N are small.

trigger_signals: Constraints indicate M can be around 1e9, making any O(M) precomputation (like factorial up to M) clearly impossible in both time and memory.
Observation that the binomial top parameter is M+e-1, so a factorial table would need to go up to approximately M, not just up to small e.
MemoryError or extreme slowdown when attempting to allocate or initialize arrays of size ≈ 1e9 in Python.

Compute the prime factorization of N directly (as in func_1), using trial division up to √N to get exponents e_i.

For each exponent e, compute C(M+e-1, e) via a simple multiplicative formula: numerator = Π_{j=1..e} (M+e-j), denominator = Π_{j=1..e} j, then take the integer ratio numerator // denominator and finally apply modulo 1e9+7.
Accumulate the product of all such binomial coefficients modulo 1e9+7, keeping intermediate values reduced by mod to avoid unnecessary big integers in the combined result.
Rely on the small size of Σ e_i (properties of prime exponents for N ≤ 1e9) to ensure that these loops remain very short, eliminating the need for factorial tables while staying efficient.

The factorial and inverse-factorial loops each run up to var_11. If var_11 is on the order of 1e9, this is ~1e9 multiplications and mod operations, which will time out by many orders of magnitude.

The solution assumes we can build combination tables up to n ≈ M, but the intended solution relies on the fact that the prime exponents of N are small (sum of exponents ≲ 30) to avoid any dependence on M in precomputation.

complexity_delta.from: O(sqrt(M) + MAX) time, O(MAX) space (factorization plus global factorial/inverse precomputation for combinations)

trigger_signals: Profiler or manual timing shows a disproportionate amount of time spent in combinatorics helper initialization (__init__) and factorial precomputation relative to the small number of combinations actually evaluated.

Replace generic nCk infrastructure with a direct multiplicative combination function that runs in O(k) where k is the small exponent e in C(e+N-1, e), applying the modulus at each multiplication to control intermediate sizes.
Keep the straightforward trial-division factorization of M, but streamline the data path: return a flat list of prime factors, then use Counter to obtain exponents.

Eliminate unused helpers (multi-dimensional list constructors, multiple YES/NO printing functions, recursion-limit tweaks) from the hot path so the code is lean and easier for the interpreter to optimize.

bottlenecks.detail: Two length-(L+1) arrays (fact and invs) are stored where L = var_11, even though the number of combination queries is very small (only one per prime factor). This doubles the O(L) memory footprint without proportional benefit.

A full modular exponentiation pow(var_8[var_11], var_12 - 2, var_12) is performed to get the inverse of fact[L]. Although only done once, for large L and tight time limits this is a noticeable constant factor.

Use of a class wrapper with method dispatch (Class_1 and func_1) and an unused/mismatched call to self.combination introduces extra indirection and potential bugs without performance gain.

Introduce func_1 implementing a multiplicative binomial coefficient computation: C(n, k) is built as a product of (n * (n-1) * ...)/(k * (k-1) * ...) using integer arithmetic, exploiting that k (the exponent e) is small.
Compute the final answer by iterating over exponents e from the Counter and multiplying C(e + N - 1, e) modulo 1e9+7, avoiding any factorial table lookups.

Keep the same number-theoretic structure (factorize M, derive exponents, then combine via stars-and-bars style combinations) but localize heavy computation only to the small exponents rather than the full N range.

bottlenecks.detail: Precomputing factorial and inverse factorial arrays var_7 and var_6 of size ~N dominates runtime and memory for large N. This is unnecessary because each exponent from the factorization is small (<= O(log M)), so combinatorial values can be computed without full factorial tables.
Two arrays of size ~N (var_7, var_6) store factorials and inverse factorials. For large N this increases memory usage and cache pressure.

The factorial construction loop and inverse factorial back-loop perform O(N) modular multiplications; while each is cheap, the sheer count dominates over the very small number of combinations actually needed (one per distinct prime factor of M).

complexity_delta.from: O(N + sqrt(M)) time, O(N) space (due to factorial and inverse-factorial precomputation up to N+100)
complexity_delta.to: O(sqrt(M) + log M) time, O(#prime_factors(M)) space (no dependency on N in precomputation)
complexity_delta.note: The new code removes the linear dependence on N by avoiding global factorial tables and using a multiplicative binomial computation whose cost depends only on the (small) exponents from M’s factorization.

trigger_signals: N appears only as a parameter of binomial coefficients, but the original implementation builds factorial tables up to N+100, which is infeasible for N as large as 1e9.
Typical problem pattern (-style factorization/combinations) indicates that the sum of exponents of M is small (O(log M)), suggesting that per-exponent work, not per-N work, is sufficient.
Memory growth O(N) in arrays `var_6`, `var_7`, and `var_9` is incompatible with large N constraints and is clearly unnecessary given only a handful of binomial evaluations.
The combination values C(N+e-1, e) are exactly integers; therefore, a multiplicative formula without modular inverses is safe and can avoid expensive modular precomputation.
transformation_steps: Keep the same mathematical formulation: factor M, and for each prime factor with exponent e, multiply by C(N+e-1, e) (equivalently C(N+e-1, N-1)) under modulo 1e9+7.

Replace the prime factorization helper with a simpler function `func_2` that returns the multiset of prime factors directly (list of primes, one per occurrence), then use `collections.Counter` to aggregate exponents.
Remove global precomputation of factorials and modular inverses (`var_6`, `var_7`, `var_9`) that iterated up to N+100.

Introduce a direct combinatorial helper `func_1(n, k)` computing C(n, k) by multiplicative formula: multiply n * (n-1) * ... * (n-k+1) and then divide by 1 * 2 * ... * k, using exact integer arithmetic; apply symmetry k = min(k, n-k) to reduce loop length.
Apply modulo 1e9+7 only to the final product (and implicitly via Python’s big integers during intermediate steps), eliminating modular inverses and modular factorial arithmetic.
Retain the overall result aggregation loop: for each exponent e in the factorization, update the answer with C(N+e-1, e), then output the result modulo 1e9+7.

bottlenecks.detail: Factorials and inverse factorials are precomputed up to N+100 even though only combinations C(N+e-1, N-1) for small exponents e (sum of exponents is O(log M)) are needed. For N up to 1e9 this is impossible in time and memory.
Arrays `var_6`, `var_7`, and `var_9` grow linearly with N, requiring O(N) memory. This does not fit typical limits when N can be ~1e9.
The pattern of linear factorial and modular inverse precomputation scales only to N around 1e6–1e7 in Python. Any larger N leads to TLE or MLE, making the solution invalid for high constraints.

complexity_delta.note: Transition from factorial-based nCr requiring precomputation up to N to a direct multiplicative nCr computation over only e terms per prime factor.

trigger_signals: Use of arrays sized by the raw input N (var_2) where N can be as large as 1e9.
O(N) loops that are not guarded by small constants and are independent of the actual combinatorial exponent sizes.
Memory allocation proportional to N while the theoretical solution in the editorial uses only O(1) or O(number_of_prime_factors) memory.
Known pattern: for D / similar problems, official solutions explicitly avoid factorial precomputation up to N due to large N.
transformation_steps: Recognize that the combinatorial term for each prime factor exponent e is C(N-1+e, e), where e is small even when N is large.
Remove factorial and inverse-factorial array precomputation (`var_7`, `var_6` of size N+50).
Replace factorial-based nCr formula `fact[n] * invfact[r] * invfact[n-r]` with a multiplicative formula that iterates only e times: C(N-1+e, e) = Π_{k=1..e} (N-1+k) / k.
Implement the multiplicative formula using Python's big integers (`var_4` for numerator and `var_10` for denominator), then take integer division and apply modulo 1e9+7.
Keep the same prime factorization method for M (trial division up to sqrt(M)), as this part is already asymptotically acceptable for the constraints.
Accumulate the result over all prime factors by multiplying each combination term modulo 1e9+7.

bottlenecks.detail: Loop `for var_8 in range(len(var_7) - 1): var_7[var_8 + 1] = var_7[var_8] * (var_8 + 1) % var_9` performs O(var_2) modular multiplications; impossible for var_2 up to 1e9 within time limits.
Loop that fills inverse factorials `for var_8 in range(1, var_10): var_6[var_10 - 1 - var_8] = ...` is also O(var_2); doubles the linear-time precomputation cost.
Arrays `var_7` and `var_6` of length (var_2 + 50) require O(var_2) memory. For var_2 ~ 1e9 this exceeds typical memory limits by orders of magnitude.

The combinatorial parameters are C(N-1+e, e) where e is small (sum of exponents of prime factorization). Precomputing factorials up to N is unnecessary and asymptotically wrong; you only need O(e) work per prime factor.

top_delta_type: algorithmic re-design (from per-number trial division to global sieve + multiple-counting) and data representation change (from ad-hoc prime set to range-based sieve over [2
top_complexity_delta: Always performs a full sieve-style scan over all divisors up to U (≈10^6), regardless of when a shared divisor is detected: Θ(U log U + N log A_max) for every input.->Retains Θ(U log U + N log A_max) in the worst case (pairwise coprime inputs) but in many cases stops after the first shared divisor is found, typically at a small var_5, reducing actual runtime substantially while keeping asymptotic bounds unchanged.

Effectively O(10^6 log log 10^6 + N log A_max) with multiple O(10^6) arrays, independent of actual max(A_i).->O(A_max log A_max + N log A_max) with a single main O(A_max) SPF array, where A_max is the true maximum of the inputs.
O(10^6 log log 10^6 + N log A_max) time, O(10^6) space (fixed bound regardless of input values)->O(A_max log log A_max + N log A_max) time, O(A_max) space (bound scales with max(A))
O(A_max * log A_max + n * log A_max) time, O(A_max + n) space->O(n * π(√A_max) + n * log A_max) time, O(n + π(√A_max)) space
O(A_max * log log A_max + n * sqrt(A_max)) time, O(A_max) space->O(n * π(√A_max)) time, O(π(√A_max)) space (effectively linear in n for fixed A_max)
O(A_max log A_max + N log A_max) time, O(A_max) space->O(N * sqrt(A_max) + N log A_max) time, O(A_max) space

O(A_max log A_max + N) with very large Python-loop and slicing overhead->O(N * avg_factorization_cost + A_max log log A_max) in practice, dominated by factorization and selective multiples marking for primes that appear in inputs

O(A_max log log A_max + n log A_max) time, O(A_max) space, with high Python overhead from sieve and set-based factorization.->O(A_max log A_max + n) time, O(A_max) space, using a frequency array and divisor-multiples aggregation with fewer Python-level operations.
O(A_max log log A_max + n log A_max) with A_max fixed at 10**6->O(A_max' log log A_max' + n log A_max') with A_max' = max(input)

O(A_max log log A_max + n log A_max) with a full, unconditional sieve over [1..A_max]->O(A_max log log A_max + n log A_max) worst-case but only sieving primes that actually occur in the input, with early termination once a shared prime is found
top_triggers: A factor table (var_14) built for the entire domain, even though only a relatively small subset of values is ever queried.
A final max() over an always-large array (var_2) when most entries remain zero.
Asymptotic mismatch: pairwise gcd checks or repeated factorizations give O(n^2) or O(n * √A_max) behavior, which is clearly incompatible with typical /competitive constraints.
Awareness that only per-prime presence is needed (not per-multiple status) for pairwise coprime detection, indicating that the global multiples marking is overkill.
Awareness that sqrt(10**6) is only 1000, so prime factorization via trial division using a fixed small prime set is viable and simple, turning the global sieve into an avoidable cost.
Code inspection shows a loop that detects a terminating condition (var_3 > 1) but merely sets a flag and continues scanning through all remaining candidates.
Code review indicating that scanning all integers up to sqrt(a_i) is unnecessary when a prime table can be used.
Code review reveals eval(input()) and unnecessary sorting in a performance-critical section.
Competitive-programming context where N can be large (e.g., 2e5), so reduction of constant factors and superfluous passes is critical.
Constraints suggesting N up to ~1e5 with A_i up to ~1e6, where naive pairwise gcd checks would be too slow.
Constraints where A_max is large (around 1e6) but N is relatively smaller (around 1e5), suggesting that per-value processing is preferable to full-range sieving by all divisors.
Creation of a new set() for every input number and updates to a defaultdict inside the main loop indicate high allocation and hashing overhead.
Desire to simplify the code by removing a custom sieve implementation and substituting a fixed prime table when constraints are known and narrow.
Detection that the problem only needs to know multiplicities of shared prime factors, not full factorisation of each number.
Factorization logic that processes multiplicities one division at a time instead of collapsing runs of the same prime factor.
Full factorization and counting for every number even though any repeated prime factor suffices to disprove pairwise coprimality, indicating missed opportunities for early stopping.
top_steps: 1. Abandon the hard-coded small-prime set and instead compute primality for all integers up to max(a_i) using a sieve (dictionary var_3 in the provided code, though a list would also suffice).
2. Build a frequency structure var_2 that counts how many times each integer up to max(a_i) appears in the input. This lets us query 'how many numbers are equal to k' in O(1) time.

3. Enumerate only primes (keys in var_3 with True) and, for each prime p, iterate over its multiples k=p,2p,3p,... up to max(a_i), accumulating var_2[k] into var_4[p]. This replaces individual factorization of each a_i with a single pass over all multiples for each prime.

4. Compute the maximum value of var_4, i.e. the largest number of input elements sharing a common prime factor. Use this single statistic to classify the set as pairwise coprime, setwise coprime, or not coprime.

5. Remove any pairwise-gcd nested loops or per-number factorization logic that scales as O(n^2) or O(n * π(√A_max)), ensuring the main work is done once per prime/multiple rather than once per input element.
Accumulate prime factor counts in a global frequency array var_2 to test pairwise coprimality (all counts <= 1).
Adjust pairwise-coprime detection logic to rely on which primes divide which numbers (tracked via sets/counters) instead of relying on var_3 being a dense mark array over [1..A_max].
After the pairwise check, compute the gcd over all numbers to distinguish 'setwise coprime' from 'not coprime'.
Aggregate prime factor counts globally and classify: if any prime count >= 2 then numbers are not pairwise coprime; combine with GCD information to distinguish 'setwise' vs 'not' coprime.
Allocate the prime-count array var_2 with length var_6+1 instead of 10^6+5.
Avoid constructing slices var_7[d::d] and instead rely on direct indexed accesses and loops, eliminating repeated allocations and lowering per-iteration overhead.
Avoid extra per-number allocations (sets and dictionary updates); rely on preallocated arrays and simple integer arithmetic.
Avoid unnecessary sorting: the fast code processes the numbers in input order, eliminating the O(N log N) sort.

Change the pairwise coprimality check from counting prime-factor occurrences across all numbers to maintaining a global set of used primes and checking intersection with each new number’s prime set, with immediate early exit if any overlap is found.
Changed GCD computation from math.gcd in a loop to a recursive Euclidean implementation func_2 applied incrementally over the list.
Compute the gcd of all array elements once (folding gcd over the list) to distinguish setwise vs not coprime in O(n log A_max) time, instead of mixing gcd computation with heavy factorization work.

complexity_delta.from: O(A_max log log A_max + n log A_max) with a full, unconditional sieve over [1..A_max]
complexity_delta.to: O(A_max log log A_max + n log A_max) worst-case but only sieving primes that actually occur in the input, with early termination once a shared prime is found
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotic big-O remains the same, but the fast version avoids a full sieve and redundant prime-factor processing, substantially reducing constant factors and typical runtime.

trigger_signals: Presence of a full sieve up to a constant upper bound (1e6) regardless of actual input distribution.
Nested Python loops over ~1e6 with an inner loop also potentially visiting a large fraction of that range.
A factor table (var_14) built for the entire domain, even though only a relatively small subset of values is ever queried.
Use of a custom recursive gcd instead of the optimized math.gcd, indicating missed opportunities to leverage built-in primitives.
No early exit from the sieve when pairwise coprimality has already been invalidated; work continues even after the answer is determined.
transformation_steps: Replace the global precomputation of prime factors for all numbers up to 1e6 with an input-driven factorization: for each A[i], factor it on the fly by trial division.
Introduce a global boolean/int array (var_3) indexed by prime, used to record whether a prime has already appeared in any previous A[i].

For each new prime factor p of the current number, mark all multiples of p (p, 2p, 3p, ...) in var_3, so that any later A[j] with a factor whose remaining value hits a marked index is detected immediately as sharing a prime factor.
Short-circuit the pairwise-coprime check: if func_1 detects that any prime factor is already used (via var_3[var_11] == 1), return False early without further factorization or marking.
Compute the overall gcd using math.gcd in a simple loop, instead of a custom recursive gcd, to decide between setwise coprime and not coprime after the pairwise check.
Maintain the existing output logic, but rely on the more efficient factoring/marking scheme to ensure scalability.

bottlenecks.detail: func_2 always precomputes var_14 for all numbers up to 1e6, even if the actual input uses few primes. This unconditional O(A_max log log A_max) sieve dominates runtime for large constraints.

The sieve assigns var_14[var_10] = var_6 for every multiple without checking whether it was already set to a smaller factor, so each composite may be updated multiple times (once per prime factor). This increases constant factors compared to a canonical SPF sieve.

The nested for-loops over range(2, 1_000_001) and range(var_6, 1_000_001, var_6) are executed entirely in Python, which is relatively slow for ~1e6-scale loops even if the asymptotic complexity is acceptable.
func_1 implements gcd recursively instead of using math.gcd; although the depth is small (O(log A_max)), recursive calls in Python add overhead compared to the optimized C implementation.
Reading n using int(eval(var_7())) adds overhead and is unsafe; a simple int(var_7()) would suffice. This is a minor overhead but avoidable.

complexity_delta.from: O(A_max * log A_max + n * log A_max) time, O(A_max + n) space
complexity_delta.to: O(n * π(√A_max) + n * log A_max) time, O(n + π(√A_max)) space
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotically, both are feasible for A_max=10^6. The key gain is removing the unconditional O(A_max log A_max) term and its heavy slicing allocations, replacing it with a cost proportional to n and sqrt(A_max). For large A_max with smaller n, this is a clear win; for maximal n the cost is comparable but with better constants and much smaller memory.

trigger_signals: Presence of a fixed upper bound A_max = 10^6 and loops scanning all values up to this maximum regardless of actual input n or value distribution.
Use of list slicing in the hot path (var_7[d::d]) inside a nested loop, which implies repeated allocations and copying in a counting/sieve-like routine.
Problem semantics only depend on prime factors of the given numbers, not on absent values, suggesting per-number factoring is more natural.
Memory usage dominated by large global arrays of zeros when n may be much smaller than A_max.
transformation_steps: Replace the global frequency array var_7 of size 10^6+1 with per-number factorization; no need to represent all values up to A_max explicitly.
Precompute or hard-code the list of primes up to sqrt(A_max) (here, primes up to 997 for A_max=10^6) into a set or list.
For each input value, factor it using the prime list: for each prime p <= sqrt(a), while a % p == 0, record p and divide a //= p; ensure each prime factor is only processed once per number.
Maintain a global set of 'used prime factors'; if a prime factor p is already in this set when processing a new number, the sequence is not pairwise coprime.
After the pairwise check, compute the gcd over all numbers to distinguish 'setwise coprime' from 'not coprime'.
Remove the O(A_max) loop over d and the creation of slices var_7[d::d], as these are no longer needed.

bottlenecks.detail: Loops d from 2 up to 10^6 regardless of n or the magnitude/distribution of the actual inputs. Many d values correspond to no present numbers, but still require scanning (and slicing) through zero counts.
var_7[d::d] creates a new list for each d. Total copied elements are ~A_max * log A_max (~2e7 entries), causing extra allocations and memory traffic compared to iterating in-place.
The frequency array var_7 has size 10^6+1 even if n is small or input values are much smaller than 10^6. This is several MB of memory plus Python list overhead.

complexity_delta.from: O(A_max log log A_max + n log A_max) with A_max fixed at 10**6
complexity_delta.to: O(A_max' log log A_max' + n log A_max') with A_max' = max(input)
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotic form is similar, but the sieve bound shrinks to the actual data range and per-factor overhead is reduced by using arrays instead of sets and skipping multiplicities during factorization. This significantly improves constant factors and practical runtime, especially when max(A_i) << 10**6.

trigger_signals: Constraints suggesting N up to ~1e5 with A_i up to ~1e6, where naive pairwise gcd checks would be too slow.
Presence of a sieve or factorization routine built up to a fixed large constant bound rather than the observed maximum value in the data.
Use of Python sets or other high-overhead structures in inner loops that run once per prime factor of each number.
Factorization logic that processes multiplicities one division at a time instead of collapsing runs of the same prime factor.
transformation_steps: Limit the SPF sieve to the maximum value present in the input instead of a hard-coded 10**6, reducing both time and memory when the data range is smaller.
Replace the per-number and global Python sets used to track prime factors with a single integer array (var_2) that counts, for each prime, how many input numbers it divides.
Use the SPF table (MINFact) to factor each number while skipping repeated divisions for the same prime factor, looping once per distinct prime factor rather than per multiplicity.
Aggregate prime factor counts globally and classify: if any prime count >= 2 then numbers are not pairwise coprime; combine with GCD information to distinguish 'setwise' vs 'not' coprime.
Reorganize the logic so that cheap global properties (like gcd over the array) can be used to short-circuit heavier computations when applicable.

bottlenecks.detail: Sieve upper bound is fixed at 10**6 regardless of the actual maximum value in the input; if max(A_i) << 10**6 this wastes time and memory.
Use of Python sets for prime tracking in func_3 adds per-factor overhead (hashing, dynamic resizing) compared with a simple frequency/count array indexed by prime.
The pairwise-coprime check (factorizing all numbers and checking shared primes) is performed before the cheaper global gcd check. For many non-coprime cases this does more work than necessary.

complexity_delta.from: O(M log log M + N log A_max) time, O(M) space, with M = 10^6 fixed
complexity_delta.to: O(N * sqrt(A_max) + N log A_max) time, O(A_max) space
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotically, SPF sieve is better for large M, but here M is fixed at 10^6 and is overkill for small inputs. The modified version removes the fixed sieve cost and makes runtime depend more directly on actual values, at the price of a potentially worse N * sqrt(A_max) factor if N and A_max are both large. For typical constraints (N up to 1e5, A_max up to 1e6), both are acceptable; performance depends on constant factors and input distribution.

trigger_signals: Presence of a full-range sieve up to a fixed constant (10**6) regardless of actual maximum A[i].
Multiple large auxiliary arrays (var_7, var_9) sized to the global bound rather than the true data range.
Use of eval(input()) for reading integers, hinting at avoidable overhead and non-competitive I/O style for tight constraints.
Problem structure that only needs unique prime factor occurrences, which can be tracked without full factor information for all numbers up to the upper bound.
transformation_steps: Removed global SPF sieve precomputation over range [2, 10^6] and the associated array var_7.
Introduced func_1 to factor each number on demand via trial division up to sqrt(value), collecting distinct prime factors.
Replaced last-index tracking array (var_9) with a boolean visited array (var_4) indexed by prime factor to detect repeated prime usage (pairwise coprimality check).
Changed GCD computation from math.gcd in a loop to a recursive Euclidean implementation func_2 applied incrementally over the list.
Switched from input() to sys.stdin.readline() for faster input, and from fixed upper bound var_2 = 10**6 to max(var_2) to size the visited-prime array more tightly.

bottlenecks.detail: The SPF sieve always runs up to 10^6, even if max(A[i]) is much smaller. For small inputs, this is a large fixed cost independent of actual data.
Two integer arrays of size 10^6+1 (var_7, var_9) consume noticeable memory and may affect cache behavior.
Using plain input() and eval(input()) is slower and less safe than sys.stdin.readline() + int().

complexity_delta.from: Slow version: O(A_max log log A_max + N log A_max) with large constant factors from Python-level sieve and factorization.
complexity_delta.to: Fast version: O(A_max log A_max + N log A_max) with far fewer Python-level operations due to simple frequency counting and divisor iteration.
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotically both are close, but the sieve + factorization approach performs many more Python operations (inner loops, divisions, set handling), making it slower in practice. The fast version relies on linear array operations and simple loops, which are much cheaper in Python.

trigger_signals: Nested loops over range(2, 10**6+1) combined with inner loops over multiples suggest millions of Python-level iterations.
Per-element while-loop for factorization with integer division indicates potentially heavy computation for each large input value.
Creation of a new set() for every input number and updates to a defaultdict inside the main loop indicate high allocation and hashing overhead.
Recursive gcd function calls instead of using math.gcd in an iterative loop hint at unnecessary function call overhead.
Total work depends primarily on A_max (=1e6), not N, so any extra passes over [1..A_max] are critical for performance.
transformation_steps: Remove the SPF sieve construction and per-element factorization. Instead, build a frequency array freq[0..A_max] and increment freq[a_i] for each input value.
Replace prime-factor counting via defaultdict with a divisor sweep: for each d in [2..A_max], sum freq[d], freq[2d], freq[3d], ... until exceeding 1 or reaching A_max to detect shared prime factors.
Reduce gcd overhead by using an iterative approach with math.gcd instead of a recursive custom gcd implementation.
Keep the overall logic: if no divisor has count >1, classify as 'pairwise coprime'; otherwise compute gcd of all numbers and classify as 'setwise coprime' if gcd == 1, else 'not coprime'.
Avoid extra per-number allocations (sets and dictionary updates); rely on preallocated arrays and simple integer arithmetic.

bottlenecks.detail: SPF sieve up to 1e6 with nested loops performs millions of Python-level iterations and assignments; this dominates runtime.
Per-element factorization with a while-loop and integer division, plus a temporary set for distinct prime factors, adds significant overhead on top of the sieve.
Recursive gcd (func_1) introduces Python function call overhead for every gcd step, which is slower than using math.gcd in a tight iterative loop.
Use of collections.defaultdict for counting prime factors, repeated set allocations, and repeated integer divisions all increase constant factors even though asymptotic complexity is acceptable.

complexity_delta.from: O(N * sqrt(A_max) + A_max log log A_max) time, O(A_max) space
complexity_delta.to: O(N * sqrt(A_max)) time, O(A_max) space
complexity_delta.note: Removed the per-prime O(A_max/p) 'mark all multiples' sweep, transforming a hybrid factorization + partial sieve approach into a pure on-demand factorization with O(1) marking per prime factor. Input and control-flow optimizations reduce constant factors.

trigger_signals: Profiling or inspection shows func_1 dominating runtime due to the inner loop that marks all multiples of each prime factor up to 10^6.

Time limit exceeded (TLE) or near-timeout behavior on test cases with many elements and numbers dense in small prime factors, where many distinct primes appear and the O(A_max log log A_max) marking term becomes costly.
Code review reveals eval(input()) and unnecessary sorting in a performance-critical section.
Awareness that only per-prime presence is needed (not per-multiple status) for pairwise coprime detection, indicating that the global multiples marking is overkill.
transformation_steps: Replace the global multiples-marking scheme: instead of, for each discovered prime p, looping var_10 from p to 10^6 and marking var_3[var_10], the fast version simply stores a boolean flag var_4[p] to remember whether prime p has appeared before.

Simplify pairwise coprimality detection: for each number, factor it into distinct primes, then check var_4[p]; if already True, the set is not pairwise coprime and factorization for subsequent elements can be skipped.
Retain but decouple the GCD logic: compute the GCD of all elements in a simple loop (func_2), used at the end to distinguish 'setwise coprime' (gcd == 1) from 'not coprime'.
Avoid unnecessary sorting: the fast code processes the numbers in input order, eliminating the O(N log N) sort.
Optimize I/O: use sys.stdin.readline() instead of input() and remove eval() for integer parsing, reducing overhead.
Use a tight factorization loop: iterate var_6 from 2 up to int(var_10**0.5) and divide var_10 completely by var_6 once it is detected as a factor, minimizing repeated work for the same prime.

bottlenecks.detail: func_1 marks every multiple of each newly discovered prime factor p up to 10^6 (a sieve-like loop) even though the algorithm only needs to know whether prime p has been seen before, not whether its multiples exist in the input. This adds an unnecessary O(A_max/p) loop per distinct prime factor.

Pairwise-coprime check is embedded into a factorization routine that relies on trial division up to sqrt(a_i); with N up to ~1e5 and A_max up to ~1e6, a worst-case O(N * sqrt(A_max)) ≈ 10^8 trial divisions is borderline in Python.
Input is read using input() and n is parsed via eval(input()), which is slower and unnecessarily powerful compared to int(sys.stdin.readline()).
The array is sorted even though the algorithm’s logic does not require sorted order for correctness; this costs O(N log N) extra work, though this is secondary compared to the factorization cost.

complexity_delta.from: O(A_max log log A_max + n log A_max) time, O(A_max) space, with high Python overhead from sieve and set-based factorization.
complexity_delta.to: O(A_max log A_max + n) time, O(A_max) space, using a frequency array and divisor-multiples aggregation with fewer Python-level operations.
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotically both depend linearly (up to log factors) on A_max, but the fast version removes per-number factorization and set overhead, and converts the logic into a single structured multiples scan, which is much faster in CP Python.

trigger_signals: Presence of a full sieve up to a fixed large bound (10**6) even when the maximum input value can be smaller.
Nested Python for-loops over ranges proportional to 10^6 in func_2, indicating potentially millions of interpreted iterations regardless of input.
Inner loops that perform repeated division and Python set operations for each prime factor of every number in func_3.
Separate gcd folding pass over all elements in addition to the factorization-based analysis, suggesting redundant computation.
The problem structure (checking common divisors over a bounded integer domain) naturally suggests a frequency-and-multiples approach instead of explicit factorization.
transformation_steps: Replace smallest-prime-factor sieve (func_2) and per-number factorization (func_3) with a single frequency array var_2 of size max(A_i)+1 that counts occurrences of each value.
For each integer d from 2 to max(A_i), compute how many input numbers are divisible by d by summing var_2 at indices d, 2d, 3d, ... (i.e., sum(var_2[d::d])). Track the maximum such count var_6.

Use var_6 to classify: if var_6 < 2 then no divisor > 1 appears in two different numbers (pairwise coprime); else if var_6 < n then some divisor > 1 appears multiple times but none appear in all numbers (setwise coprime); else var_6 == n implies some divisor > 1 divides all numbers (not coprime).
Remove explicit gcd folding (func_1) and the prime-factor-set logic (func_3/func_4); the classification is now derived solely from the divisor-multiples counts.
Tighten memory layout to a single integer list var_2; avoid sets and auxiliary arrays from the sieve.

bottlenecks.detail: Sieve size is hard-coded to 10**6 in func_3 regardless of the actual maximum value in the input, forcing a full 10^6-scale sieve even when unnecessary.
func_2 implements the sieve entirely in Python with nested for-loops over up to 10^6 indices; this has substantial interpreter overhead compared to more linear scans.

For each prime factor of each number, func_3 performs membership checks and insertions into two Python sets (var_8 and var_9). These operations are O(1) average but relatively slow, and are in the inner factorization loop.

Every number is factored individually via repeated division by smallest prime factors. Even though asymptotically acceptable, it incurs many Python-level divisions and loop iterations when there are many inputs or larger values.
The global gcd is computed separately in func_1 by folding gcd over all elements, even though the factorization phase already contains enough information to infer setwise vs not coprime in principle.

complexity_delta.from: Per-element naive trial division plus ad-hoc marking: O(n * sqrt(A_max) + A_max * log log A_max) time, O(A_max) space.
complexity_delta.to: Single global SPF sieve plus fast factorization: O(A_max * log log A_max + n * log A_max) time, O(A_max) space.
complexity_delta.note: As n grows large relative to sqrt(A_max), replacing O(sqrt(A_max)) per-number trial division with O(log A_max) SPF-based factorization yields a substantial speedup; the sieve cost is paid only once.

trigger_signals: Input size n is large (up to around 2e5) and maximum value A_max is moderate (about 1e6), a classic regime where per-element O(sqrt(A_max)) trial division is too slow in Python.
The original code performs a nested structure: for each number, a divisor loop and possibly a full pass marking multiples up to 10**6, leading to many millions of Python-level iterations.
The logical task (coprimality classification) depends only on prime factor multiplicities and overall gcd, which are well-suited to sieve-based precomputation.
Presence of a fixed-size global array var_3 of length 10**6 suggests that a more standard sieve/prime-factor precomputation is appropriate.
transformation_steps: Replace repeated naive trial division in func_1(var_11) with a precomputed smallest-prime-factor (SPF) array built once up to max(a_i).

Construct the SPF array using a sieve of Eratosthenes style loop: for each integer i that is still marked as prime, set MINFact[i] = i and propagate i as the smallest factor for its multiples, instead of marking multiples repeatedly from each input value.

Refactor factorization into a method (func_2 / prime_factor) that repeatedly looks up MINFact[x] and divides x by that factor until it changes, counting distinct prime factors for each input efficiently.
Accumulate prime factor counts in a global frequency array var_2 to test pairwise coprimality (all counts <= 1).
Compute the gcd of all array elements once (folding gcd over the list) to distinguish setwise vs not coprime in O(n log A_max) time, instead of mixing gcd computation with heavy factorization work.
Remove sorting as a functional requirement for coprimality classification (it is unnecessary for the logic and costs O(n log n)).

bottlenecks.detail: Per-number factorization restarts trial division from 2 for every input, performing up to sqrt(a_i) / constant trial divisions even though many small divisors repeat across numbers. With n up to ~2e5 and A_max = 1e6, this can approach ~2e8 simple operations, which is borderline in Python.

Inside factorization, when a new prime factor p is found, an inner loop marks all multiples of p up to 10**6 (var_10 += var_8). This sieve-like marking is implemented with Python-level loops and can be costly if many distinct primes appear across the data.

The candidate divisor var_8 increments by 1 regardless of primality; the code does not reuse a precomputed prime list or smallest-prime-factor table, so it spends time testing composite divisors with no benefit.

Even though var_3 is used to detect repeated prime factors across numbers, the code does not exploit this to skip factorization paths; it still re-factorizes each number from scratch rather than using a global sieve built once.

problem_type_tags: prime factorization with SPF (smallest prime factor)

complexity_delta.from: Time: O(M log log M + n log A) with M fixed at 10^6+5. Space: O(M) with M fixed at 10^6+5.
complexity_delta.to: Time: O(A_max log log A_max + n log A_max). Space: O(A_max).
