Change prime-factor accounting from a frequency array (var_2 indexed by prime, followed by max(var_2)) to a pair of sets: a global set of primes seen across all numbers (var_9) and a per-number set of its factors (var_8). This allows immediate detection of a shared prime via membership checks.

Introduce an early-exit mechanism in func_3: as soon as a prime factor is found that appears both in the current number and the global set of primes, the function returns False (not pairwise coprime). This avoids factorizing all numbers in non-pairwise cases.
Refactor GCD computation into func_1 (new version), computing the GCD cumulatively from 0, which is linear in n and uses no extra memory beyond the input array.

Reorganize control flow into small, single-purpose functions: func_2 for SPF sieve, func_3 for pairwise coprime detection, func_1 (new) for GCD, and func_4 for mapping classification (pairwise, setwise, not) to an index.

Eliminate the large global count array var_2 used in the original code, instead relying on sets of primes that store only primes actually encountered in the input, improving memory usage for sparse prime distributions.

bottlenecks.detail: func_1 builds a full sieve up to 10^6+5 using nested Python loops and a separate boolean array var_10. Although asymptotically O(M log log M), the constant factors are high: Python loops over ~10^6 with inner loops on each prime do tens of millions of iterations.

Uses two large arrays of size ~10^6 (var_3 for SPF and var_2 for prime counts). While acceptable, it adds memory pressure and cache misses, and var_3 is allocated globally and filled via a nested function, which prevents reuse across test cases in other contexts.

The method to detect pairwise coprimality counts prime factors for all numbers and only then calls max(var_2). There is no early exit when a shared prime factor is detected, so in worst case it always processes all inputs fully.
Uses eval(input()) to read n, which is slower and less safe than int(input()), though its impact is negligible compared to the sieve cost.

complexity_delta.from: O(M log log M + n * log M) time, O(M) space (sieve of largest prime factor + per-number factorization and gcd)
complexity_delta.to: O(M log M + M log log M + n) time, O(M) space (primality sieve + per-prime multiple counting using a histogram)
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotically both are near-linear in M with an additional near-linear-in-n term. The refactor shifts work from per-element factorization and set operations to a more global per-prime aggregation. In pure big-O terms, the new sieve is slightly worse (O(M log M) vs O(M log log M)), but it trades repeated factorization and dynamic set manipulation for more regular integer loops, which can be competitive in CP despite similar asymptotics.

trigger_signals: Need to handle up to ~2e5 integers with values up to ~1e6 under tight time limits (typical 1–2 seconds in Python).
Presence of a hard-coded sieve up to 10**6, even when max(a_i) is smaller, suggesting potential wasted work and memory.
Per-element prime factorization in Python with dynamic sets in the inner loop, which can be relatively expensive compared to straight integer loops.
Awareness that aggregating divisibility information in a global pass over primes (using a histogram) is a common optimization pattern for coprimality problems.
transformation_steps: Replace the largest-prime-factor table func_2 and per-number factorization loop with a primality sieve up to max(a_i).
Introduce a frequency table (histogram) var_2 of size up to max(a_i) to count how many times each integer value appears in the input.
For each prime p, iterate over its multiples and aggregate how many input elements are divisible by p using the frequency table, storing these counts in var_4.

Use the maximum prime-divisibility count var_11 to detect whether any prime is shared across multiple elements (pairwise coprimality test) and then classify into pairwise/setwise/not coprime using simple comparisons instead of explicit gcd plus per-element factorization.
Inline the logic directly in the main script instead of through nested helper functions, reducing Python function-call overhead in tight loops.

bottlenecks.detail: func_2 always sieves up to 10**6 regardless of the maximum input value; when max(a_i) << 10^6, a large portion of the sieve space and work is wasted.

Pairwise-coprime detection in func_3 walks through prime factors of every number, using Python sets var_8 and var_9 for membership checks on each factor. These set operations sit in the hottest loop and add overhead versus simple integer counting.

Using a full factor-table and per-element factorization requires repeated divisions and set insertions; it scales with the number of prime factors of each number, instead of aggregating divisibility information in a single pass over primes.
Hard-coding the sieve limit (10**6) prevents exploiting smaller max(a_i) for potential speedups and tighter memory usage.
Using eval(input()) for reading integers is slower and less safe than int(input()) and provides no benefit here.

complexity_delta.from: O(N log log N + Q * P^2 log P) with large constants due to Python-level loops and P=100
complexity_delta.to: O(N log log N + Q) with very small per-query constants and C-level bulk operations
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotic big-O in N is similar, but the slow version multiplies the per-query constant by roughly a factor of 20–30, and also implements the sieve in pure Python loops rather than via C-optimized slice operations.

trigger_signals: Profiler / timing shows a large fraction of time spent inside Python loops over combinations_with_replacement and sieve-multiple loops.
Per-query runtime grows linearly with the size of the chosen prime window (100) even though the problem only needs a small, fixed number of primes.
High CPU usage with relatively small memory footprint, indicating interpreter overhead rather than algorithmic superlinear blowup.
Sieve phase taking noticeably long despite modest N (110000), hinting that the sieve implementation is suboptimal in Python.
transformation_steps: Replace Python nested-loop sieve with slice-based sieve: use `var_7[start :: step] = [0] * count` to mark multiples in bulk, letting CPython run the inner loops in C.
Reduce the per-query prime window from 100 down to the minimal required 23 primes after the input bound, matching the original logic.
Use combinations over a small fixed-size list (`itertools.combinations(var_8, 2)` plus a comprehension for doubles) rather than combinations_with_replacement over 100 primes.
Minimize per-query allocations: construct a fixed-size list of 23 primes, compute ≈276 sums, and sort that small list instead of ~5000 elements.
Reuse precomputed primality structure (`var_7`) directly for scanning primes instead of materializing a full list of all primes and slicing it on each query.

bottlenecks.detail: Inner sieve loop `for var_6 in range(var_4 * var_4, var_1, var_4): var_11[var_6] = False` executes in Python and performs tens of thousands of iterations with interpreted index updates.

Per-query loop over `combinations_with_replacement(var_3, 2)` with P=100 produces about 5050 iterations; each iteration constructs a Python int sum and appends to a Python list, which is relatively expensive.
Using 100 primes after the bound instead of the minimal required set (23 in the fast solution) multiplies the per-query work by ~20x for both the combinations and the final sort.
Sorting ~5000 integers per query (`var_7.sort()`) is significantly heavier than sorting the ~276 integers in the fast version.
Building a new list of all primes `var_10 = [...]` and then slicing `var_10[var_5:var_5+100]` per query adds memory and slice-allocation overhead.

complexity_delta.from: O(M log log M + N log M) time, O(M + N) space (SPF sieve + per-element factorization + gcd chain)
complexity_delta.to: O(N + M log M) time, O(M + N) space (frequency counting + multiple enumeration)
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotically, both are acceptable for M≈1e6 and N≈2e5. The change is less about big-O and more about Python-level constant factors: the sieve+factorization approach uses many nested Python loops with divisions and set operations, while the multiple-counting approach uses simpler linear scans and integer additions, which tend to be faster in CP Python despite an O(M log M) step count.

trigger_signals: Presence of a sieve with nested Python loops over all values up to max(A) combined with additional per-element factorization loops, which is often too heavy in Python for max(A) ≈ 1e6.
Per-element factorization and set manipulation inside inner loops, leading to many millions of Python operations for large N.
Need for simpler, tighter inner loops dominated by integer additions instead of divisions and dynamic set operations to fit within time limits.
Observation that the problem only needs to know if any integer divides at least two elements, not the exact prime factorization of each number, allowing a coarser multiple-counting approach.
transformation_steps: Remove the SPF sieve construction and its inner loop over multiples, as well as the per-element factorization logic based on that sieve.
Introduce a frequency array `var_2` of size max(A)+1 and populate it by incrementing `var_2[value]` for each input number.

Replace the prime-factor-based pairwise-coprime detection with a loop over all integers d from 2 to max(A), computing the number of elements divisible by d via `sum(var_2[d::d])`, and tracking the maximum such count as `var_6`.
Use the maximum multiple count `var_6` and the total count N (`var_5`) to classify the set: if `var_6 < 2` then pairwise coprime; else if `var_6 < N` then setwise coprime; else not coprime.
Eliminate the explicit gcd reduction function for setwise coprime detection, relying instead on the multiple-counting based threshold comparison, which avoids repeated gcd calls.

bottlenecks.detail: The sieve loop `for var_6 in range(2, var_9 + 1)` with inner `for var_7 in range(2 * var_6, var_9 + 1, var_6)` performs on the order of M log log M Python-level iterations. For M≈1e6 this is borderline in CP Python, especially combined with other work.

In `func_2`, each A[i] is factorized via repeated SPF lookups and divisions in a while loop, plus `set` operations. This costs ~O(log A[i]) Python steps per element, so ≈ several million operations for large N.

`var_5 = set()` per element and repeated `add` calls, plus membership checks against the global `var_11` set, are all inside the per-element factorization loop, adding overhead on top of arithmetic operations.

Functions `func_2` and `func_3` declare many unused globals (`N, A, soinsu, a, b`), and rely on outer-scope mutation (`var_11`). While not asymptotically bad, this can slightly increase call overhead and obscures data flow.

complexity_delta.from: O(M log log M + N log V) time, O(M) space, with fixed M = 10**6 regardless of input max
complexity_delta.to: O(V log log V + N log V) time, O(V) space, where V = max(A[i]) <= 10**6
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotic forms look similar when V ≈ M, but the fast version scales with the actual maximum value in the input. If V is substantially smaller than 10**6, the work done by the sieve is reduced proportionally, leading to a clear runtime improvement. This also makes the solution more robust to different constraints without hard-coding bounds.

trigger_signals: Detection of a hard-coded upper bound (10**6) passed into the sieve regardless of the actual max(A[i]).
Sieve construction dominating runtime in profiling or reasoning about the code, especially when many inputs are much smaller than the hard-coded limit.
Problem constraints specifying an upper limit on A[i], suggesting that precomputation could instead be sized dynamically based on input.
Awareness that competitive-programming problems often require input-sensitive optimizations to avoid TLE on worst-case batches.
transformation_steps: Replace the hard-coded SPF construction func_2(10**6) with a sieve whose upper bound is the maximum value present in the input array (var_9 = max(var_1) and var_2 sized to var_9 + 1).

Use the SPF table to factor each number exactly once, maintaining a global set of primes seen so far (var_11) and a per-number set to detect repeated primes within the same number, thereby performing a clear pairwise-coprime check.
Separate the concerns of pairwise coprime checking (func_2) and setwise coprime checking via gcd aggregation (func_3) for clarity and to avoid repeated or unnecessary work.
Avoid hard-coding numeric limits that mirror current constraints; instead, derive necessary bounds from the input so the algorithm adapts automatically to the provided data.
Keep auxiliary data structures (like SPF and sets of used primes) at the minimal size needed for the specific test case, reducing memory traffic and cache pressure.

bottlenecks.detail: func_2(10**6) constructs the SPF table up to a hard-coded upper bound 10**6 instead of up to max(A[i]). When max(A[i]) << 10**6, this performs significantly more work than necessary, both in time and memory traffic.

The SPF sieve loops over all odd indices up to 10**6 and, for each prime, iterates its multiples starting from the prime itself. This is standard but still the dominant cost; any inflation of the upper bound directly multiplies this cost.

For each element A[i], a new temporary set var_8 is allocated and populated with prime factors, and global set var_9 is updated. These per-element allocations and hash operations add overhead, but they are asymptotically smaller than the sieve cost.
The code assumes all A[i] <= 10**6. If constraints change (e.g., A[i] up to 10**7), the fixed SPF size becomes incorrect (index errors) or must be increased, further amplifying the sieve cost.

Using eval(input()) to read an integer and printing a tuple instead of a flat string are minor inefficiencies compared with the sieve, but still unnecessary overheads in a tight competitive-programming setting.

complexity_delta.from: Sieve: O(M log log M) with heavy Python-loop constants; per query: O(P^2 log P) with P≈100 (≈5050 sums) plus Python overhead in cwr generation.
complexity_delta.to: Sieve: O(M log log M) with low constant via slice operations; per query: O(1) over a tiny fixed P=22 (253 sums), with much lower constants.
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotic big-O in terms of M and Q stays similar, but constant factors drop by roughly an order of magnitude in both preprocessing (sieve) and per-query processing (sums generation and sorting).

trigger_signals: Profiling or timing shows most runtime spent in the sieve’s inner loop over multiples and in the per-query double loop generating sums with combinations_with_replacement.
Observation that each query always slices exactly 100 primes regardless of problem requirements, suggesting unnecessary work.
Counting per-query sums reveals ~5050 elements are generated and sorted, compared to the theoretical need (~253) derived from 22 primes.
TLE or slow performance for inputs with many queries, even though numeric bounds (up to ~1e5) are modest.
transformation_steps: Replace the nested-loop sieve with a slice-based sieve: for each prime index, mark its multiples using a single slice assignment, leveraging C-level loops instead of Python-level iterations.
Adjust prime representation to 0-based indexing (i -> number i+1) allowing simple arithmetic to map indices to values and slice strides to multiples.
Change per-query logic to gather only the first 22 primes greater than the given threshold, matching the mathematical requirement instead of using an arbitrary 100-prime window.
Generate pair sums using itertools.combinations on the fixed-size array of 22 primes and manually add p+p terms, producing only 253 sums instead of ~5050.
Sort the much smaller sums list and index directly for the requested k-th value.

bottlenecks.detail: Inner loop `for var_6 in range(var_4 * var_4, var_1, var_4)` is pure Python and runs for every prime up to ~sqrt(110000); this is asymptotically fine but relatively slow in CPython due to many small interpreted steps.
For each query, the code takes 100 primes (`var_10[var_5:var_5+100]`) although the original logic only needs a fixed small count (22). This inflates the pair set size from 253 to 5050.
Using `combinations_with_replacement` over 100 primes generates P(P+1)/2 ≈ 5050 pairs; each append in `var_7.append(var_4 + var_6)` is a Python operation, repeated thousands of times per query.
Sorting ~5050 integers per query (`var_7.sort()`) costs O(P^2 log P). With many queries this dominates runtime even though P is constant.

complexity_delta.from: O(10^6 log log 10^6 + N * log A_max) with heavy constant factors from set operations and per-exponent divisions in factorization.
complexity_delta.to: O(A_max log log A_max + N * ω(A_max)) with lower constants using an array of prime counts and per-distinct-prime processing.
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotic time remains sieve + factorization, but the sieve range is tightened to max(A) and set-based bookkeeping is replaced with simple integer arrays, which is significantly faster in Python.

trigger_signals: Use of Python sets inside nested loops during prime factorization, which is costly for large N.
A fixed sieve size (10^6) rather than adapting to max(A), indicating easily avoidable extra work.
Problem constraints (N up to ~1e5, A[i] up to ~1e6) that are borderline for Python when combined with multiple heavy data structures in tight loops.
Task structure that only needs to know whether a prime appears in more than one number, which can be solved with simple counters instead of sets.
transformation_steps: Limit the sieve to the necessary range by using max(A[i]) instead of a fixed 10^6 upper bound.
Build a smallest-prime-factor array (MINFact) once in a dedicated Class_1, storing for each integer its smallest prime divisor.

Refactor factorization so that for each number you only process each distinct prime factor once: divide out all powers of the prime in a tight inner loop, then increment a counter array for that prime.
Replace per-number and global sets of used primes with a single integer array var_2 indexed by prime, counting in how many input numbers each prime appears.
Compute the overall GCD of the array once, then decide between 'not coprime', 'pairwise coprime', and 'setwise coprime' using the GCD result and max(var_2).
Keep all inner loops on simple list and integer operations, avoiding Python set/dict operations in the hot path.

bottlenecks.detail: Using Python sets (var_8 per number and global var_9) for tracking prime usage creates many hash-table operations and allocations in the tight factorization loop.
func_2 always sieves up to 10^6 regardless of the maximum value in the input, potentially doing more work than needed when max(A) << 10^6.
Factorization loop divides by SPF for every exponent of each prime (handling multiplicity), which increases the number of iterations compared to processing each distinct prime factor once.
Multiple nested Python loops (sieve + factorization + set operations) create high interpreter overhead close to time limits for worst-case inputs.

complexity_delta.from: O(M log log M + n log M) time, O(M) space
complexity_delta.to: O(M log log M + n log M) time, O(M) space (plus small O(P) set overhead)
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotic complexity is unchanged; modifications mainly change constants and code organization. Sieve is parity-optimized; tracking of used primes switches from an indexed array to sets.

trigger_signals: Presence of a large fixed bound (10**6) with nested loops suggests sieve operations are a dominant cost and may benefit from minor optimizations (e.g., parity tricks).

Use of large auxiliary arrays for bookkeeping (var_9 of size 1e6+1) indicates potential for replacing them with more compact structures when the number of actually used entries is far smaller (only primes that appear).
Interleaving multiple logical tasks (gcd accumulation, pairwise coprime checks, factorization) inside one loop complicates reasoning and makes targeted micro-optimizations harder.
Use of eval(input()) for integer reading is a mild smell both for performance and safety, hinting that the code could be cleaned up.
transformation_steps: Encapsulated sieve construction into func_2, using a parity-aware initialization (evens default to factor 2, odds to sentinel) and iterating only over odd i >= 3 as prime candidates.
Replaced the array-based prime-usage tracking (var_9 with last-index markers) with set-based tracking (var_8 for primes in current number, var_9 for globally seen primes) in func_3.
Separated concerns: func_3 handles pairwise coprimality detection, func_1 computes global gcd, func_4 combines their results into a small state (0/1/2).
Reorganized main flow to compute the decision code via func_4 and map it to output strings instead of branching prints directly.
Moved gcd accumulation into its own tight loop (func_1) operating on the list of values, instead of interleaving gcd updates and factorization inside a single loop.

bottlenecks.detail: Sieve building var_7 with two explicit Python loops (for over 2..M and while over multiples) dominates runtime for large M=1e6.
For each of n numbers, while-loop repeatedly divides by a prime factor from var_7. This is necessary but adds O(n log M) work.
Two length-(M+1) integer arrays (var_7, var_9) consume noticeable memory (a few MB), though still acceptable for M=1e6.
Using eval(input()) instead of int(input()) for reading an integer is slower and unnecessary; minor but avoidable overhead.

complexity_delta.from: As implemented: O(M log M) numerical work with a very large constant due to O(M) Python slices and nested interpreter loops over ~M log M elements; plus O(N log A) for gcd.
complexity_delta.to: O(V log V + N log A) with all heavy work in tight integer loops and O(log A) factorization per value; no per-divisor slicing or large temporary lists.
complexity_delta.note: Both approaches conceptually scan divisors/multiples up to about 1e6, but moving from repeated slicing+summation to a precomputed SPF table radically reduces constant factors and memory churn, making Python fast enough for the constraints.

trigger_signals: Hard-coded upper bound var_1 = 10**6 + 2, combined with a full loop from 2 to var_1 regardless of actual max(A).
Use of slicing with a large step inside the main loop: `var_7[var_5::var_5]` executed ~1e6 times, indicating heavy allocation and per-iteration overhead.
Total operation count dominated by nested loops over a 1e6-sized structure, which is known to be borderline or too slow in pure Python for competitive-programming limits.
Absence of sieve-based factorization or other standard number-theory optimizations for coprimality checks, despite classic solutions using them.
transformation_steps: Replace the fixed-size frequency array over [0..1e6] with a limit based on max(A), reducing unnecessary work when max(A) < 1e6.
Remove the divisor loop that uses `var_7[var_5::var_5]` to count how many array elements are divisible by each candidate divisor.
Introduce an SPF (smallest prime factor) array `var_5` computed via a sieve-like nested loop, filling `var_5[x]` with the smallest prime dividing x.
Implement `func_2` to factor an integer using the SPF table in O(log A) time and return a set of its distinct prime factors.
Track a global set `var_3` of all primes seen so far; if `var_3 & current_primes` is non-empty, then some prime divides at least two elements, so the sequence is not pairwise coprime.
Track `var_4` as the running intersection of prime-factor sets of all elements; if this intersection is non-empty, there is a prime dividing all elements, meaning gcd > 1 (not coprime).
Encode the result as an index into ['pairwise coprime', 'setwise coprime', 'not coprime'] based on the pairwise failure flag and the final intersection.

bottlenecks.detail: Using a divisor-multiple summation with fresh list slicing for every candidate divisor: `var_7[var_5::var_5]`. While numerically O(M log M), it is implemented with very heavy Python-level overhead and repeated allocations.
Each `var_7[var_5::var_5]` allocates a new list of size ≈ M/var_5. Across all var_5 this causes allocation and garbage-collection of ~M log M elements, stressing the allocator and cache.
The outer loop runs about 1e6 iterations, each doing a Python-level slice and Python-level for-loop to sum counts; the interpreter overhead dominates for these sizes.

complexity_delta.from: Pseudo-sieve with slicing: O(MAX_A * H_MAX_A + N * log MAX_A) with very high constant factors due to ~MAX_A slice allocations and ~MAX_A * H_MAX_A element copies.
complexity_delta.to: Per-element prime factorization and gcd: O(N * sqrt(MAX_A) + N * log MAX_A) with low constant factors and no large temporary allocations in the inner loops.
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotically, O(N * sqrt(MAX_A)) may appear worse than O(MAX_A log MAX_A), but for N≈1e5, MAX_A≈1e6 the dominating factor is Python overhead: the slicing-based sieve is much slower in practice than tight loops over individual numbers with integer operations and boolean array marking.

trigger_signals: Inner loop contains var_7[var_5::var_5], i.e., full list slicing inside a hot double loop over up to 1e6 divisors.
Observed or expected TLE / slow performance despite seemingly acceptable O(MAX_A log MAX_A) theoretical complexity.
Use of Python lists and slicing rather than simple index-based stepping (for i in range(d, MAX_A+1, d)).
Memory usage spikes or high GC activity due to repeated allocation of large slices.
Problem constraints where MAX_A is large (~1e6) and N is moderately large (~1e5), making Python-level overhead critical.
transformation_steps: Replace the global divisor-counting loop over d with slicing (for var_5 in range(2, var_1): for var_4 in var_7[var_5::var_5]) by per-number prime factorization using trial division up to sqrt(a_i).

Introduce a boolean array indexed by prime factor to mark which primes have already appeared; a repeated prime indicates that two numbers share a common prime factor, so they are not pairwise coprime.
Maintain a running gcd over all numbers to determine whether the set is setwise coprime when pairwise coprimality fails.
Use fast I/O (sys.stdin.readline) and avoid large temporary data structures in tight loops.
Short-circuit loops once a non-pairwise-coprime condition is detected to avoid unnecessary factorization work on remaining elements.

bottlenecks.detail: Inner loop uses slicing var_7[var_5::var_5]; each slice allocates a new Python list and copies all referenced elements. The number of slices is MAX_A-1 (~1e6), and the total copied elements is ~MAX_A * H_MAX_A (~1e7). Allocation and copying cost dwarfs the arithmetic.
Each slice can be large (up to length ≈ MAX_A/2, MAX_A/3, ...). Creating and garbage-collecting many large lists causes significant memory churn and cache misses.
Double loop over d and slice elements is in pure Python with no vectorization or low-level optimization; the hot path is Python-level iteration of integers stored in lists.
Using eval(input()) for reading N is unnecessary and slower/unsafe, though its performance impact is negligible compared to the slicing issue.

complexity_delta.from: Time: O(10^6 log log 10^6 + N log 10^6) with high constant factors from dict/set usage. Space: O(10^6 + K) (SPF + defaultdict keys).
complexity_delta.to: Time: O(M log log M + N log M) with lower constants, where M = max(A[i]) ≤ 10^6 in the original constraints but not hard-coded. Space: O(M) for arrays only.
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotic orders remain similar, but the new version scales only with max(A[i]) instead of a fixed 10^6 and avoids heavy hash-based structures, giving a substantial real-world speedup and lower memory usage.

trigger_signals: Performance profiling shows most time spent in func_2: building the SPF table and performing many dict/set operations per number.
Observed TLE or near-timeout behavior on maximal test cases with N large (e.g., 1e5) and numbers clustered near 10^6.
High memory usage due to allocating a 10^6-length SPF table even when all A[i] are small.
Microbenchmarks showing that replacing dict/set with contiguous arrays yields significant speedup.
transformation_steps: Replace fixed sieve upper bound (10^6) with dynamic bound M = max(A[i]) so that preprocessing cost matches actual input scale.
Switch from collections.defaultdict(int) to a preallocated integer list indexed by prime value for counting prime-factor occurrences.
Eliminate per-number set allocations by counting prime occurrences directly via factorization with smallest prime factors stored in an array.
Use a class to encapsulate the SPF array (MINFact) and associated factorization method, improving locality and reuse of the sieve.
Replace custom recursive gcd implementation with math.gcd for faster and more robust GCD computation.
Perform GCD-based check first: if global gcd > 1, immediately classify as 'not coprime' without needing to rely on prime-count structure.

bottlenecks.detail: SPF array is always built up to 10^6, even if max(A[i]) is much smaller, wasting time and memory in smaller cases.
For every A[i], a new set is created to hold its distinct prime factors, and a defaultdict(int) is updated for each factor; this creates many Python objects and hash-table operations.
All N numbers are fully factorized using the SPF, even though only multiplicities ≥ 2 matter for deciding pairwise coprimality.
Custom recursive gcd has more Python-call overhead than math.gcd, though this is a secondary bottleneck compared to the sieve and dict/set work.

complexity_delta.from: O(10^6 log log 10^6 + N * log A_max) time with a fixed sieve bound and heavier Python-level per-factor set operations
complexity_delta.to: O(max(A_i) log log max(A_i) + N * log A_max) time with lighter per-number work and smaller sieve when max(A_i) < 10^6
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotic behavior in worst case (max(A_i) = 10^6) is similar, but the fast version reduces the sieve range when possible and uses more efficient set operations, improving practical performance.

trigger_signals: N up to around 10^5–10^5+, A_i up to 10^6, with strict time limits typical of competitive programming tasks.
Use of Python sets and loops inside tight inner loops (per prime factor) suggesting high constant-factor overhead.
SPF sieve built up to a worst-case constant bound (10**6) regardless of actual input, suggesting avoidable work when values are smaller.
Multiple membership checks and updates on Python sets on every factorization step, instead of aggregating per-number work and using efficient set operations like intersection implemented in C.
transformation_steps: Change SPF construction to depend on the actual maximum value in the input (max(var_1)) instead of a fixed 10**6 bound, avoiding unnecessary sieve work when values are smaller.

Factor each input number into a set of distinct prime factors via an inner helper (func_2) and then perform a single set intersection with the global prime set to detect shared primes, instead of per-factor membership checks in two sets.
Read all input at once via open(0).read().split() and unpack (n, *a) in one operation, which reduces Python I/O overhead compared to repeated input() calls.
Use functools.reduce(gcd, var_1) directly to compute the global gcd in a tight loop, instead of a manual loop with repeated function calls and external state.
Short-circuit the pairwise coprime decision as soon as a conflict is found, and only then compute the global gcd if needed to classify between setwise and not coprime.

bottlenecks.detail: func_2 always builds SPF up to 10**6 regardless of the actual maximum value in var_1. If max(A_i) << 10^6, a large portion of the sieve is wasted.

Prime factorization loop in func_3 performs multiple Python-level set operations per prime factor: two membership checks (in var_8 and var_9), plus adds to both sets, inside a while-loop. This introduces significant constant overhead compared to doing set operations on prebuilt factor sets.
Using input() and even eval(input()) is slower and less direct than bulk-reading from stdin as text and mapping to integers.
The pairwise-coprime check is interleaved with factorization via repeated membership tests, instead of factoring each number once to a set and using fast built-in set intersection.

complexity_delta.from: O(M log log M + N log A_max) time, O(M) space
complexity_delta.to: O(M log log M + N log A_max) time, O(M) space
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotic bounds are unchanged, but the fast version reduces Python-level work (less scanning, smaller hot data structures, early exits) and tends to run faster in practice for M≈1e6.

trigger_signals: Constraints include Ai up to 1e6 and N up to around 2e5, making O(M log log M) sieving borderline when implemented with heavy Python-level loops and extra scans.
Presence of nested while-loops over multiples with Python conditionals inside suggests potential constant-factor issues even when the big-O is optimal.
Use of a large global frequency array followed by a full max() scan across ~1e6 entries indicates unnecessary work when only primes that appear in the input matter.
TLE or near-timeout behavior on worst-case tests where many numbers are large and close to 1e6, stressing both sieve and factorization stages.
transformation_steps: Use a compact largest-prime-factor table (var_14) instead of a frequency array of size M for counting prime occurrences across all numbers.
Avoid a full max() scan over a 1e6-length array by tracking only primes that actually appear (via a defaultdict) and short-circuiting as soon as a prime divides at least two numbers.

Design the sieve so that the outer loop runs over all integers but the inner loop executes only when the current index is still its own factor (i.e., a prime), reducing work on composites via a simple if-continue check.
Factor each number by repeated division using largest prime factors; use a per-number set to deduplicate primes instead of relying on inner loops over smallest prime factors.
Retain a separate global gcd computation to distinguish between pairwise and setwise coprimeness while keeping this phase O(N log A_max).
Use buffered input via sys.stdin.readline for slightly faster I/O in tight constraints.

bottlenecks.detail: func_1 uses a while-loop over multiples for each prime (var_7 from 2 upwards). This is O(M log log M) but with a relatively high Python-level loop overhead.
After counting prime occurrences into var_2 (length ≈ 1e6), it calls max(var_2), scanning the entire array even though only a small prefix is touched for typical inputs.
Maintains two large arrays var_3 and var_2 of size ~1e6+5 each, plus a temporary boolean array var_10 in func_1; this is fine for constraints but still heavy in Python.
The sieve and factorization logic are entirely in Python, with nested loops over up to 1e6, making it sensitive to constant factors and interpreter overhead.
Uses eval(input()) for reading N, which is slower and unnecessary compared to int(input()) or buffered I/O, though this is a minor cost relative to the sieve.

complexity_delta.from: Global sieve up to fixed M=1e6 plus per-number factorization and set tracking: O(M log log M + N log A_max) time, O(M) space.
complexity_delta.to: Per-number trial division up to sqrt(A_max) with boolean prime-usage array: O(N * sqrt(A_max)) worst-case time, O(A_max) space.
complexity_delta.note: Although the asymptotic worst-case of trial division is higher, for A_max = 1e6 and typical inputs, avoiding the heavy 1e6-scale sieve and Python-set overhead yields faster actual runtime.

trigger_signals: Profiling or inspection shows func_2 (the sieve) dominating CPU time despite relatively small input size.
Recognizing that 10^6-scale nested Python loops are expensive compared to per-number trial division for N ~ 1e5.
Observation that set-based prime tracking is heavier than necessary when indices are bounded and an array can be used.
Awareness of competitive programming patterns: fast I/O and avoiding eval, using lightweight arrays instead of generic containers.
transformation_steps: Replace the global sieve-based func_2(10**6) with per-number factorization func_1 using trial division up to sqrt(n), shrinking n as factors are removed.
Swap the global set-based prime tracking (var_9) with a preallocated boolean array var_4 of size max(Ai)+1 to mark whether a prime factor was already seen.

Inline pairwise/setwise/not-coprime logic into a single main loop, computing gcd cumulatively (func_2: Euclid) while also checking pairwise coprimality, instead of separate passes via func_3 and func_1.
Improve I/O by using sys.stdin.readline and direct int conversion instead of input() with eval(), reducing parsing overhead.
Avoid constructing large unused tables by sizing structures (var_4) to max(var_2) rather than a fixed upper bound of 10^6.

bottlenecks.detail: Manual sieve (func_2(10**6)) uses pure Python loops over all numbers up to 1e6, including composite multiples; this dominates runtime despite its acceptable asymptotic complexity.
The sieve is always built up to a fixed bound 10^6 even if max(var_1) is much smaller, wasting time and memory on unused entries.
Tracking prime usage with Python sets (var_8, var_9) incurs hashing and allocation overhead for each factor; a boolean array keyed by prime would be cheaper.
Using eval(input()) for reading integers is slower and unnecessary; plain int(input()) or sys.stdin.readline is faster and safer.

complexity_delta.from: O(M log M + n log A) time, O(M + n) space (array-based SPF + per-value factorization).
complexity_delta.to: O(M log M + M log log M + n) time, O(M + n) space (dict-based sieve + multiple-counting per prime).
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotic time and space remain the same order in M and n. The new approach removes the per-number factorization term but adds a multiples-summing pass over all primes and replaces lists with dictionaries, increasing constant factors. Net performance is likely worse in Python despite the conceptual simplification.

trigger_signals: Presence of a full-range sieve up to about 10^6 with nested loops (var_6 outer and var_7 inner) in Python, which is near the performance limit for typical contest time constraints.
Use of while var_6 * var_7 <= var_11 style loops instead of range-based stepping, hinting at avoidable overhead in inner loops.
Repeated factorization of up to n (~2e5) integers, suggesting potential gains via aggregated counting over multiples rather than per-number factorization.
Hard-coded sieve maximum (10**6 + 5) independent of actual input maximum, indicating wasted work when inputs are smaller.
transformation_steps: Switched from a list-based smallest-prime-factor array (var_3) to a dictionary-based primality table (var_3), losing direct factorization capability but keeping prime identification.
Removed SPF-based factorization func_3 and instead counted occurrences of each value in a frequency dictionary var_2 over the range [1..max(A_i)].
Introduced a prime-wise accumulation loop: for each prime p, sum frequencies of all multiples of p by stepping through var_2 in increments of p, storing results in var_4[p].
Replaced the gcd-based classification (gcd != 1 ⇒ 'not coprime') with a check on max prime multiple count: max(var_4.values()) compared against 1 and n to decide pairwise/setwise/not coprime.
Adjusted sieve upper bound to depend on max(A_i) instead of a fixed 10^6+5, avoiding some unnecessary work when max(A_i) is significantly smaller.

bottlenecks.detail: Sieve implementation iterates var_6 all the way to var_11 and marks multiples starting at 2*var_6 instead of var_6^2 and without stopping at sqrt(M). This makes the sieve O(M log M) instead of the more efficient O(M log log M).
Sieve upper bound is hard-coded to 10^6+5 regardless of the actual maximum input value. For smaller test cases this performs unnecessary work up to the fixed maximum.
Inner while-loop in func_1 uses multiplication var_6 * var_7 at each step; this adds overhead compared with a for-loop using range(start, limit, step).
Using eval(input()) to parse an integer is slower and less safe than int(input()), but this is a minor cost compared to the sieve work.

complexity_delta.from: Effective O(N + M log M) with heavy work done in C (slicing + sum) and relatively small Python overhead.
complexity_delta.to: O(M log log M + N log M) with most work done in Python loops (sieve + factorization), similar asymptotic size but much worse constant factors.
complexity_delta.note: While both variants are roughly linear or n log n in the value range M≈1e6, the original uses C-level operations for the expensive aggregation, whereas the modified version shifts most work into Python loops, leading to significantly slower real-world performance despite reasonable Big-O.

trigger_signals: Presence of nested Python loops over a large numeric range (up to 10**6) for sieve operations.
Multiple while-loops inside factorization for each input element, indicating repeated Python-level integer operations.
Fixed upper-bound sieve (10**6+5) independent of actual max(A_i), suggesting potential overwork.
Large auxiliary lists (size ~1e6) being iterated multiple times, hinting at cache-inefficient and interpreter-heavy code paths.
Replacement of vectorized/built-in aggregations (slicing and sum) with manual Python logic.
transformation_steps: Replaced the frequency-based multiple counting via var_2[d::d] and sum(...) with an explicit sieve (func_1) to compute smallest prime factors for all numbers up to ~1e6.
Added a gcd reduction over all array elements to early-detect the 'not coprime' case.
Introduced a per-element factorization routine (func_3) that uses the SPF table to increment prime-factor counts, instead of counting multiples directly via slicing.
Allocated and traversed multiple large arrays (SPF table, sieve flags, prime count array) entirely in Python, increasing memory traffic and interpretation overhead.
Removed the C-accelerated slice-sum pattern that compactly processes multiples of each candidate divisor.

bottlenecks.detail: The sieve in func_1 uses pure Python nested loops: for var_6 in range(2, var_11+1) and an inner while over var_7 to mark multiples. Each composite marking is a Python-level iteration, making O(M log log M) operations expensive at M≈1e6.

Inner loop uses var_7 incremented by 1 (while var_6 * var_7 <= var_11) instead of stepping by var_6, causing extra multiplications and condition checks and increasing constant factors compared to a more cache-friendly or vectorized sieve.
func_3 factorizes each value with nested while-loops. Even though the number of prime factors is small, each step is in Python and adds noticeable overhead for N up to ~1e5.
func_1 is called with a fixed upper bound 10**6 + 5 regardless of the actual maximum value in the input. If max(A_i) is significantly smaller, a large portion of the sieve work is wasted.
Three arrays of size ≈1e6 (var_3, var_10 inside func_1, and var_2) increase memory footprint and cache pressure, further slowing down Python-level traversals.

complexity_delta.from:
Time: O(MAX_A * log MAX_A + N * log MAX_A), Space: O(MAX_A) with MAX_A fixed at 1e6 regardless of input.
In practice: ~1e6 log(1e6) sieve steps + full factorization of N values and a separate pass over counts.
complexity_delta.to:
Time: O(maxA * log log maxA + N * log maxA), Space: O(maxA) where maxA = max(A[i]) is input-dependent.
In practice: sieving only up to the true maxA, early exit on detecting non-pairwise coprime, and iterative GCD.
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotic bounds are similar in terms of symbolic MAX_A, but the new code uses maxA instead of a fixed 1e6 and improves constants (log -> log log for sieve, eliminates extra passes, and adds early exit). For cases where maxA << 1e6 or pairwise non-coprime is detected early, the speedup can be substantial.

trigger_signals: Presence of a large fixed constant (10**6) in sieve bounds unrelated to actual input values, indicating possible over-sieving.
Nested loops of the form for i in range(2, M): for j in range(i, M, i) for prime precomputation, combined with M set to a worst-case constant rather than depending on max(A).
Post-processing patterns like list(var_5.values()) followed by a separate scan to check for a simple condition (e.g., any count >= 2) after all work has been done, instead of checking incrementally.
Recursive GCD implementation (func_1) in performance-critical code paths, where iterative GCD is known to be cheaper in Python.
Factorization and coprime checks performed for all N numbers with no early termination path when non-pairwise coprimality is already evident.
transformation_steps: Replace recursive GCD (func_1) with an iterative Euclidean loop: while b: a, b = b, a % b. This removes recursion overhead and is typically faster in Python.
Change the SPF sieve upper bound from a hard-coded 10**6 to max(A), i.e., allocate var_2 of length max(A)+1 instead of a static 1e6+1 array.

Use a more efficient sieve pattern: when var_2[i] == 0, mark var_2[i] = i and then mark multiples starting from 2*i. This behaves like a standard SPF sieve with roughly O(maxA log log maxA) operations.

Introduce a global set (var_11) to store prime factors that have already appeared; during factorization of each number, collect its distinct prime factors in a per-number set and immediately return False from func_2 if any factor is already in var_11 (early exit for non-pairwise coprime).
Remove the defaultdict-based counting and final scan of prime counts; the set-based membership check replaces the counter and eliminates an O(#distinct_primes) post-processing pass.
Keep the GCD-based setwise coprime test but implement it as func_3 with iterative GCD inside a loop across all numbers, only invoked if pairwise coprimality fails.

bottlenecks.detail: The sieve array var_14 is always built up to 10**6 even if max(A) is much smaller, causing up to ~1e6 log(1e6) operations regardless of input scale.

The pairwise coprime check computes prime factorization for all N numbers and only then inspects counts in var_5, missing the opportunity to exit early as soon as a prime appears in two different numbers.
After building var_5, it creates list(var_5.values()) and iterates it just to check if any count >= 2, adding an extra O(#distinct_primes) pass instead of checking during counting.
func_1 implements GCD recursively; although depth is only O(log A_max), iterative GCD avoids Python recursion overhead and is more cache-friendly.
Allocating an array of length 1e6+1 for smallest prime factors even when max(A) << 1e6 increases memory footprint and cache pressure unnecessarily.

complexity_delta.from: O(M log log M + N log A_max) time, O(M + N) space
complexity_delta.to: O(M log log M + N log A_max) time, O(M + K + N) space (K = distinct primes used)
complexity_delta.note: Both versions share the same big-O behavior dominated by the sieve and factorization. The main differences are in constant factors and data structures (arrays vs dictionary) and in early-exit behavior for the pairwise-coprime check.

trigger_signals: Input bounds reaching up to 1e6 for values make naive O(A_i) or trial-division per element too slow, motivating sieve-based precomputation.
Need to test pairwise coprimality across up to about 1e5 numbers makes an O(N^2) gcd pairwise check infeasible; factorization-based global prime tracking becomes necessary.
Profiling or reasoning shows that dictionary operations can be a noticeable constant-factor cost, leading to a design using direct indexing arrays for prime tracking.
Recursive gcd calls in pure Python can be slightly slower and risk recursion depth issues, which motivates switching to `math.gcd` or iterative gcd.
transformation_steps: Changed gcd implementation from a custom recursive function to `math.gcd` in the modified (slow) version, which is normally faster per call but not asymptotically different.

Replaced dictionary-based prime occurrence counting with an indexed array `var_9` of size 1e6 that records the last index where each prime was seen; this trades hash-table overhead for dense array lookups.

Modified the pairwise-coprimality detection strategy: the modified version updates `var_8` during factorization and can short-circuit further factorization once a repeated prime across different indices is detected.

Adjusted the sieve representation from an explicit `var_14 = [i for i in range(M+1)]` initialization and multi-assignment for multiples to a zero-initialized array where primes are detected by `var_7[p] == 0`; both end up storing a prime factor per integer and have similar O(M log log M) cost.
Kept overall classification logic: first attempt pairwise coprime, and if that fails, use gcd of the whole set to distinguish setwise vs not coprime.

bottlenecks.detail: The outer loop from 2 to 1e6 with an inner while stepping through multiples performs about M log log M Python-level operations; this is the single biggest fixed cost per test run.

For each input value, repeated divisions using the factor table incur multiple Python integer operations; in worst case (values near 1e6 with small prime factors) this is ~log A_i divisions per number.
The inner factorization loop is guarded by `while var_8 and var_4 > 1`, introducing branch checks on every iteration. This is necessary for early exit but adds some overhead.
Using arrays of length 1e6 for both the factor table and the last-seen-index table stresses cache/memory bandwidth; all are touched at least once in the sieve and repeatedly during factorization.

from.time: O(N log log N + Q * m^2 log m) with m = 100 primes per query (~5050 sums)
from.space: O(N + m^2) with m = 100 (≈5050 per-query sums)

to.time: O(N log log N + Q * m'^2 log m') with m' = 30 primes per query (~465 sums)
to.space: O(N + m'^2) with m' = 30 (≈465 per-query sums)

complexity_delta.note: Asymptotic forms are the same in terms of N and Q, but the per-query constant factors are significantly reduced (≈10x fewer pair sums). Sieve constants are also improved via slice operations.

trigger_signals: Profiling or reasoning shows high time spent in Python-level loops over combinatorial pairs (combinations_with_replacement and append).
Each query performs thousands of arithmetic operations and a sort on several thousand elements, which is expensive when Q is large.
The sieve's inner loop is written in pure Python, which is known to be slower than slice-based or vectorized approaches.
Observation that the problem only requires a limited prefix of the ordered sum sequence, making it unnecessary to use as many as 100 primes per query.
transformation_steps: Sieve optimization: Replace the nested inner loop for marking composites with list slice assignment: var_6[2 * i + 1 :: i + 1] = [0, 0, ...], pushing the heavy work into C-level operations.

Prime representation tweak: Switch from a boolean list var_11 and explicit prime list var_10 to a single list var_6 that stores primality flags directly indexed by value-1, and infer primes on the fly.

Per-query prime collection: Instead of taking the next 100 primes from a precomputed list via bisect_right, collect a smaller fixed number (30) of primes greater than the query value by scanning forward and checking var_6.

Combinatorics refactor: Replace combinations_with_replacement over 100 primes and a Python append-loop with itertools.combinations over 30 primes plus a separate list of doubled primes, built using list comprehensions.
Sum generation and sorting: Build all pair sums in one expression and sort them once: sorted([p+q for combinations] + [2*p for p in primes]), reducing Python overhead and the size of the sorted list.
Loop style tweaks: Use while 1 and minimal branching to reduce Python control-flow overhead in the input-processing loop.

bottlenecks.detail: for var_4, var_6 in cwr(var_3, 2): var_7.append(var_4 + var_6) performs ~5050 iterations in Python per query; across many queries this dominates runtime.
var_7.sort() sorts ~5050 elements per query; sorting cost is non-trivial when Q is large.
Always using the next 100 primes, even if the requested index k is small, increases the constant factor (m^2 with m = 100).
Inner sieve loop uses Python for var_6 in range(var_4 * var_4, var_1, var_4): var_11[var_6] = False, which is less cache-friendly and slower than a slice-based or more vectorized marking approach.

complexity_delta.from: O(M log log M + N log M) time, O(M) space (SPF sieve + per-element factorization + gcd)
complexity_delta.to: O(M log M + N) time, O(M) space (frequency array + divisibility sweep)
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotically both are near-linear in M with a slowly growing factor. The modified code removes explicit factorization and the global SPF table, instead using a divisor-sweep over frequencies. In practice, the use of Python slicing in `sum(var_2[d::d])` may increase constant factors despite similar big-O.

trigger_signals: Detection that a full SPF sieve up to a fixed upper bound (10^6) might be unnecessary when the actual maximum input value is smaller.
Concern about the overhead of per-element factorization loops in Python, which can be significant when N is large.
Observing that coprimality properties can be deduced via counting multiples, without constructing explicit prime factorizations.
Desire to avoid recursive gcd computations and additional data structures (defaultdict, sets) in the hot path.
transformation_steps: Removed the global smallest-prime-factor table construction (SPF sieve) that ran up to a fixed 10^6 irrespective of input values.
Replaced per-element prime factorization via the SPF table with a single frequency array var_2 sized to max(A_i), populated by counting occurrences of each input value.

Introduced a divisor-sweep loop over d in [2..max(A_i)] that aggregates counts of numbers divisible by d via `sum(var_2[d::d])`, tracking the maximum such count (var_6) to detect whether any prime divides at least two numbers.
Eliminated the explicit gcd-based setwise check from the original solution and instead inferred setwise vs not coprime using `var_6 < var_5` as the condition for 'setwise coprime'.
Simplified input handling by using input() directly instead of sys.stdin.readline and removed recursion for gcd entirely (no gcd computation).

bottlenecks.detail: Sieve is always built up to var_1 = 10**6, regardless of the actual maximum value in the input; if max(A_i) << 10^6 this does unnecessary work.
The sieve loop `for var_6 in range(2, var_1 + 1)` with inner `for var_10 in range(var_6, var_1 + 1, var_6)` performs on the order of M log log M iterations (~2e7 when M=1e6) in pure Python.
Each number is factorized via repeated division using the SPF table. This per-element loop adds O(N log M) operations and Python-level loops / set operations for each input value.
The GCD function func_1 is written recursively, incurring Python call overhead for each modulus step. This is a minor cost but still slower than an iterative gcd or math.gcd.
Using eval on input (e.g., int(eval(var_7()))) is slower and unsafe compared to direct int() parsing, though this is a small fraction of total time.

complexity_delta.from: O(M log log M + n log A) time, O(M + n) space, with M fixed at 10**6 regardless of inputs
complexity_delta.to: O(n) time (for fixed A ≤ 10**6) + O(n log A) for gcd, O(n) space
complexity_delta.note: By eliminating the SPF sieve and using a compact precomputed prime set for trial division, the solution removes the dependency on the global numeric bound M from both time and space. For the given constraints (A ≤ 10**6), the cost of prime trials is effectively a small constant per number.

trigger_signals: Precomputation that iterates over a large fixed numeric range (up to 10**6) independent of actual input size.
Nested loops where an inner loop steps over multiples of every candidate base up to a large bound in pure Python.
Auxiliary arrays or tables whose size is tied to max(a_i) rather than to n, especially when max(a_i) is large but fixed (1e6) and the language is not optimized for tight numeric loops.
Presence of a precomputed prime table that is unused or shadowed, indicating an opportunity to replace per-run sieving with constant data.
transformation_steps: Delete func_2 and the construction of the 10**6-sized SPF array; stop calling func_2(10**6) in func_3.
Introduce or reuse a global constant table of primes up to sqrt(maxA) (here stored in var_9) and ensure it is not shadowed by a local variable inside func_3.
Replace SPF-based factorization in func_3 (`var_2[var_3]` and repeated division) with trial division by primes from var_9, plus a final check for a leftover >1 factor.
Keep the high-level coprimality logic the same: maintain a global set of seen prime factors for pairwise check, and use the gcd over all numbers to distinguish setwise vs not coprime.
Avoid unnecessary large global structures whose contents are never used (e.g., do not both build an SPF and a large global prime set).

bottlenecks.detail: func_2(10**6) always builds an SPF sieve up to 1e6, even if the maximum input value is much smaller. This precomputation dominates runtime in Python.
The sieve inner loop `for var_6 in range(var_5, var_7 + 1, var_5)` runs for many var_5, giving roughly M * (1/2 + 1/3 + 1/5 + ...) ≈ O(M log log M) pure-Python iterations.
The SPF list of size ~1e6 plus per-number sets for prime factors increase memory usage and can reduce cache locality. The SPF dominates space at O(M).
A large global set var_9 containing many primes is defined but immediately shadowed inside func_3 (`var_9 = set()`), so its construction is useless overhead if executed in the real code.

complexity_delta.from: O(M log log M + N log A_max) time with heavy Python-level nested loops and set operations; O(M + N) space.
complexity_delta.to: O(M log M + N) time where the log-factor work is mostly done in C via slicing and sum; O(M + N) space.
complexity_delta.note: Big-O is comparable, but the key improvement is moving the dominant inner work from Python loops and dynamic sets into contiguous array operations implemented in C, dramatically reducing interpreter overhead.

trigger_signals: Presence of a hand-written sieve with nested Python loops up to a large fixed limit (10**6) regardless of input maximum.
Use of per-element prime factorization and repeated divisions in Python for every input number.
Allocation and update of Python sets inside an inner loop over prime factors, indicating heavy overhead for membership checks and hashing.
Observed or anticipated runtime dominated by millions of Python-level iterations rather than vectorized or built-in operations.
Awareness that frequency-based divisor counting can be implemented via strided slices and sum, pushing heavy work into C.
transformation_steps: Replace global SPF sieve func_2 up to fixed bound 10**6 with a frequency array sized only up to max(var_1).
Eliminate per-number prime factorization and per-number temporary sets; instead, count frequency of each integer value directly in var_2.

For each candidate divisor d from 2 to max(var_1), compute how many inputs are divisible by d as sum(var_2[d::d]) using slicing and built-in sum, which are implemented in C and avoid Python-level inner loops.
Track the maximum such count (var_6) and classify as pairwise/setwise/not coprime based on comparisons with N, replacing explicit factor overlap tracking via sets var_8 and var_9.
Remove the separate gcd-based pass (func_1) since the divisibility-count method already distinguishes all three coprimality cases.

bottlenecks.detail: func_2 implements the sieve entirely in Python with nested for-loops over up to 10**6 values and their multiples. This leads to ~10^7+ Python-level iterations, which is expensive in CP time limits.
func_2(10**6) always sieves up to 1e6 regardless of the actual maximum value in var_1. For inputs where max(A_i) << 1e6, a large fraction of the sieve work is wasted.

In func_3, for each input number a fresh set var_8 is created and populated with its prime factors, while var_9 tracks global factors. Frequent set allocations and membership checks add significant constant-factor overhead.
Repeated divisions var_3 //= var_2[var_3] cause non-sequential accesses to the SPF array and repeated Python integer operations, which are slower than contiguous scanning/aggregation with built-ins.

complexity_delta.from: O(M log M + N log A) time with large constants from per-iteration list slicing; O(M + N) memory from frequency array and input list.
complexity_delta.to: O(M log log M + N log A) time using an SPF sieve shared across all numbers; O(M + N) memory for the SPF table and input.
complexity_delta.note: Key improvement is switching from scanning all multiples via fresh slices for each divisor to a one-time sieve plus per-number factorization with set-based tracking.

trigger_signals: Constraints where max(A_i) is as large as 1e6 and N is up to ~2e5, making any O(M * (#divisors) with heavy allocation) approach borderline in Python.
Profiling or timing shows most runtime spent inside the loop over var_5 and in creation of slices var_7[var_5::var_5].
Performance issues or TLE when using a divisor-counting approach that relies on Python list slicing in nested loops.
Recognition that checking pairwise coprimality can be expressed more naturally via prime factorization and reuse of a sieve rather than scanning all multiples of each potential divisor.
transformation_steps: Replace the frequency-array plus full-multiples scanning approach with a smallest-prime-factor sieve (func_2) computed once up to the maximum possible A_i (1e6).
For each input value, repeatedly divide by its smallest prime factor from the SPF table to obtain its distinct prime factors in O(log A) time.

Maintain a global set of primes that have appeared in previous numbers and a per-number set to ignore repeated prime factors within the same number; if a prime is new for this number but already in the global set, detect non-pairwise coprimality immediately.
Keep the gcd fold over all numbers (func_1) to decide between setwise coprime and not coprime after the pairwise test.
Eliminate the O(M) outer loop that scans every potential divisor and avoid constructing list slices var_7[var_5::var_5], thus removing ~1e6 list allocations and associated overhead.

bottlenecks.detail: The pattern var_7[var_5::var_5] constructs a fresh list on every iteration (about 1e6 lists), each of size ~M/var_5. This creates substantial allocation and garbage-collection overhead in addition to the arithmetic.
Even when var_3 > 1 (already proves not pairwise coprime for that divisor), the inner loop does not break, and the outer loop continues through all remaining var_5 up to M.
Repeatedly scanning strided slices over a large array (size ~1e6) with new list allocations harms cache locality compared to a single precomputed structure reused across numbers.

O(8 * (N log N + N + M)) time, O(N) space->O(8 * (N log N + N + M)) time, O(N) space

O(N * M) (worst O(N^2) when M ≈ N)->O(N log N)
O(N * M) (worst case O(N^2)) time, O(N) space->O(N log N) time, O(N) space

O(N * M)->O(N log N)
