top_triggers: A loop over all indices where the body calls sum() on the whole list every iteration.
A loop variable (var_4) that is never used in its body, indicating redundant repetition of the same computation.
A per-iteration full scan over all remaining elements to choose a 'best' candidate, indicative of an unoptimized greedy approach.
A search range for p that depends on max(a) - min(a) rather than on known small constraints from the problem statement.
Algorithmic intent (formula-based O(n)) doesn’t match the actual implementation (O(n^2)).
An outer loop variable that is never used inside its body, suggesting redundant repeated work.
An outer loop whose induction variable (var_4) is never used in downstream calculations, suggesting redundant repeated work.
Arithmetic on every element of an array inside Python loops instead of leveraging vectorized libraries like NumPy.
Awareness from problem statement/constraints that all a_i lie in a small, fixed interval such as [1,100].
Calling list.index() repeatedly over overlapping regions of the list.
Code pattern where the same minimum computation is performed multiple times with identical initial conditions and no state dependence on the outer loop variable.
Comparing to the original/fast version reveals that the search over t only needs one pass over the value range, not two nested passes.
Competitive programming environment where importing heavy libraries and using slow IO are known to cause timeouts.
Complexity smell: three nested loops over n and value ranges, where the conceptual problem only requires trying each candidate p once.
Constraints inferred to be around N up to 1e5, where O(N^2) is too slow.
Constraints likely of order 1e5, making O(N*M) infeasible when M is comparable to N.
top_steps: Accumulate the squared differences in a simple inner loop using integers, maintaining the running minimum cost in a Python variable.
Add a small optimization/edge case: if n == 1, directly print 0, since any p equal to the sole element yields zero cost.
Add a special-case early return for n == 1, where the optimal cost is trivially 0, avoiding unnecessary loops.
Added multiple thin wrapper functions for input parsing (func_1, func_2, etc.), which add overhead without improving algorithmic complexity.
After computing var_5 for each candidate (var_1 + 1), compare it to var_4.
Avoid constructing augmented 4-element records per point (`[sum(_abc)] + _abc`) and instead compute only a scalar score per point (`x*sx + y*sy + z*sz`).
Avoid re-creating and handling larger temporary lists in inner loops; work with simple integers wherever possible to minimize Python-level overhead.
Avoid repeated recalculation of the same initial sum `sum((var_7[0] - var_7[i])**2)` on every outer iteration by either computing it once or directly folding it into the main candidate enumeration.
Avoid sorting the input array; simply read the values into a list and operate directly on them.
Avoided sorting the input list by directly iterating over values; min/max are not needed when the candidate range is known a priori.
Change the outer loop to iterate p in increasing order starting from the lower bound (1) and track the best value seen so far.
Collapse the explicit nested loops over indices into a single loop over candidate centers, with the per-candidate cost computed in a vectorized way using NumPy arrays.
Collapse the search for the best p into a single loop: directly iterate `var_4` as the candidate p over the range from min(a) to max(a).
Compute squared differences, sum across elements for each candidate p, and then take the minimum in a single pass using NumPy reductions.
Compute the final answer as the sum over i>=1 of a[i] * prefix_sum[i-1] in one linear pass, removing the inner O(N) summation.
Compute the resulting sums of coordinates for the chosen M points and turn them back into |sum x|+|sum y|+|sum z|; track the maximum over the 8 patterns.

complexity_delta.from: O(n^2) time, O(n) space (fast_code: direct double loop with minimal per-iteration work)
complexity_delta.to: O(n^2) time, O(n) space (slow_code: formula-style loop but with an O(n) sum() inside each iteration)
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotic complexity remains O(n^2) in both versions, but slow_code roughly multiplies the constant factor by an additional scan of the entire array per outer iteration, making it substantially slower in practice. For large n both are too slow, but slow_code is even worse.

trigger_signals: sum(var_1) appears inside a loop where var_1 is not modified, indicating repeated full-array scans.
The presence of a precomputed sum (var_5 = sum(var_1)) that is never used, suggesting a bug or missed optimization.
Runtime dominated by Python-level loops and built-ins over large n, where profiling would show sum() being called n times.
Algorithmic intent (formula-based O(n)) doesn’t match the actual implementation (O(n^2)).
transformation_steps: Replaced the explicit double nested loop over i < j with a single loop over indices trying to use the identity sum_{i<j} a[i]*a[j] = (sum(a)^2 - sum(a^2))/2, or a variant of it.
Introduced a precomputation var_5 = sum(var_1), but then failed to use it inside the loop, instead recalculating sum(var_1) on every iteration.
Kept the underlying method as O(n^2) by combining an O(n)-per-iteration sum() with an O(n)-length loop, neutralizing any potential gain from the formula-based approach.
Added multiple thin wrapper functions for input parsing (func_1, func_2, etc.), which add overhead without improving algorithmic complexity.
Preserved the use of eval in input handling, retaining both its overhead and its security risks.

bottlenecks.detail: sum(var_1) is recomputed in every iteration of the loop, even though var_1 never changes. This turns a would-be O(n) loop (for a formula-based solution) into O(n^2), and compared to fast_code it roughly doubles the constant factor of the O(n^2) algorithm.
var_5 = sum(var_1) is computed before the loop but never used. The loop instead calls sum(var_1) again, wasting the precomputed value and causing unnecessary repeated scans.

Multiple small input-wrapper functions (func_1, func_2, etc.) add call overhead without algorithmic benefit. It’s minor compared to the O(n^2) cost but still makes slow_code slower than a direct implementation.
Using eval in int(eval(input())) is unnecessary and potentially unsafe, though it doesn’t dominate runtime. int(input()) would be sufficient and slightly faster.

complexity_delta.from: O(n) time, O(n) space
complexity_delta.to: O(n log n) time, O(n) space
complexity_delta.note: The modified solution introduces a global sort, increasing asymptotic time from linear to n log n, but greatly simplifies the construction logic and removes multiple passes and list mutations. Given typical constraints (n up to about 2e5), O(n log n) is still easily within limits.

trigger_signals: Original code uses several full-array scans to extract structural information that is naturally exposed by sorting (min, max, and sign structure).
List deletions and front insertions are red flags in Python performance, as each entails O(n) element shifting and interacts poorly with large n.
The objective (maximize a chained subtraction expression and output a sequence of operations) matches a known greedy pattern whose standard solution is sort-based.
The branching around cases (presence/absence of positives and negatives, smallest |a[i]|) complicates reasoning, suggesting a more global viewpoint can give a simpler invariant and implementation.
transformation_steps: Replace ad-hoc multi-pass scans (for first positive, first negative, and minimal |a[i]|) with a single global sort of the array.
Eliminate the separate absolute-value array by using a single pass over the sorted list to compute the maximum achievable value via the formula: max_value = max - min + sum(abs(middle_elements)).

Standardize the construction order: start from the largest element as the initial accumulator and sequentially combine it with other elements in sorted order, deciding which operand is subtracted from which based on whether we want to increase or decrease the current accumulator.
Simplify list handling by removing all deletions and front insertions; instead, use pure index-based iteration over the sorted array.
Unify the printing pattern for the pairwise operations into a single loop that runs exactly n-1 times, avoiding the heavily branched logic of the original implementation.

bottlenecks.detail: Three separate O(n) loops are used to locate the first positive index, first negative index, and the index of the element with minimal absolute value. These could be combined into a single pass.
Use of del var_1[idx] and creating new lists with [element] + var_1 incurs O(n) element moves each time. While constant in count, they increase constant factors and hurt cache behavior.
Complex branching around positions of first positive/negative values introduces multiple code paths and makes it harder to reason about correctness and optimize or refactor safely.
Maintaining a separate absolute-value array var_2 doubles memory traffic and space usage compared to computing needed quantities directly from the sorted list or in a single pass.

complexity_delta.from: O(n^2) time, O(n) space
complexity_delta.to: O(n) time, O(n) space
complexity_delta.note: Key improvement is removing the repeated O(n) sum computation inside an O(n) loop by using a closed-form mathematical formula and vectorized operations.

trigger_signals: A loop over all indices where the body calls sum() on the whole list every iteration.
Total operation count grows quadratically with n, but the conceptual task is aggregating over elements in a way that suggests a formula (pairwise products).
Redundant recomputation of a value (global sum) that does not change inside the loop.
Pattern resembling sum of pairwise products, which is a classic sign that ((sum a)^2 - sum(a^2)) / 2 can be applied.
transformation_steps: Recognize that the original loop computes sum over all ordered pairs (i,j) with i != j, then divides by 2, which is equivalent to sum_{i<j} a[i]*a[j].
Use the mathematical identity: sum_{i != j} a[i]*a[j] = (sum a)^2 - sum(a^2). Therefore, sum_{i<j} a[i]*a[j] = ((sum a)^2 - sum(a^2)) / 2.
Replace the O(n^2) loop and repeated sum(var_1) calls with a one-pass computation of sum(a) and sum(a^2).
Implement this using NumPy: convert the list to a NumPy array, compute np.sum(var_2) and np.sum(var_2**2), and apply the formula.
Retain integer arithmetic by using integer inputs and integer division (// 2) to match the original semantics.

bottlenecks.detail: Repeatedly calling sum(var_1) inside the loop makes the overall time O(n^2). The logical operation is only pair-wise aggregation, which can be done in O(n).
The main accumulation is done in a Python for-loop, which is slower than using a closed-form formula or vectorized operations when n is large.
The global sum of the array does not change during the loop, but it is recomputed every iteration instead of being cached.
Using eval(input()) to parse an integer is slower and unsafe compared to int(input()).

complexity_delta.from: O(n log n + R * n) time, O(n) space
complexity_delta.to: O(C * n) time with small constant C (≈100), O(n) space
complexity_delta.note: Removed the dependence on the numeric range R = max(a) - min(a) by using a fixed, constraint-driven bound on P and removed an unnecessary O(n log n) sort.

trigger_signals: Outer loop iterates over every integer between min and max of the data (value-domain brute force).
Inner loop is a full O(n) pass for each candidate P, producing an O(R * n) pattern where R is the numeric range.
Presence of an unnecessary sort whose result is only used for min/max rather than for order-dependent logic.
Problem statement provides a tight bound on possible P values (e.g., all a_i in [1,100]) that is not being exploited.
transformation_steps: Eliminate the sort: stop using sorted(a) since the objective sum (P - a_i)^2 does not depend on the order of a.
Replace scanning P from min(a) to max(a) with scanning P over the fixed, problem-given domain [1,100].
Leverage convexity of the cost in P to add an early-break condition: once the total cost ceases to decrease as P increases, terminate the outer loop.
Simplify the implementation: drop the extra indexing variables and use direct list iteration to reduce overhead (though asymptotics stay the same).

bottlenecks.detail: Scanning every integer P in [min(a), max(a)] makes the outer loop size depend on the numeric range R = max(a) - min(a), which can be much larger than n or a known small bound.
Sorting the array is not needed to compute sum_i (P - a_i)^2 for each P; it introduces an O(n log n) overhead on top of the O(R * n) scan.

The algorithm explores the full value domain from min to max instead of using the problem’s stated bound (e.g., [1,100]) or the fact that the objective is convex in P and can be minimized with far fewer candidates.

complexity_delta.from: O(N*M) (worst O(N^2) when M≈N), with multiple full scans and heuristic passes
complexity_delta.to: O(N log N), dominated by 8 sorts of N scores
complexity_delta.note: This change is the difference between likely TLE and comfortably passing when N is on the order of 1e5.

trigger_signals: Input size N can be large (≈1e5), making any O(N^2) strategy infeasible in Python.
The slow version performs an outer loop up to M times and an inner loop over all N points each time, a classic red flag for O(N*M) complexity.
The objective function is a sum of absolute values of linear sums, a known pattern that often admits an 8-sign enumeration trick.
Presence of per-iteration abs and arithmetic in Python loops rather than leveraging vectorized or batched operations and sorting.
transformation_steps: Recognize that maximizing |sum x|+|sum y|+|sum z| over a subset can be rewritten as maximizing a linear form under an optimal choice of signs: |X|+|Y|+|Z| = max_{sx,sy,sz∈{±1}} (sx*X + sy*Y + sz*Z).

Instead of greedily choosing points one-by-one, fix a sign pattern (sx,sy,sz) and compute a scalar score s = sx*x + sy*y + sz*z for each point; under that pattern the best M-point subset is simply the M points with largest scores.
Enumerate all 8 sign patterns, and for each: compute the N scores, sort them in descending order, and compute the sum of the largest M scores.
Take the maximum of these 8 candidate sums as the final answer, eliminating the need for any iterative greedy selection driven by current partial sums.
Implement this in a batch style with contiguous arrays and built-in sort, removing nested Python loops over both selections and items.

bottlenecks.detail: Greedy one-by-one selection of points with a full O(N) scan for each of M picks leads to O(N*M) time, which is too slow in Python for N,M up to around 1e5.

The heuristic based on current sum and marginal |Δ| gain does not exploit the structure that allows reduction to 8 fixed sign patterns; it effectively searches in a much larger decision space than needed.
Nested Python loops with multiple abs and arithmetic operations in the inner loop incur high interpreter overhead per operation, amplifying the O(N^2) cost.

complexity_delta.from: O(R^2 * n + n log n) time, O(n) space
complexity_delta.to: O(100 * n) time, O(n) space
complexity_delta.note: By removing the unused outer loop and replacing a data-dependent candidate range [min, max] × [min, max] with a fixed 1..100 range, the modified version avoids the R^2 factor. Under the assumption that the optimal p lies in this fixed range, the runtime becomes effectively linear in n with a small constant.

trigger_signals: Presence of a loop whose index (var_4) is never used inside the loop body, indicating redundant repeated computation.
Triple-nested loops with two loops effectively spanning the same numeric range of candidate p values, implying a potential R^2 blow-up in operations.
Sorting used only to obtain min and max values, suggesting unnecessary O(n log n) overhead on top of heavy nested loops.
Recomputing the same structure of sums from scratch many times without any change that would affect the result, a common pattern behind time limit exceed errors.
transformation_steps: Remove the outer for-loop over var_4, which did not influence the computation and only caused repeating the same search multiple times.
Drop the inner loop over var_2 spanning [min(a) + 1, max(a) - 1]; instead, use a single loop over a fixed set of candidate integer p values (here 1..100).
For each candidate p, compute the cost sum_i (a_i - p)^2 in one pass over the array and maintain the minimum cost seen so far.
Avoid sorting the input array; simply read the values into a list and operate directly on them.
Initialize the current best cost to a large sentinel (var_4 = 10000000) and update it if a lower cost is found.

bottlenecks.detail: The outer loop over var_4 in range(var_7[0], var_7[-1] + 1) does not use var_4 and repeats the entire inner search for the best p R times, multiplying work by a factor of R.
Loop variable var_4 is never referenced in the body. Its sole effect is to cause recomputation of identical sums, a classic signal of redundant looping.

The effective search over candidate p is done in the inner loop over var_2 from min+1 to max-1. Combined with the redundant outer range loop, this yields a quadratic dependence on the value range, R^2, instead of linear.
var_7.sort() is used only to access min and max; sorting incurs O(n log n) though min and max can be obtained in O(n) without sorting.
Using eval(input()) to read an integer is both slower and unsafe compared to int(input()), though this is a minor performance issue compared to the algorithmic redundancy.

complexity_delta.from: O(R^2 * N + N log N)
complexity_delta.to: O(R * N) (with R=100, effectively O(N))
complexity_delta.note: Removed a redundant outer loop and avoided repeated recomputation of identical sums, reducing one factor of R. Also dropped the sort and unsafe eval-based input.

trigger_signals: Presence of an outer loop whose loop variable is never used inside the body.
Triple-nested loops combining candidate-value iteration with full scans over the entire array.
Recomputation of the same sum or expression in each iteration without dependency on the loop variable.
Use of sorting where only min/max or bounded ranges are needed.
Use of `eval(input())` for reading simple integers, suggesting unoptimized I/O.
transformation_steps: Recognize that the optimization variable is a single integer p over a small bounded range; you only need to evaluate each candidate p once.
Remove the unused outer loop over `var_4`, which did not affect any computations and only multiplied runtime by R.
Eliminate the redundant per-iteration recomputation of the baseline sum for `var_7[0]` inside the outer loop; only compute sums that actually depend on the candidate p.
Drop the sorting step; min and max from the input are unnecessary when the constraints guarantee p is in [1, 100].
Simplify input handling: use `int(sys.stdin.readline())` (or `int(input())`) instead of `eval(input())` for faster and safer integer parsing.
Use a simple two-level structure: iterate over candidate p in a fixed range, and for each, accumulate the sum of squared distances over all elements.

bottlenecks.detail: Outer loop `for var_4 in range(var_7[0], var_7[len(var_7) - 1] + 1):` does not use `var_4` inside; it repeats exactly the same inner computation R times, multiplying runtime by R with no effect on the result.

Inner structure `for var_2 in range(...):` plus `for var_1 in range(len(var_7)):` recomputes the full sum of squared distances for every candidate `var_2`. Combined with the redundant outer loop, this yields O(R^2 * N) work.

The baseline sum `var_6 = var_6 + (var_7[0] - var_7[var_1]) ** 2` is recomputed from scratch on each outer iteration, but both the formula and the result are independent of the outer variable and repeated identically.
The array is sorted just to obtain min and max, costing O(N log N); min and max could be computed in O(N) without sorting. This is minor relative to O(R^2 * N) but still unnecessary.
Using `eval(input())` adds overhead and risk; a simple `int(input())` is faster and safer. This doesn’t change asymptotic complexity but impacts constant factors.

complexity_delta.to: O(R * N) with R fixed to 101 ⇒ O(N)
complexity_delta.note: Removed an entire redundant outer loop, restricted the candidate p range to a small fixed interval [0,100] per problem constraints, and delegated inner loops to optimized NumPy operations.

trigger_signals: Multiple nested Python for-loops (three levels) dominated by simple arithmetic on a list, indicating high interpreter overhead.
An outer loop variable that is never used inside its body, suggesting redundant repeated work.
Recomputation of a full sum-of-squares from scratch for each candidate p rather than leveraging the simple quadratic structure or a bounded search space.
Problem statement implying small bounded coordinates (e.g., xi in [0,100]), making a fixed-range scan or vectorization especially attractive.
Use of eval(input()) and other non-essential operations hinting at unrefined or experimental code rather than an optimized solution.
transformation_steps: Eliminate the redundant outer loop over var_4 whose index was unused, avoiding an extra multiplicative factor of R.
Directly iterate candidate p over the known small bounded range 0..100 instead of nested loops over the min..max of the input in two separate levels.
Replace explicit Python loops over the list for each p with a NumPy array var_6 holding the values and compute (var_6 - p) ** 2 in a vectorized way.
Use NumPy’s internal optimized C loops and sum to compute the total squared distance for each p, reducing Python interpreter overhead.
Track the minimum sum via a simple scalar comparison in the outer loop and cast the final result back to int for output.
Simplify input handling by reading all integers in one pass (open(0).read().split()), treating the first as N and the rest as the positions.

bottlenecks.detail: The outer loop over var_4 repeats exactly the same entire computation R times; var_4 is never used inside. This multiplies work by ~R with no effect on the result.

For each candidate p, the sum of squared differences is recomputed in pure Python with an explicit loop over all N elements, and this itself is nested in another loop over p values. Python-loop overhead dominates for even moderate N and R.

For every candidate p, the expression (p - var_7[var_1]) ** 2 is recomputed from scratch for all elements; no incremental update or prefix-sum style optimization is used despite the simple quadratic form.
Use of eval(input()) is unnecessary and unsafe. Repeated calls to len(var_7) and accessing var_7[0] and var_7[len(var_7)-1] inside loops add minor but avoidable overhead.

The inner sum uses (var_2 - var_7[var_1]) ** 2 as expected only in the second inner loop; the first inner loop always uses (var_7[0] - var_7[var_1]) ** 2, and the outer loop’s p (var_4) is never used. While this is a correctness bug, it also means work is not aligned with the intended search over p.

complexity_delta.from: O(N^2) time, O(N) space
complexity_delta.to: O(N log N) time, O(N) space
complexity_delta.note: Replacing a per-step greedy selection (rescan all N for each of M selections) with an 8-pattern global projection + sort eliminates the quadratic factor.

trigger_signals: Presence of nested loops where the outer loop runs up to M (number of items to select) and the inner loop scans all N elements, especially when problem constraints allow N≈1e5.
Use of a greedy incremental choice criterion for a problem with an L1-norm objective (sum of absolute values), which often has known global sign-pattern tricks.
Total operations estimate approaching or exceeding ~1e8–1e9 in Python (e.g., N*M with N≈M≈1e5 → 1e10), which is an immediate red flag.
Multiple full-array scans with substantial per-element work (several abs calls) occurring inside another loop over selection count.
Contest/problem discussions or editorials hinting at a bitmask + sort solution, suggesting that a greedy O(N^2) approach is not intended.
transformation_steps: Recognize that the objective |sum_x|+|sum_y|+|sum_z| can be rewritten as a maximum over 8 fixed sign combinations: for any chosen subset, its contribution for a fixed sign pattern is simply the sum of per-point signed projections.
Instead of iteratively picking the next best point via a greedy scan, precompute for each of the 8 sign combinations the projected value v_i = s_x*x_i + s_y*y_i + s_z*z_i for all points.

For each sign pattern, sort the list of v_i in descending order once and compute the prefix sum of the top M entries. This exploits a global ordering rather than recomputing local marginal gains each step.
Take the maximum result over the 8 patterns as the final answer, since one of these patterns attains |sum_x|+|sum_y|+|sum_z| for the optimal subset.
Remove repeated O(N) scans driven by greedy updates and abs() recalculations; replace them with a fixed number (8) of scans and sorts over the entire data.

bottlenecks.detail: Greedy selection loop: for _ in range(var_1) contains an inner loop over all N points. With N up to ~1e5 and M≈N, this becomes ~1e10 per-point evaluations, which is far beyond feasible limits for Python.

For each candidate point inside the inner loop, the code performs 6 abs() calls and several additions/subtractions to compute var_15. Even if asymptotically dominated by N^2, this heavy constant factor exacerbates runtime.

After the greedy building of var_16, the code performs additional full scans over all points (to compute var_11/var_10 arrays) and then appears to start another selection process (incomplete in snippet). These extra O(N) or worse passes add overhead without reducing overall complexity.

The greedy heuristic chooses the next point based on immediate gain in |sum_x|+|sum_y|+|sum_z|, but the correct optimal structure is to fix one of 8 (sign_x, sign_y, sign_z) patterns and then globally sort by projected value. The greedy per-step choice prevents benefiting from this global ordering and forces repeated rescans.

complexity_delta.from: O(R^2 * N) time, O(N) space
complexity_delta.to: O(R * N) time, O(N) space
complexity_delta.note: The key improvement is removing an unnecessary outer loop over R and relying on a single pass over the candidate range, coupled with vectorized arithmetic to reduce constant factors.

trigger_signals: Presence of a loop variable (var_4) that is never used in the loop body, suggesting redundant repeated computation.
Nested loops over both the value range and the array indices, whose total number of iterations multiplies to R^2 * N, while the problem conceptually only requires R * N work at worst.
Recomputing the same sum of squared differences for identical candidate p values multiple times within different loops.
Use of pure Python loops for numeric aggregation over arrays where vectorization (numpy) is available and natural.
transformation_steps: Remove the outermost loop over var_4 that redundantly wraps the inner search; instead, iterate once over the candidate integer p in the range from min(a_i) to max(a_i).
For each candidate p, compute the sum of squared deviations sum_i (a_i - p)^2 exactly once rather than recomputing the same costs in multiple outer iterations.

Replace explicit Python loops over indices with numpy vector operations: construct a numpy array of the inputs, then use (array - p) ** 2 followed by np.sum(...) to evaluate the cost in optimized C rather than Python.
Track the running minimum of the cost across candidates using a scalar variable (var_3) rather than recomputing or resetting intermediate minima inside nested loops.
Simplify the search bounds by using min(var_2) and max(var_2) directly; rely on range(max - min + 1) and offsetting by min to generate all candidate p values.

bottlenecks.detail: The outer loop for var_4 in range(var_7[0], var_7[len(var_7) - 1] + 1): does not use var_4 at all. All computations inside depend only on var_7 and var_2, not on var_4, so the same work is repeated R+1 times.

For each candidate p (var_2), the code recomputes var_6 from scratch via a loop over all array elements: for var_1 in range(len(var_7)): var_6 += (p - var_7[var_1]) ** 2. This is unavoidable in a naive brute-force, but here it is nested inside an unnecessary outer loop, effectively multiplying cost by another factor of R.

The first inner loop always evaluates the cost at p = var_7[0], and the second inner loop evaluates p from var_7[0] + 1 to var_7[-1] - 1. Because all of this is wrapped in an outer loop that does not change any of these values, the algorithm repeats the identical search for the minimal var_6 in each outer iteration.

All sums of squared differences are performed via explicit Python loops over the list, incurring Python-level overhead per element. With N up to ~100 this is not catastrophic, but combined with the redundant R factor it becomes unnecessarily slow compared to a vectorized approach.

complexity_delta.from: O(M * N) ≈ O(N^2) time, O(N) space (greedy scan for each selection plus extra greedy phase).
complexity_delta.to: O(N log N) time, O(N) space (8 sign-pattern passes with sorting and prefix sums).
complexity_delta.note: The optimized approach uses a mathematically equivalent linearization of the objective under all sign patterns, avoiding repeated full rescans over remaining points. This simultaneously improves performance and yields a clean, provably optimal solution pattern.

trigger_signals: Nested loops over the same data structure with sizes up to N and M, especially when M can be as large as N, yielding O(N^2) operations.
A per-iteration full scan over all remaining elements to choose a 'best' candidate, indicative of an unoptimized greedy approach.
Problem structure involving |sum of coordinates| or Manhattan distances; these often admit transformations via sign enumeration and linearization.
Contest or problem constraints suggesting N around 1e5 while using Python, where anything worse than O(N log N) tends to time out.
transformation_steps: Recognize that the score being maximized is of the form |sum_x| + |sum_y| + |sum_z| over a chosen subset of size M; this is the L1 norm (Manhattan distance) of the sum vector.

Observe that for any fixed choice of signs (sx, sy, sz) ∈ {+1, -1}^3, we can rewrite the objective as sx*sum_x + sy*sum_y + sz*sum_z, which is linear in the selected points. The absolute values can be handled by enumerating all possible sign combinations.

For each sign combination, transform each point (x_i, y_i, z_i) into a scalar score v_i = sx*x_i + sy*y_i + sz*z_i. Under this transform, the best subset of size M is simply the set of M points with the largest v_i (or smallest, depending on sign orientation), because the objective is the sum of v_i over chosen points.

Replace the greedy O(MN) selection with a sort-based selection: for each of the 8 sign-variant arrays, sort in O(N log N) and then take the top/bottom M elements to compute a candidate score. Track the maximum over the 8 candidates.
Remove the second heuristic greedy loop, which neither improves asymptotic performance nor guarantees correctness, and is redundant once the sign-enumeration + sorting strategy is in place.

bottlenecks.detail: Greedy incremental selection: in each of M iterations, the code scans all N points to recompute the best candidate based on the marginal gain var_15. This repeated full scan gives O(MN) time.

The second greedy strategy (starting from the point with largest absolute coordinate) again iterates up to M times with an implied scan over many candidates (truncated in the snippet but clearly following the same pattern), adding another O(MN) phase if implemented.
With N up to ~1e5, O(N^2) operations (≈1e10) are far too slow in Python under typical contest time limits (1–2 seconds).

The greedy heuristic maximizes local increments in |sum_x|+|sum_y|+|sum_z|, but this objective is non-linear; local choices are not guaranteed to lead to the global optimum. Besides being slow, this approach is likely wrong on adversarial inputs.

complexity_delta.from: O(N * R) with R = 100 for all inputs; outer loop always scans all 100 candidates.
complexity_delta.to: Worst-case O(N * R) but practical O(N * K) with K <= R, using early break once the objective stops improving.
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotic big-O remains the same, but the expected number of outer-loop iterations decreases, improving constant factors. Early termination leverages unimodality/convexity of the squared-error objective over the bounded integer range.

trigger_signals: Fixed, small candidate range (1..100) combined with a nested loop suggests opportunities to cut unnecessary iterations.
The objective is a sum of squared differences, a standard convex function in the parameter p; this hints that the sequence of costs over integer p is unimodal.
The original modified code recomputes the full objective for all candidates and then uses min, a pattern often replaced with early-stopping scans when unimodality is available.
transformation_steps: Recognize that the objective sum_i (a_i - p)^2 is convex in p; over integers in a bounded interval, the sequence of costs as p increases is unimodal (decreases until the minimum, then increases or stays flat).
Change the outer loop to iterate p in increasing order starting from the lower bound (1) and track the best value seen so far.

Replace the unconditional full scan of all 100 candidates with a conditional break: if the newly computed cost is not strictly better than the current best, break the loop, since any further p will not decrease the cost.
Optionally (as in Code A), avoid extra operations like abs() before squaring, using (a - p) ** 2 directly to save minor constant time per iteration.

bottlenecks.detail: The outer loop always runs through all 100 candidate values, even though the objective sum of squared differences is convex/unimodal in p and once past the minimum, values will only increase. This wastes iterations when the optimal p is far from 100.

Using abs(var_3 - var_5) ** 2 performs an absolute value before squaring; for real/integer arithmetic, (x - y) ** 2 is already non-negative, so abs() is redundant and adds minor overhead each inner-loop iteration.
Using eval(input()) to parse an integer is slower and unsafe compared to int(input()). Performance impact is negligible for small input, but it is unnecessary overhead.

complexity_delta.from: O(N * M) (worst case O(N^2)) time, O(N) space
complexity_delta.to: O(N log N) time, O(N) space
complexity_delta.note: Replacing per-pick greedy scanning with sign-enumeration plus sorting reduces the worst-case work from quadratic to near-linearithmic, which is tractable for N≈1e5.

trigger_signals: Input size N is up to around 1e5, making O(N^2) approaches infeasible within typical contest time limits.
The original code performs a nested loop over M (var_1) and N (var_2), clearly indicating O(NM) behavior.
Greedy incremental selection on a symmetric objective (Manhattan norm of a sum) is often suboptimal and hints that a structural simplification (like sign enumeration) may exist.
The improved solution pattern (8 sign combinations, sort, take top M) is a known standard for 3D Manhattan-distance maximization problems in competitive programming.
transformation_steps: Recognize that maximizing |sum x| + |sum y| + |sum z| over subsets can be reformulated using sign enumeration: |X|+|Y|+|Z| = max_{sx,sy,sz in {+1,-1}} (sx*X + sy*Y + sz*Z).
Switch from incremental greedy selection to a global scoring approach: for each fixed sign triple (sx,sy,sz), assign each point a scalar score = sx*x_i + sy*y_i + sz*z_i.
For each sign triple, sort the N scores once and take the sum of the top M scores; this directly yields the best subset under that sign configuration.
Take the maximum of these 8 candidate sums to recover the true maximum of |sum x| + |sum y| + |sum z|.
Exploit that M can be large (up to N), so avoiding per-pick scans (O(NM)) in favor of one sort per sign pattern (O(N log N)) is crucial.

bottlenecks.detail: Greedy selection with a full O(N) scan for each of M picks leads to O(NM) ≈ O(N^2) in the worst case, which is too slow for N up to ~1e5 (on the order of 1e10 operations).
The tight inner loop is pure Python with many abs() and list accesses per element, amplifying the cost of the already-quadratic algorithm.

The greedy step maximizes immediate gain in |Sx|+|Sy|+|Sz| but does not exploit the known structure of Manhattan distance (sign separability), preventing a reduction to a more efficient sorting-based solution.

complexity_delta.from: O(8 * (N log N + N + M)) time, O(N) space
complexity_delta.to: O(8 * (N log N + N + M)) time, O(N) space
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotic complexity is unchanged (still 8 sorts of N elements), but the fast code eliminates per-point coordinate storage and repeated summations, reducing constant factors and memory traffic.

trigger_signals: Use of large composite list elements (e.g., length-4 lists) when a single scalar key would suffice for sorting and selection.
Multiple passes with sum(...) and abs(...) over the same top-M slice for each iteration of an outer loop.
Inner loops that construct temporary lists (_abc, _xyz elements) for every point and every configuration.
Same O(N log N) structure as known accepted solutions but with significantly heavier operations per element, leading to timeouts near upper constraints.
transformation_steps: Recognize that for a fixed sign pattern (sx, sy, sz), maximizing |sum x|+|sum y|+|sum z| over a chosen subset is equivalent to maximizing sum(sx*xi + sy*yi + sz*zi) for that subset.
Instead of storing [score, sx*xi, sy*yi, sz*zi] for each point, store only the scalar score = sx*xi + sy*yi + sz*zi in a list var_4.
Sort var_4 in descending order and compute the objective for that sign pattern as sum(var_4[:M]), removing the need for three separate abs(sum(...)) calculations over coordinates.
Replace the bit-manipulation loop that constructs var_1 in the slow code with explicit nested loops over (1, -1) for each coordinate, which is clearer and removes a small overhead.
Avoid re-creating and handling larger temporary lists in inner loops; work with simple integers wherever possible to minimize Python-level overhead.

bottlenecks.detail: Instead of storing a single scalar key per point, each element of _xyz is a list of 4 integers: [sum(_abc), sx*xi, sy*yi, sz*zi]. Sorting and slicing this larger structure increases memory footprint and constant factors for comparisons and moves.

After sorting, the code computes _r by running three separate sum() calls over generators on _xyz[:var_4] (indices 1, 2, and 3). This means 3*M accesses and Python-level additions for every sign combination, even though a single scalar objective per point would suffice.

The inner loop over var_3 (0..N-1) creates _abc via a Python list comprehension and then wraps it again in a new list when appending to _xyz. That is a lot of small list allocations and Python arithmetic in tight loops.

For every sign pattern var_2, the sign vector var_1 is rebuilt bit-by-bit in a loop. While small (only 3 elements), it’s still repeated 8 times and can be simplified or inlined logically as nested loops as in the fast version.

complexity_delta.from: O(N * M)  (worst O(N^2) when M ≈ N)
complexity_delta.to: O(N log N)
complexity_delta.note: For the expected constraints (N up to ~1e5, M up to N), O(N^2) is infeasible in Python, while O(N log N) with a small constant factor (8 patterns) comfortably fits typical time limits. The faster method is also exact, not heuristic.

trigger_signals: N is as large as 1e5, and the slow code performs a nested loop over M and N, potentially closer to N^2 operations, leading to clear risk of TLE.
The inner loop’s workload (multiple abs evaluations and comparisons per (iteration, point)) is nontrivial, so there is little slack for quadratic algorithms.

The problem structure (maximizing a sum of absolute values of sums of coordinates) is a classic pattern solvable via enumeration of sign patterns and sorting, indicating that a greedy heuristic is unnecessary and suspicious.

The presence of additional heuristic phases (starting from the max-|coordinate| point) suggests algorithmic uncertainty rather than a known optimal method; this is a red flag for both correctness and performance.
transformation_steps: Recognize that the objective |sum x| + |sum y| + |sum z| can be rewritten as the maximum over 8 sign combinations of (sum (sx*x + sy*y + sz*z)), where sx, sy, sz ∈ {+1, -1}. This removes the need for greedy incremental selection.
For each of the 8 sign vectors (sx, sy, sz), precompute, for every point (x, y, z), the signed contributions: s = sx*x + sy*y + sz*z, and store [s, sx*x, sy*y, sz*z].
Sort the N-element list for each sign pattern in descending order by s. The top M entries correspond to the best subset under that sign pattern.
For each pattern, sum the x-, y-, and z-contributions of the top M points separately and take the sum of their absolute values: |sum_x| + |sum_y| + |sum_z|.
Return the maximum value over all 8 patterns. This yields the exact optimum and replaces the O(N*M) greedy process with a deterministic O(N log N) computation.
Drop the additional heuristic phase (starting from the point with largest |coordinate|) altogether, since the 8-pattern method already finds the global maximum.

bottlenecks.detail: Quadratic behavior O(N^2) (N*M with M up to N) is too slow for N ≈ 1e5: ~1e10 primitive operations, which will time out in Python.

Each item selection re-scans all remaining points, even though their relative desirability changes in a structured way. This wastes work compared to exploiting the underlying structure of |sum x|+|sum y|+|sum z|.

The greedy strategy choosing the point with best immediate marginal gain is a heuristic; for this objective there is a known exact solution based on enumerating 8 sign patterns and sorting once per pattern. The greedy both risks TLE and is not guaranteed to find the global optimum.
Heavy use of Python-level loops and abs computations in the inner loop amplifies the cost of the O(N*M) structure; there is no vectorization or precomputation to reduce per-iteration cost.

complexity_delta.from: O(100*n) = O(n) worst-case; always 100 passes over the array
complexity_delta.to: O(100*n) = O(n) worst-case; usually fewer than 100 passes due to early termination
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotic worst-case complexity is unchanged, but expected runtime is improved by avoiding unnecessary candidate evaluations after the minimum is passed.

trigger_signals: The objective function is a sum of squared deviations in p, which is known to be convex/unimodal in p.
The outer loop iterates over a contiguous, ordered set of candidate p values.
Observed pattern: sequence of objective values first decreases then increases as p moves across the domain.
Desire to reduce repeated full-array scans when later candidates cannot improve the best value.
transformation_steps: Maintain the current best sum var_4 as before.
After computing var_5 for each candidate (var_1 + 1), compare it to var_4.
If var_5 is smaller, update var_4 and continue searching.
If var_5 is not smaller (else branch), break out of the outer loop, assuming the objective will not decrease again for larger p due to convexity.

bottlenecks.detail: The outer loop always evaluates all 100 candidate p values even though the quadratic objective in p is convex/unimodal; once the minimum is passed, further iterations cannot improve the result.
Using eval(input()) for integer reading is unnecessary and potentially unsafe; int(input()) is sufficient and avoids overhead and security risk.

delta_type: remove_redundant_value-range nesting and fix candidate-range strategy

complexity_delta.from: O(n * R^2) time, O(n) space
complexity_delta.to: O(n * C) ≈ O(n) time (with C=100), O(n) space
complexity_delta.note: By eliminating an unnecessary outer loop over the value range and iterating directly over a single fixed candidate range [1,100], the algorithm moves from quadratic in the value range (R^2) to linear in n with a small constant factor.

trigger_signals: Presence of a loop variable (`var_4`) that is never used in the loop body, indicating likely redundant repetition.
Nested loops where both ranges depend on roughly the same span of values (min..max), inflating complexity to R^2 unnecessarily.
Recomputation of the same sum for the same candidate p in multiple loop iterations instead of caching or computing once.
Knowledge from the problem statement that ai ∈ [1,100], which allows a simple fixed-range brute force instead of an expansive dynamic search.
Complexity smell: three nested loops over n and value ranges, where the conceptual problem only requires trying each candidate p once.
transformation_steps: Recognize that the optimization target is `min_p sum_i (ai - p)^2`, and that p only needs to be considered over a bounded domain [1,100] given by the problem constraints, rather than dynamically scanning [min(ai), max(ai)] in nested loops.
Remove the redundant outer loop that iterated over `var_4` without using it, which caused a full recomputation of all candidate sums for every outer iteration.
Flatten the candidate search to a single loop over p: for p in allowed_range: compute `sum((ai - p)^2)` exactly once per p.
Replace index-based inner loops over `range(len(var_7))` with direct iteration over list values (`for var_3 in var_1:`), simplifying the code while preserving O(n) cost per candidate.
Use a running minimum (`var_4 = min(var_4, var_6)`) to track the best objective value instead of nested comparisons across repeated full scans.

bottlenecks.detail: The loop `for var_4 in range(var_7[0], var_7[len(var_7) - 1] + 1)` does not use var_4 at all, yet repeats the entire computation of sums for all candidate p on every iteration. This multiplies the work by a factor of R unnecessarily.

Within the outer loop, `var_6` for p = var_7[0] is recomputed from scratch in every outer iteration. Likewise, for each candidate p (var_2) the sum over all elements is recomputed, even though this same set of computations is repeated for every outer-iteration of var_4.

`var_7.sort()` is only used to derive min and max, but the algorithm then loops over the full min..max range redundantly. Sorting adds O(n log n) setup cost without enabling a more efficient search strategy.

There is a nested loop: outer over `var_4` in [min, max], and inner over `var_2` in [min+1, max-1]. Both ranges are essentially the same size R, causing the R^2 factor. Only a single loop over candidate p values is needed.

complexity_delta.from: O(R^2 * n + n log n)
complexity_delta.to: O(R * n)
complexity_delta.note: By eliminating a redundant outer loop that did not affect the calculations and by scanning the value range only once, the algorithm improves from quadratic to linear in the value range R (min-to-max).

trigger_signals: Presence of three nested loops where the innermost work depends only on the loop indices and array values, suggesting O(R^2 * n) behavior.
A loop variable (var_4) that is never used in its body, indicating redundant repetition of the same computation.
Recomputing `sum((constant - a_i)^2)` multiple times with the same constant instead of computing once.
Sorting the array even though the subsequent algorithm does not actually rely on order beyond min/max.
Comparing to the original/fast version reveals that the search over t only needs one pass over the value range, not two nested passes.
transformation_steps: Recognize that the outer loop over var_4 in the slow code does not use var_4 and thus simply repeats the exact same inner computations R times.
Remove the redundant outer loop, keeping a single loop over candidate t values; conceptually, keep only what the fast code does: one loop over t, one loop over the array for each t.
Avoid repeated recalculation of the same initial sum `sum((var_7[0] - var_7[i])**2)` on every outer iteration by either computing it once or directly folding it into the main candidate enumeration.
Drop unnecessary sorting because the brute-force method in the fast code needs only max(a) (and optionally min(a)); retrieving max is O(n) without sorting.
Resulting structure: single outer loop over t (value range) and single inner loop over n elements, tracking a running minimum of the sum of squared differences.

bottlenecks.detail: Loop `for var_4 in range(var_7[0], var_7[len(var_7) - 1] + 1):` does not use var_4 at all, causing every computation inside to be repeated R times unnecessarily.
For each candidate t (var_2), the code recomputes `sum((t - a_i)^2)` from scratch over all n elements, and this entire scan is itself repeated in the redundant outer loop, leading to O(R^2 * n) work.

The first inner loop in each outer-iteration computes `sum((var_7[0] - var_7[i])**2)` into var_6 and assigns it to var_5, but this same value is identical on every outer pass and could have been computed once—or avoided entirely by merging logic.
Sorting the array O(n log n) is not needed for a brute-force scan over t if you only care about min and max or max; contributes small overhead relative to the inflated O(R^2 * n) term.

complexity_delta.from: O(N * M) time, O(N) space
complexity_delta.to: O(N log N) time, O(N) space
complexity_delta.note: Replaced incremental greedy selection (scanning all N points M times) with a sort-based strategy over fixed linear functionals (sign patterns) and a single pass per pattern.

trigger_signals: Nested loop where the outer loop runs up to M (~N) times and the inner loop runs over all N points: clear O(N*M) structure.
Constraints inferred to be around N up to 1e5, where O(N^2) is too slow.
Per-iteration recomputation of improvement scores using abs() and multiple array accesses in pure Python.
Greedy heuristic structure (pick best next point each time) for a combinatorial optimization problem, often a red flag when constraints are large.
Known competitive-programming pattern: maximize sum of absolute values of coordinate sums by enumerating sign patterns and sorting once.
transformation_steps: Recognize that the objective |sum x| + |sum y| + |sum z| can be represented as maximizing a linear functional for some choice of signs (sx, sy, sz) in {+1, -1}, up to a global sign flip.
Observe that it suffices to evaluate 4 distinct sign combinations ((+,+,+), (+,+,-), (+,-,+), (+,-,-)), since the remaining 4 are just global negations and give the same absolute-value objective.
For each point (x,y,z) and each of the 4 sign patterns, precompute a scalar score s = sx*x + sy*y + sz*z and store it in an array corresponding to that pattern.
For each of the 4 arrays, sort the scores once in O(N log N).
For each sorted array, compute the sum of the best M scores by taking either the largest M or the smallest M (since abs(sum) is used) and track the maximum absolute sum over all patterns.
Eliminate the O(N)-per-selection greedy inner loop and all repeated rescans; the new approach uses only one sort per pattern and linear passes, turning the quadratic-ish behavior into O(N log N).

bottlenecks.detail: Core selection is a greedy incremental heuristic: for each of M iterations, it scans all N remaining points to find the best improvement, giving O(N*M) time. With N,M up to ~1e5, this is on the order of 1e10 operations and completely infeasible in Python.
The inner loop is Python-level with many abs() calls and list accesses per iteration; this exacerbates the already bad O(N*M) complexity.
The slow code attempts multiple heuristic passes (starting from points with largest |x|, |y|, |z|, etc.), which add more O(N*M) and O(N^2)-like work without changing the fundamental complexity class.

complexity_delta.from: O(R^2 * N) time, O(1) space
complexity_delta.to: O(R * N) time, O(N) space
complexity_delta.note: By removing an unnecessary outer loop that repeated the entire candidate-search computation, the modified code reduces one factor of R in time complexity, at the cost of a small, linear-in-N temporary list per iteration.

trigger_signals: Presence of a loop variable (`var_4`) that is never used inside the loop body, indicating redundant repetition.
Two nested loops over what is effectively the same value range [min(a), max(a)] combined with an inner loop over N, a pattern that typically indicates accidental O(R^2 * N) behavior.
Recomputation of the same sum of squared differences for the same candidate p multiple times.
Problem structure suggests a simple 1D scan over candidate p (min to max), so more than two nested loops (over candidates and elements) is a red flag.
Use of `sort` only to access min and max, suggesting that min/max could be computed directly without extra complexity (though not the primary bottleneck here).
transformation_steps: Removed the unused outer loop over `var_4` that caused the entire candidate-evaluation logic to run (R+1) times with no change in behavior.

Reframed the candidate search as a single loop: `for var_6 in range(min(var_7), max(var_7) + 1):`, iterating directly over all possible integer targets p between the minimum and maximum values in the array.
For each candidate p, computed squared differences against all elements once using a list comprehension, and summed them to get the total cost.
Maintained a running minimum of these costs (`var_1 = min(var_1, var_2)`), replacing the previous two-stage computation with a straightforward global minimum over candidates.
Simplified state: removed redundant variables (`var_3` as length, `var_4` as unused loop index) and clarified roles of variables (e.g., `var_5` used as N, `var_6` as candidate value).

bottlenecks.detail: Loop `for var_4 in range(var_7[0], var_7[len(var_7) - 1] + 1):` does not use `var_4` inside its body, so the entire cost of computing candidate sums is repeated (R+1) times with identical work and identical result.
The cost for the candidate p = var_7[0] is recomputed from scratch in every outer iteration, even though it is independent of `var_4`.
Effective pattern is: over value-range (outer), over value-range (inner), over N elements – a three-level nesting that scales as R^2 * N instead of the intended R * N.

In the first inner loop, `(var_7[0] - var_7[var_1]) ** 2` uses a constant target `var_7[0]`; it does not explore different candidates, yet it is placed inside the outer loop, compounding redundant work.
Using `eval(input())` to read an integer (`var_3`) is slower and unsafe compared to `int(input())`, though this is negligible compared to the algorithmic inefficiency.

complexity_delta.from: O(N^2) time, O(N) space
complexity_delta.to: O(N) time, O(N) space
complexity_delta.note: The key improvement is eliminating the repeated O(N) sum inside the loop by restructuring the computation into a single-pass prefix-sum-based accumulation.

trigger_signals: Presence of sum(var_1) or any O(N) aggregate function inside a loop that itself runs O(N) times.
Constraints suggesting N up to ~10^5 or 10^6, where O(N^2) is infeasible.
Pattern of computing pairwise interactions (i<j) using a double loop structure or equivalent repeated full-array operations.
Observing that a precomputed global value (like total sum) is recomputed in each iteration.
transformation_steps: Recognize the target quantity as the sum over all distinct pairs i<j of a[i]*a[j].
Observe that this can be computed incrementally: when processing a[i], all earlier elements contribute prefix_sum * a[i], where prefix_sum = sum of a[0..i-1].
Replace the loop with an accumulate-based prefix sum to maintain the running total of previous elements.
Compute the final answer as the sum over i>=1 of a[i] * prefix_sum[i-1] in one linear pass, removing the inner O(N) summation.
Optionally, avoid constructing intermediate lists (e.g., do not wrap the generator in list()) to reduce overhead, though this does not change asymptotic complexity.

bottlenecks.detail: sum(var_1) is recomputed in every loop iteration instead of using the already computed total (var_5). This turns a conceptually linear algorithm into quadratic time.
The formula for sum of pairwise products can be computed in one pass using prefix sums or the identity: (total^2 - sum(a_i^2)) / 2. Current code does not exploit this.

complexity_delta.from: O(8 * (N log N + N + M)) with high constant factors due to 4-int tuples, multiple list allocations per point, and 3 passes over top M.
complexity_delta.to: O(8 * (N log N + N + M)) with reduced constants by using scalar scores and a single pass over top M.
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotic complexity is unchanged (both are O(N log N)), but the optimized code substantially reduces Python-level work per element and per pattern, which is critical for N up to ~1e5 in Python.

trigger_signals: N is as large as ~1e5, and the slow version performs heavy Python-level work (multiple list creations and passes) inside nested loops over 8 sign patterns.
Observed or likely TLE or very tight runtime margins when using the more allocation-heavy approach in Python.
Profiling or code inspection shows that most runtime is spent in list construction, sorting complex objects, and repeated summations over the same slice.
Problem editorial or common solutions suggest the scalar-score trick for this well-known maximization pattern.
transformation_steps: Avoid constructing augmented 4-element records per point (`[sum(_abc)] + _abc`) and instead compute only a scalar score per point (`x*sx + y*sy + z*sz`).
Eliminate the intermediate `_abc` 3-element list; compute the scalar directly from the coordinates (e.g., `var_8 * var_1 + var_10 * var_2 + var_11 * var_3`).
Sort a flat list of scalar scores instead of a list of 4-element lists, reducing comparison and memory overhead.
Replace three separate summations (one for each coordinate) with a single summation over top M scores, which already encode the chosen sign pattern’s effect.
Use direct iteration over sign choices via nested loops (for var_1, var_2, var_3 in (1, -1)) instead of bit-decomposing integers, simplifying and slightly speeding up the sign pattern generation.
Track and update a single best score (`var_7`) instead of collecting all pattern scores into a list and applying `max` at the end.

bottlenecks.detail: Sorting N elements for each of 8 sign patterns is required, but the structure of each element is a 4-element list, increasing comparison and memory overhead.
For each point and each pattern, it allocates `_abc` (length-3 list) and `[sum(_abc)] + _abc` (length-4 list), causing many small Python object allocations and GC pressure (O(8 * 4N) list creations).

After sorting, it computes three separate sums over the same top M entries: `sum(var_2[1] ...)`, `sum(var_2[2] ...)`, `sum(var_2[3] ...)`, causing three passes over the top-M slice instead of a single pass.

The sort key is implicit: Python compares full 4-element lists, which is slower than sorting scalars. Only the first component truly matters for order, but the remaining components participate in comparisons when ties occur.

complexity_delta.from: O(N*K) time (worst-case O(N^2)) and O(N) space
complexity_delta.to: O(N log N) time and O(N) space
complexity_delta.note: The new solution replaces repeated greedy scans over all points with a global scoring approach using 8 sign patterns and sorting by those scores.

trigger_signals: Nested `for` loops where the inner loop scans all N points and the outer loop runs K times: classic O(N*K) structure, suspicious when K can be as large as N.
Heuristic greedy approach that picks the best next point by local marginal gain, rather than solving the combinatorial maximization globally.
Typical constraints (N up to ~1e5) imply that N^2 algorithms are too slow in Python, suggesting the need for an O(N log N) or better approach.
Objective function involves sums of coordinates wrapped in absolute values, a pattern known to admit transformations using sign combinations.
transformation_steps: Recognize that the objective for a chosen subset S, f(S) = |Σ_(i∈S) x_i| + |Σ_(i∈S) y_i| + |Σ_(i∈S) z_i|, can be expressed as max over (sx, sy, sz) ∈ {±1}^3 of Σ_(i∈S) (sx*x_i + sy*y_i + sz*z_i).
For each fixed sign triple (sx, sy, sz), compute for every point i a scalar score v_i = sx*x_i + sy*y_i + sz*z_i.
Observe that, for a fixed sign triple, the optimal subset of size K is simply the K points with the largest v_i values; sorting v_i and taking the top K maximizes the sum.
Iterate over all 8 sign triples, and for each one, sort the scores, sum the top K, and update the global maximum answer.

Eliminate the greedy per-step candidate search and any per-iteration recomputation of abs() on cumulative sums, thereby collapsing the quadratic nested loop into a small constant (8) times an N log N sort.

bottlenecks.detail: Greedy selection of the next point based on local marginal increase in |Σx|+|Σy|+|Σz| requires scanning all remaining points each step, giving O(N*K) ~ O(N^2) work.
Greedy marginal improvement does not exploit the structure that |Σx|+|Σy|+|Σz| can be rewritten as a maximum over 8 sign combinations and optimized via global scoring.
For N ≈ 1e5, an O(N^2) algorithm (≈1e10 operations) is far too slow in Python.
The per-iteration recomputation of abs() on the current sum for every candidate is relatively heavy inside the already-quadratic loop, though dwarfed by the algorithmic complexity.

complexity_delta.from: O(R^2 * n) time, O(1) space
complexity_delta.to: O(R * n) time, O(1) space
complexity_delta.note: Eliminating the unused outer loop over var_4 and doing a single scan over all candidate positions brings the algorithm from quadratic in the candidate-range size to linear in that size.

trigger_signals: Outer loop variable (var_4) is unused in its body, indicating redundant repeated work.
Two nested loops ranging over the same interval [min_x, max_x] where only one is needed to explore all candidates.
Total running time dominated by repeated identical computations of the same cost values.
Presence of triple nested loops in a brute-force numeric problem suggests revisiting whether all levels are necessary.
transformation_steps: Identify that the outer loop variable var_4 is never used inside the loop body; recognize that the inner var_2 loop already enumerates all candidate positions once.
Remove the outer loop over var_4 entirely, and keep a single variable (e.g., var_3 in the fast code) that tracks the minimum total cost across all candidates.
Simplify the computation of squared distances by iterating directly over the array values in the inner loop, rather than indexing via range(len(...)).
Initialize the current best answer to a large sentinel (float('inf')) and update it using a simple min() call after computing each candidate’s cost.
Add a special-case early return for n == 1, where the optimal cost is trivially 0, avoiding unnecessary loops.

bottlenecks.detail: The outer loop over var_4 repeats the full inner scan over var_2 for every var_4, even though var_4 is never used inside. This re-computes the same minimum R times, multiplying total work by another factor of R.
The value var_6 for the first candidate (var_7[0]) is recomputed from scratch on every iteration of the outer loop, despite being identical across all outer iterations.
The innermost loops use range(len(var_7)) with index access instead of iterating directly over array elements, adding minor overhead on top of the much larger asymptotic inefficiency.
The first read integer (var_3) is never used. While not a performance hotspot, it indicates possible logic confusion in the original design.

complexity_delta.to: O(K * n) with K=100 (effectively O(n))
complexity_delta.note: Removed a redundant outer loop and switched from scalar triple loops over values and indices to a single vectorized broadcast over a small fixed candidate range.

trigger_signals: Presence of multiple nested loops where only one loop over the data is fundamentally necessary.
Outer loop whose induction variable (var_4) does not influence the state or calculation inside, indicating redundant repeated work.
Recomputing an aggregate (sum of squared differences) from scratch for each candidate p rather than using vectorized or aggregated operations.
Use of Python-level loops over all elements for simple arithmetic expressions that map naturally to NumPy vectorization.
transformation_steps: Eliminate the redundant outer loop over var_4 that repeated the entire candidate search without affecting the result.
Remove the inner nested loops over candidate p and array indices; instead, express the cost function as a vectorized operation using NumPy arrays.
Load all input values into a NumPy array to enable broadcasting and fast elementwise arithmetic.
Generate the candidate p values using `np.arange(1, 101)` (based on known value bounds) and reshape for broadcasting over the 1D data array.
Compute squared differences, sum across elements for each candidate p, and then take the minimum in a single pass using NumPy reductions.
Use buffered, low-overhead input (`sys.stdin.readline`) instead of `input()` and avoid `eval` for reading integers.

bottlenecks.detail: The outer loop `for var_4 in range(var_7[0], var_7[len(var_7) - 1] + 1):` does not affect any computation inside its body; the same cost search is repeated V times, multiplying run time by V unnecessarily.
For each candidate p (var_2) the code recomputes `sum((p - ai)^2)` from scratch in O(n), instead of reusing partial results or computing more directly from statistics (e.g., mean).
All operations over the array elements are performed in pure Python loops, incurring high overhead per element for sum of squared differences.
Using `eval(input())` to read an integer is both slower and unsafe compared to `int(input())`, although this is a minor issue compared to the algorithmic redundancy.

complexity_delta.from: O(N^2) time, O(N) space
complexity_delta.to: O(N) time, O(1) extra space (O(N) including input storage/stream)
complexity_delta.note: The key change is replacing repeated suffix max recomputation with a single forward pass that tracks a running minimum.

trigger_signals: Nested behavior: presence of max(list_slice) inside a loop over indices of the same list indicates potential O(N^2).
Slicing in loops: expressions like var_2[var_4+1:] in performance-critical sections are red flags for quadratic time.
Constraints: typical problem variants specify N up to 10^5–10^6, incompatible with quadratic algorithms.
Problem pattern: maximizing price[j] - price[i] with j > i is a known pattern solvable via prefix minima in O(N).
transformation_steps: Recognize that the goal is to maximize price[j] - price[i] with j > i, which can be expressed as, for each j, price[j] - min(price[0..j-1]).
Eliminate storage and tracking of dynamic right-side maxima and their indices; instead, track only the minimum price seen so far on the left.
Implement a single loop over prices: update the best profit as max(best_profit, current_price - min_so_far) and update min_so_far as min(min_so_far, current_price).
Stream the input prices rather than constructing and repeatedly slicing large lists, reducing both algorithmic complexity and memory traffic.
Simplify control flow: remove conditional recomputation branches tied to the max index, resulting in a straight-line one-pass computation.

bottlenecks.detail: Recomputing the maximum of a suffix using max(var_2[var_4+1:]) each time the current index reaches var_5. This produces quadratic behavior in worst-case patterns (e.g., monotone decreasing prices).
Creating new list slices var_2[var_4+1:] inside the loop copies O(N) elements repeatedly, increasing both runtime and transient memory traffic.
Using list.index on large lists inside the loop adds an additional O(N) scan per update of the sell index.

complexity_delta.from: O(R^2 * n) time, O(n) space
complexity_delta.to: O(K * n) time with K = 100 (≈ O(n)), O(n) space
complexity_delta.note: By removing redundant nesting over the candidate range and instead iterating linearly over a small fixed set of candidates p, the runtime drops by a factor of roughly R^2 / K. For R up to 100 this is about 100× faster; for larger R it would be far more dramatic.

trigger_signals: Presence of three nested loops with two loops ranging over similar integer intervals tied to the data range, indicating O(R^2 * n) behavior.
An outer loop whose induction variable (var_4) is never used in downstream calculations, suggesting redundant repeated work.
Recomputation of the same sum of squared differences (using var_7[0] as candidate) in every outer-loop iteration.
Problem structure: objective is sum of squared distances in one dimension, which is known to be convex in p, so exhaustive double scanning of p is suspiciously heavy.
Knowledge of typical constraints for such problems (values bounded by 100) makes a fixed-range linear scan obviously sufficient.
transformation_steps: Removed the redundant outer loop over var_4, which was not used in the computation and only caused repeated full searches.
Replaced nested scanning of the candidate domain (outer loop over var_4 and inner loop over var_2) with a single loop over candidate p in a fixed small range (1..100) dictated by problem constraints.
In each iteration of the outer loop, compute the sum of squared differences in a single pass over the array, instead of recomputing it many times for different but overlapping candidate sets.
Maintain a running minimum of the cost and stop the search once the cost stops decreasing, leveraging the convex/unimodal shape of sum_i (a_i - p)^2 in p.
Simplified data handling: avoid repeated len(var_7) calls inside loops by using var_3 as n and iterating over indices directly.

bottlenecks.detail: The code explores the candidate integer p range twice (nested over var_4 and var_2), causing O(R^2 * n) work rather than the O(R * n) that a straightforward brute-force search would need.
For each var_4 iteration, the initial loop computes sum((var_7[0] - var_7[i])^2) for all i. This result is independent of var_4 and is recomputed R times.
The outer loop over var_4 does not affect the computation at all (var_4 is never used); it simply repeats the full inner search, multiplying runtime by R.
Inside the first inner loop, the candidate p is fixed at var_7[0] instead of the loop variable; this prevents reuse/structure and leads to pointless recomputation.
The sum of squared differences is convex in p; the code does not exploit this unimodality or known small bounds on p, and instead scans the value range extremely inefficiently.

complexity_delta.from: O(n^2) time, O(n) space
complexity_delta.to: O(n) time, O(1) extra space
complexity_delta.note: The modified fast_code replaces repeated suffix rescans and slicing with a single linear pass that tracks a running minimum and maximum difference.

trigger_signals: Use of max() or min() over a slice or subrange inside a loop ('max(var_2[var_4+1:])'), especially when the slice size grows or shrinks with the loop index.
Creation of list slices (a[b:c]) in a loop body, which implies O(k) copying per iteration.
Calling list.index() repeatedly over overlapping regions of the list.
Nested or repeated linear scans over the same data structure, even if not written as explicit nested loops.
Reliance on eval(input()) per element for numeric input, which is a red flag for both performance and safety.
transformation_steps: Stop storing all suffixes and recomputing max(var_2[i+1:]) inside a loop; instead, process each value once in order.
Maintain a running minimum of all values seen so far (var_3 in fast_code) to represent the best buy price up to the current index.
On each new value, compute the candidate difference (current - running_min) and update the global maximum difference if it is better.
Remove all list slicing and index() calls in the hot loop; only simple arithmetic and comparisons remain.
Switch input handling to a streaming style (map(int, sys.stdin.readlines())) so that the algorithm can operate in a single pass over the data.

bottlenecks.detail: Recomputing suffix maxima via max(var_2[var_4+1:]) in the loop leads to repeated O(k) scans for varying k, which sum to O(n^2) in the worst case.
List slicing var_2[var_4+1:] allocates new lists on each recomputation of the suffix, increasing memory traffic and GC overhead.
Using eval(input()) for each integer is significantly slower than int(input()) due to parsing arbitrary Python expressions and compilation overhead.
Reading N lines one-by-one with input() has higher overhead compared to batched reading (e.g., sys.stdin) for large N, although the main issue is still the quadratic algorithmic pattern.

complexity_delta.from: O(n * R) with R = max(a)-min(a)+1 (potentially very large)
complexity_delta.to: O(n * C) with fixed C ≤ 100 (effectively O(n) under given constraints)
complexity_delta.note: If a_i are guaranteed in [1,100], then R ≤ 100 and both approaches are same-order; the modified code makes this bound explicit, removes sorting, and adds early stopping to cut constant factors. For unconstrained a_i, the new code changes the mathematical problem (and may be incorrect) but is asymptotically faster.

trigger_signals: Presence of a nested loop where the outer loop bounds depend on raw numeric values of the input (min and max) instead of explicit problem constraints.
Potentially huge iteration count if input values can be large or widely spaced, despite small n.
Knowledge that the function f(p) = Σ (a_i - p)^2 is convex in p, implying that scanning beyond the vicinity of the minimum is unnecessary once costs start increasing.
Awareness from problem statement/constraints that all a_i lie in a small, fixed interval such as [1,100].
transformation_steps: Replaced dynamic search interval [min(a), max(a)] with a fixed constraint-driven interval p ∈ [1,100], removing dependence on input numeric spread.
Removed sorting of the array; the minimum and maximum are no longer needed because the candidate p-range is fixed.
Introduced early break based on the convexity/unimodality of the sum of squared distances: once cost(p) stops decreasing as p increases, later p will not yield a smaller value.
Kept the same core brute-force structure (enumerating candidate p and summing squared distances), but with a much smaller and constraint-aligned candidate set.

bottlenecks.detail: The outer loop iterates over every integer between min(a) and max(a). If the value range (max(a)-min(a)) is large (e.g., up to 1e9), R becomes huge and the O(nR) loop is infeasible even for modest n.

The algorithm ignores problem-value constraints (likely a_i ∈ [1,100]) and uses the raw numeric span of the input, which may be much larger than the theoretical optimum search range required by the task.
An O(n log n) sort is performed only to find min and max; this could instead be a single O(n) pass. For small constraints this is minor, but it is extra overhead compared to necessary minimum.
Use of eval(input()) to parse an integer is unnecessary and slower/unsafe compared to int(input()). This does not dominate complexity but is a poor practice.

complexity_delta.from: O(var_1 * var_2) ≈ O(N^2) when var_1 ≈ var_2
complexity_delta.to: O(8 * N log N) ≈ O(N log N)
complexity_delta.note: The original greedy heuristic scans all remaining points for each of the M choices; the revised solution converts the objective into 8 linear objectives and solves each via a single sort plus prefix sum.

trigger_signals: Problem constraints likely allow N up to 10^5, which is typical in problems of this form.
The slow_code performs a nested loop: outer loop up to var_1, inner loop over all var_2 points, implying O(N^2) operations when var_1 is close to var_2.
Python generally cannot handle ≈10^10 elementary operations within time limits (usually around 2 seconds).
The pattern |sum_x| + |sum_y| + |sum_z| with 3 coordinates is a known template where enumerating 2^3 sign combinations is feasible and leads to an O(8 * N log N) solution.
Presence of per-iteration recomputation of abs-based marginal contributions is a red flag compared to a single transform-and-sort pass.
transformation_steps: Recognize that the objective |sum_x| + |sum_y| + |sum_z| can be rewritten as max over 8 sign patterns (σx, σy, σz) ∈ {−1, +1}^3 of (σx * sum_x + σy * sum_y + σz * sum_z).

Note that for any fixed sign pattern (σx, σy, σz), maximizing σx * sum_x + σy * sum_y + σz * sum_z over a chosen subset of size M reduces to: compute score_i = σx * xi + σy * yi + σz * zi for each point i, then pick the M points with largest score_i.

Implement this by constructing 8 arrays var_3[0..7], where each var_3[k] stores the scalar score of every point under one fixed sign combination. This is a linear pass over the points for each of the 8 patterns.
For each of the 8 arrays, sort scores in descending order and compute the sum of the first var_1 elements (a prefix sum of the top M scores). Keep track of the maximum sum over all 8 patterns.

Discard the earlier greedy scanning logic (which tried to repeatedly select the best point by marginal |sum| gain) and replace it with this sort-based approach, thereby reducing from quadratic to near-linearithmic time in N.

bottlenecks.detail: Greedy selection loop: for each of the var_1 choices, the code recomputes the marginal gain var_15 for every still-available point by scanning all var_2 points. This results in Θ(var_1 * var_2) operations, which becomes Θ(N^2) when var_1 (M) approaches var_2 (N). For N ≈ 10^5, this is ~10^10 iterations and will time out in Python.
The inner marginal gain calculation uses several abs calls and list indexing operations. While each is O(1), the constant cost is applied in a tight O(N^2) loop, amplifying the time cost.

The algorithm is effectively a greedy heuristic for a combinatorial optimization problem. It does not exploit the key structure that |Sx| + |Sy| + |Sz| can be written as a maximum over 8 linear forms; hence it cannot be reduced to a small number of sort-and-sum operations and instead does repeated global scans.

top_delta_type: Constant-factor optimization via faster I/O and streamlined implementation (same asymptotic complexity).
at the cost of slightly worse asymptotic complexity in a generalized model.
constant-factor optimization on I/O and sorting
constant-factor optimization via shifting work to built-in C-level operations and simplifying control flow

Attempt to simplify the control flow by using early exit instead of carrying a boolean flag.
Attempt to write a shorter, more direct script-style solution without utility I/O functions.
Awareness that /online-judge Python solutions for scheduling/sorting problems often require these micro-optimizations to pass.
Code reviews showing unnecessary use of flags and lack of early termination in critical loops.
Codebase containing many unused generic helper functions and configuration calls in an otherwise simple problem.
Codebase contains many unused helpers and imports, indicating template bloat rather than problem-specific logic.
