Dead code and unused helpers (func_1, func_2, func_4, func_5, func_6, func_8) hint that the template isn't tailored to the specific problem, leaving room for cleanup and optimization.
Desire for shorter or more idiomatic-looking code (for-else instead of a flag, fewer helper functions).
Desire to avoid an explicit key=lambda var_8: var_8[1] in sort, possibly under the misconception that this is slower than sorting by default order of transformed pairs.
top_steps: Accumulate second-tier candidates from each group into a single list and use a final sorted() call to select the global top-2 rather than maintaining a global min/second-min via a stateful helper.

Add early exit on infeasibility: In the fast version, break immediately once cumulative time exceeds the current deadline, using a for-else construct to decide the final answer. This can save up to O(n) work in failing cases.
Adjusted the accumulation logic to match the new tuple layout and sign inversion.

Adjusted the sort key from sorting by deadline only (key=lambda var_8: var_8[1]) to sorting by (deadline, duration) (key=lambda var_7: (var_7[1], var_7[0])). This adds a deterministic tie-breaker but keeps the same optimal greedy structure and asymptotic cost.
Adopt an explicit infinite loop with a sentinel check (break when '0 0') for clearer control flow and slightly less overhead.
Apply sorted() once per group to rank all 8 records at C speed, and obtain the top-2 and next-2 using simple slicing instead of nested conditionals and helper calls.
Avoid a final conditional check on a flag; instead, rely on early program termination for the negative case and a single 'Yes' print after successfully processing all jobs.
Avoid constructing a list and then converting to tuple: in the fast version, tuple(map(int, ...)) directly converts the iterator, removing one allocation per line.
Avoid defining unused helper utilities and recursion limit adjustments that do not contribute to the solution, slightly reducing module initialization overhead.
Avoid extra temporary variables for swapping and avoid re-reading/parsing before and inside the loop separately.
Avoid reassigning function names (func_2, func_4) to local variables to prevent confusion and potential bugs.
Avoid reassigning function names to data (e.g., func_2 being reused as a list), making the runtime environment simpler and reducing potential name resolution overhead.
Avoid reassigning function names to lists or loop indices (e.g., func_2, func_4), improving clarity and avoiding potential accidental misuse.

Avoid redundant copies: Read each pair with tuple(map(int, var_5().rstrip().split())) instead of list(func_3()) where func_3 already builds a list, eliminating an extra list construction per line and favoring tuples for immutable records.

Avoid redundant list-to-tuple copying: in fast_code, parse each line directly into a list of ints using `list(map(int, var_5().rstrip().split()))` and keep it as a list; there is no extra tuple creation.
Avoid shadowing function names with variables (`func_2` as both function and list, `func_4` as both function and loop index); instead use distinct, descriptive variable names.

The loop continues scanning all tasks even after detecting var_5 > var_4 (infeasible schedule). It only flips var_3 to False and finishes the loop, which wastes up to O(n) additional operations in failing cases where early termination is possible.

No actual algorithmic performance issue: the original was already optimal in big-O terms.
transformation_steps: Replaced sys.stdin.readline()-based helpers with direct input() calls.
Replaced func_1() with int(eval(input())) for reading n.
Replaced func_3()-style input parsing with inline list(map(int, input().split())).
Replaced explicit for-loop accumulation of squared deviations with a list comprehension inside sum().
Removed all unused helper functions and module-level boilerplate, inlining logic at the top level.

bottlenecks.detail: I/O cost is already optimized with sys.stdin.readline; no real bottleneck for typical contest constraints.

Multiple small wrapper functions (func_1, func_3, etc.) and lambdas add a tiny amount of call overhead, but this does not change asymptotic behavior and is negligible relative to O(n) array processing.
Storing the entire list var_2 requires O(n) memory. This is standard for this kind of problem but may be a consideration if n is very large (e.g., >10^7).

delta_type: constant-factor optimization on I/O and sorting

complexity_delta.from: O(N log N) time, O(N) space with additional Python-level overhead in sort key and I/O wrappers
complexity_delta.to: O(N log N) time, O(N) space with reduced constant factors via direct readline use and key-less sorting
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotic complexity is identical; performance gains come from removing a lambda key in sort and avoiding per-line wrapper calls around readline.

trigger_signals: Presence of sort with a simple key function (lambda on a small index) on potentially large lists: var_1.sort(key=lambda var_8: var_8[1]).
Multiple thin wrapper functions around sys.stdin.readline for basic parsing (integers, floats, strings) suggest avoidable per-line overhead for a performance-sensitive problem.
Typical contest context where N can be up to 1e5–2e5 and sorting dominates runtime; reducing per-element overhead in sorting and I/O can be significant.
Dead code and unused helpers (func_1, func_2, func_4, func_5, func_6, func_8) hint that the template isn't tailored to the specific problem, leaving room for cleanup and optimization.
transformation_steps: Replaced multiple small input helper functions with a direct alias: var_5 = sys.stdin.readline, reducing function-call overhead per input line.

Changed reading of N from func_7() to a single call: var_6 = int(eval(var_5())), then used var_6 in a list comprehension to read all jobs. (Note: eval is functionally unnecessary here and potentially unsafe; int(var_5()) would suffice.)
Constructed each job as list(map(int, var_5().split()))[::-1], effectively storing (deadline, duration) so that Python's default tuple/list comparison sorts by deadline without an explicit key.
Used sorted(...) directly on the jobs list instead of list.sort with a key lambda, eliminating the overhead of calling a Python function for key extraction on each element.
Simplified the feasibility check loop to early-print 'No' and break on violation, and used a for-else construct to print 'Yes' only if the loop completes without breaking.

bottlenecks.detail: var_1.sort(key=lambda var_8: var_8[1]) invokes a Python lambda key for each comparison element, adding per-element overhead on large N.
Multiple thin wrappers (func_3, func_7, etc.) around sys.stdin.readline introduce additional Python function calls for every input line.
Several helper functions (func_1, func_2, func_4, func_5, func_6, func_8) and sys.setrecursionlimit are unused in this solution but clutter the namespace and slightly increase import-time work.

complexity_delta.from: O(n log n) time, O(n) space
complexity_delta.to: O(n log n) time, O(n) space
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotic complexity is unchanged; the improvement is in constant factors, especially I/O and per-iteration overhead.

trigger_signals: Uses input() inside a loop that runs up to n times, which is a known bottleneck for large n in Python.
Multiple layers of small helper functions wrapping input() and parsing, which add function-call overhead with no algorithmic benefit.
Large number of unused imports and utilities suggesting template code carried over from other problems, often a source of unnecessary overhead.
Same high-level algorithm and complexity as typical editorial solutions, implying that any TLE is due to constant factors (I/O, call overhead) rather than asymptotic inefficiency.
transformation_steps: Replaced high-overhead input() calls with sys.stdin.readline, reducing latency per line of input.
Eliminated multiple wrapper functions (func_1, func_4) around input/parsing, cutting function-call overhead in the tight input loop.
Built the list of tasks using a list comprehension over sys.stdin.readline rather than append in a Python for loop, which is generally faster in CP scenarios.
Removed unused libraries and helper utilities (math, itertools, bisect, copy, deque, Counter, Decimal, lcm helper, etc.), reducing import/startup overhead and code noise.
Simplified the scheduling loop to iterate directly over the list of tasks (for var_2, var_3 in var_1) instead of indexing through an intermediate array, slightly reducing Python-level overhead.

bottlenecks.detail: Uses input() in a loop (func_4 and func_1), which is significantly slower than sys.stdin.readline for large n.
Wraps input reading in several layers of function calls (func_4 -> eval(input()) -> int(...), func_1 -> list(map(int, input().split()))), adding call overhead on every line.
Uses eval(input()) just to parse integers. eval is slower than direct int() parsing and is unsafe; it also prevents certain optimizations by the interpreter.
Imports math, itertools, bisect, copy, deque, Counter, Decimal and defines multiple unused helper functions. This increases startup time and code size without benefit for this problem.
Reassigns names like func_2 from function to list, reducing clarity and making it harder to see the tight loop’s true operations when profiling.

delta_type: micro-optimization / control-flow improvement with unchanged asymptotic complexity

complexity_delta.from: O(N log N) time, O(N) space; always scans all N jobs in the final pass even after failure.
complexity_delta.to: O(N log N) time, O(N) space; linear pass short-circuits on first failure, improving best/average-case constants while keeping worst-case the same.
complexity_delta.note: The core algorithm (sort by deadline + cumulative sum check) is identical. The key practical improvement is early termination on infeasibility.

trigger_signals: Observed TLE or tight runtime margins in Python when N is large (e.g., 1e5–2e5) and many test cases are infeasible early but the implementation still iterates over all jobs.
Profiling indicates that a significant portion of time is spent in the main feasibility-check loop even after infeasibility has already been established.
Code reviews showing unnecessary use of flags and lack of early termination in critical loops.
Online judge or benchmarking environment with strict time limits where constant-factor improvements in hot loops materially affect pass/fail outcomes.
transformation_steps: Preserve the main algorithm: read all (duration, deadline) pairs, sort by deadline, accumulate durations and compare with deadlines.
Replace the boolean-flag pattern with an immediate exit on first failure. In the fast code, upon detecting var_2 > var_5[var_4][1], it prints 'No' and calls exit(), stopping further iterations.
Avoid a final conditional check on a flag; instead, rely on early program termination for the negative case and a single 'Yes' print after successfully processing all jobs.
Use direct tuple unpacking in the loop (for var_2, var_4 in var_1) rather than indexing inside the loop, reducing per-iteration overhead slightly.
Simplify the surrounding code by not carrying unused utility functions for various input types, focusing only on what is required for this problem.

bottlenecks.detail: No early termination in the main loop: even after detecting var_5 > var_4 (schedule impossible), the loop continues over all remaining jobs, only flipping a flag. This wastes work when the schedule becomes impossible early.
Uses a boolean flag (var_3) plus a final conditional print instead of breaking immediately or exiting. Each iteration after failure does an unnecessary comparison and addition.

Uses sys.stdin.readline with a thin wrapper (func_3). Per-line function call adds small overhead, but raw readline is slightly faster than built-in input(). Overall IO cost is not dominant compared to sorting, but the extra wrapper is unnecessary.

Defines multiple helper functions (func_1, func_2, func_4, func_5, func_6, func_8) that are never used. This does not significantly affect runtime in competitive settings but introduces noise and potential confusion without benefit.

complexity_delta.from: O(N log N) time, O(N) space
complexity_delta.to: O(N log N) time, O(N) space
complexity_delta.note: Algorithm and asymptotic complexity are unchanged. However, the modified code likely performs slightly worse in practice due to slower input and unsafe eval usage.

trigger_signals: Desire to simplify code by removing multiple small input helper functions.
Attempt to exit early on failure by calling exit() directly instead of tracking a flag and printing at the end.
Perception that using sorted(...) or different container types (lists vs tuples) might affect speed, despite having the same asymptotic behavior.
transformation_steps: Replaced sys.stdin.readline-based input wrappers with built-in input() and eval(input()) for the first integer.
Changed storage from list of tuples to list of lists; kept sorting by second element using sorted(...) instead of list.sort(), which is functionally equivalent.
Removed boolean flag and break/print logic in favor of printing 'No' and calling exit() on first infeasible task.
Simplified loop structure to index-based access instead of tuple unpacking in the for-loop.

bottlenecks.detail: Sorting the list of tasks by deadline dominates runtime at O(N log N). This is expected and asymptotically optimal for this greedy strategy.
Multiple small input-wrapper functions (func_1..func_8) add minor call overhead, but are negligible relative to sort for typical constraints.

complexity_delta.from: O(N) time, O(1) space, but with high Python-level overhead per element due to manual selection and float parsing
complexity_delta.to: O(N log N) time, O(N) space, delegating most work to C-level sort and operating on raw strings
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotically the modified code is worse (N log N vs N), but with N=24 the constant factors dominate: using sorted() in C and minimizing Python-level branching/parsing makes the 'fast' version significantly quicker and far simpler.

trigger_signals: Very small fixed input size (3×8) where algorithmic optimality is less important than minimizing Python overhead.
Manual maintenance of multiple minima (current heat top 2 and global top 2 of non-qualifiers) via complex branching logic.
Repeated use of map(float, input().split()) despite only needing relative ordering by the second field, not full float arithmetic.
Readability and verification difficulty: hard-to-follow stateful code that is error-prone and resistant to micro-optimizations.
transformation_steps: Batch input: Read each heat into a list via a list comprehension ([[input() for _ in [0]*8] for _ in [0]*3]) instead of processing line-by-line inside nested loops.
Leverage built-in sort: Replace manual multi-variable min/second-min tracking logic with sorted(var_2, key=lambda s: s.split()[1]) to order competitors by their time field.

Unify per-heat and global selection: For each heat, output the first two sorted entries and append entries 2 and 3 (indices 2:4) to a global accumulator list; then treat this accumulator itself as another 'heat' by iterating over it in the same loop pattern.

Avoid unnecessary numeric conversion: Operate directly on the input strings and compare the second token as a string, allowing sort to run without converting to float on every line (assuming fixed formatting makes lexicographic ordering equivalent to numeric).
Simplify output: Use '\n'.join(var_1[:2]) for printing each pair of winners instead of multiple formatted print calls and integer casting.

bottlenecks.detail: Input is read and split 24 times via 'input().split()', and both pieces are converted to float with map(float, ...). The float conversions dominate cost at this tiny N and are unnecessary if IDs are integers and times can be kept as strings or parsed minimally.

The manual selection logic uses deeply nested if/elif blocks on every competitor. In CPython, each branch and assignment is executed at the Python level, adding overhead compared to a vectorized or C-level operation such as sort.

State is kept in eight scalar variables (var_1, var_2, var_3, var_4, var_8, var_9, var_11, var_12). The book-keeping to maintain top-2 per heat and global top-2 among remaining is intricate, making the code hard to verify and optimize, and easy to get wrong.

The algorithm is asymptotically optimal (selection in O(N)), but N is only 24; Python overhead from many small operations outweighs any advantage over simply sorting small lists with a C-implemented sort.

delta_type: constant-factor optimization via shifting work to built-in C-level operations and simplifying control flow
at the cost of slightly worse asymptotic complexity in a generalized model.

complexity_delta.from: O(R*M) time, O(1) space dominated by Python-loop-based selection, float conversions, and function calls.
complexity_delta.to: O(R*M log M) time, O(M+R) space in a generalized sense, but with most heavy work in C-level sorted and list operations; for the fixed 3×8 input this is still O(1) and typically faster in practice.
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotically, manual selection is better (O(R*M) vs O(R*M log M)), but for tiny, fixed M and R and in CPython, reducing Python-level operations and using sorted often yields better wall-clock performance despite the theoretical n log n factor.

trigger_signals: Inner loops dominated by Python-level control flow (if/elif) and multiple assignments for every input element.
Frequent calls to a tiny helper function inside the tightest loop, which is a red flag for unnecessary function call overhead.
Use of global variables for what is essentially local or aggregating state, complicating reasoning and preventing local-variable optimizations.
Per-element float conversions and formatted printing when the problem is small and ordering might be achievable directly on strings.
Recognizing that the per-group size is fixed and small (M=8), making full sorting of each group a cheap operation that can leverage optimized C routines.
transformation_steps: Replace streaming, per-element minimum tracking with a per-group accumulation of all M=8 records into a list.
Use a list comprehension with input().split()[::-1] to read and pre-transform each record in one expression, reducing Python-level overhead per element.
Apply sorted() once per group to rank all 8 records at C speed, and obtain the top-2 and next-2 using simple slicing instead of nested conditionals and helper calls.
Accumulate second-tier candidates from each group into a single list and use a final sorted() call to select the global top-2 rather than maintaining a global min/second-min via a stateful helper.
Simplify output generation by iterating directly over the final selected list and using ' '.join(var_2[::-1]) instead of formatted numeric printing, avoiding repeated formatting overhead.

bottlenecks.detail: Manual element-by-element selection of minima is implemented with Python-level loops, comparisons, and branches (if/elif), which carry high interpreter overhead per operation compared to C-level implementations.
The helper func_1 is called frequently inside the inner loop to update global minima. Each call adds Python function call overhead for a very small amount of work.

Each input pair is converted to floats via list(map(float, input().split())), even though input size is tiny and numeric conversion is relatively expensive compared to string handling. If only ordering is needed, staying in string domain or minimizing conversions could be cheaper.

The helper uses globals (declares different global names than the actual state vars), which not only appears buggy but also makes attribute resolution slower and harder for Python to optimize compared to local variables.
Printing uses old-style string formatting with % and format specifiers for each line, which adds extra work per output compared to simpler joins or direct printing when formatting is not critical.

complexity_delta.from: O(N log N) time, O(N) space
complexity_delta.to: O(N log N) time, O(N) space
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotic complexity unchanged; performance gains come from reduced interpreter overhead (I/O, sorting key, loop exit behavior), which is crucial near tight contest time limits for large N.

trigger_signals: Runtime close to or exceeding time limits on large N despite an O(N log N) algorithm, hinting at Python interpreter overhead rather than algorithmic complexity.
Profile or contest feedback showing that the sort + loop region is hot but algorithmically optimal already.
Use of lambda as sort key and multiple thin wrapper functions on the critical input-reading path, both of which are common constant-factor offenders in Python.
Workload with many lines of input and no opportunity to amortize I/O overhead, making efficient use of readline and minimal parsing essential.
transformation_steps: Bind sys.stdin.readline to a local variable (var_5) to avoid repeated attribute lookups and indirection via helper functions for each line read.
Inline integer parsing inside the list comprehension: use list(map(int, var_5().rstrip().split())) instead of calling func_3, eliminating an extra Python function call per line.
Replace sort(key=lambda var_8: var_8[1]) with sort(key=itemgetter(1)) to use a faster C-implemented key function and avoid lambda overhead.
Move all work into a dedicated function func_1 with a local scope to slightly speed up variable access (locals are faster than globals in CPython).
Introduce early termination: when cumulative time exceeds the current deadline (var_6 > var_4), print("No") and return immediately instead of scanning the remaining tasks.
Remove generic/unneeded parsing helpers from the hot path so that only the minimal logic needed for this problem executes.

bottlenecks.detail: Input is read through sys.stdin with multiple small wrapper functions (func_3, func_7, etc.). Each call to func_3 incurs a Python function call + list() construction overhead on hot path, instead of an inlined parse around a locally bound readline.
sort(key=lambda var_8: var_8[1]) uses a Python lambda as key, which is slightly slower than using operator.itemgetter(1) due to per-call overhead and attribute lookup.

Even after detecting a missed deadline (var_5 > var_4), the code only flips var_3 = False and continues iterating over all remaining tasks. No break/return, so worst-case always processes all N elements.

Several input helpers (func_2, func_4, func_5, func_6, func_8, func_1) are defined but unused. While they do not affect runtime after import, they indicate an over-generic template and distract from optimizing the main hot path.

Accessing var_7.readline through the sys.stdin object each time is marginally slower than binding readline to a local variable (e.g., var_5 = sys.stdin.readline), which avoids repeated attribute lookups in tight loops.

complexity_delta.from: Time: O(n log n), Space: O(n)
complexity_delta.to: Time: O(n log n), Space: O(n)
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotic complexity is identical, but the modified fast_code significantly reduces constant factors via faster I/O and less overhead, which is critical for N up to around 2e5–3e5.

trigger_signals: Use of eval(input()) for numeric parsing, which is a known red flag for performance and safety in competitive programming.
Use of input() in a loop with potentially large N (e.g., 1e5–2e5) where faster I/O is typically required.
Presence of many unused imports and helper functions from a generic template, indicating template bloat and unnecessary overhead.
Function names being reused as variables (func_2 => list), suggesting ad-hoc edits and potential for hidden inefficiencies.
Problem pattern matching: task scheduling with sort + linear scan is standard; if such a solution TLEs, the I/O layer is often the culprit, not the algorithm.
transformation_steps: Replace input() + eval() with fast, direct reading: use sys.stdin.readline and int() / map(int, ...) instead of eval(input()).
Inline input parsing into the main solving function instead of multiple thin wrapper functions that add call overhead.
Read the number of tasks once via readline, then use a list comprehension to read all remaining lines efficiently.
Use tuples (duration, deadline) and sort with itemgetter(1) to make the sorting key explicit and marginally faster than default comparison.
Remove unused imports and generic helpers (math, itertools, bisect, copy, deque, Counter, Decimal, gcd wrapper) to reduce startup and mental overhead.
Avoid shadowing function names with variables (e.g., func_2) to keep code clearer and reduce confusion when refactoring for performance.
Return from the function on failure instead of calling exit(), keeping control flow local and easier to reason about.

bottlenecks.detail: Uses input() for every line, which is significantly slower than sys.stdin.readline() for large N.
func_4 uses int(eval(input())) instead of a direct int(input()); eval introduces substantial overhead and potential safety issues without any benefit here.
Multiple tiny wrapper functions (func_1, func_2, etc.) around input(). For large N, repeated function calls add non-trivial overhead compared to an inlined read/parse loop.
func_2 is first defined as a function, then reused as a list variable; while Python allows this, it harms readability and can confuse maintenance and optimization.
Imports of math, itertools, bisect, copy, deque, Counter, Decimal are unused; they add minor startup overhead and indicate template bloat.
Using exit() (intended for interactive use) instead of sys.exit() is slightly less explicit; performance impact is small but it is non-idiomatic in competitive scripts.

complexity_delta.from: O(N log N) time, O(N) space
complexity_delta.to: O(N log N) time, O(N) space
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotic complexity is unchanged, but I/O and parsing overhead are reduced substantially, which is critical under tight time limits.

trigger_signals: Use of input() in a large loop where N can be up to around 1e5–2e5.
Use of eval(input()) for integer input, which is both slow and unnecessary.
Multiple small I/O wrapper functions called per iteration in the hot path.
Problem pattern (greedy scheduling with sort) typically appearing in contests with tight Python time limits, where I/O dominates.
transformation_steps: Replace eval(input()) and input() with sys.stdin.readline-based reading to accelerate I/O.
Remove small wrapper functions for reading input (func_4, func_1, etc.) and parse directly in the main code to avoid per-line function call overhead.
Eliminate use of eval for numeric parsing; use int(...) and map(int, ...) directly.
Construct the list of (deadline, time) pairs using a list comprehension and sort it once, instead of appending via a loop with extra function calls.
Remove unused imports and global constants that are not required for the actual algorithm to reduce startup and cognitive overhead.

bottlenecks.detail: Uses built-in input() repeatedly in a loop, which is significantly slower than sys.stdin.readline for large N.
Uses eval(input()) to parse integers, which is much slower than int(...) and unsafe; also wrapped in extra functions (func_4, func_1) called per line.
Each line read goes through multiple Python function calls (func_4, func_1, etc.), adding overhead inside the tight O(N) loop.
Imports many unused modules (math, itertools, bisect, etc.) and sets a large recursion limit; these do not affect asymptotic complexity but add minor startup overhead.

complexity_delta.from: O(n log n) time, O(n) space (baseline greedy scheduling with overhead from lambdas, extra list copies, and no early exit).
complexity_delta.to: O(n log n) time, O(n) space (same asymptotics, but with lower constant factors due to faster I/O, more efficient data structures, and early termination).
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotic complexity is unchanged, but per-element and per-line overhead is reduced significantly, which matters for large n near contest limits.

trigger_signals: Presence of many tiny wrapper functions around sys.stdin.readline with no algorithmic benefit.
Redundant list(list(...)) construction indicating unnecessary copying of input rows.
Use of a Python lambda as sort key in a critical O(n log n) section where itemgetter is preferable.
Loop continues after the result is known, doing extra comparisons when a violation has already been found.
Contest context suggests large n; any avoidable per-element or per-line overhead can risk TLE even with correct asymptotics.
transformation_steps: I/O optimization: Bind sys.stdin.readline to a local name (var_5) and use it directly instead of multiple wrapper functions and attribute lookups.
Remove unused helpers: Delete func_2, func_4, func_5, func_6, func_1, and the unnecessary recursion limit adjustment, reducing startup and call overhead and clarifying the data flow.

Avoid redundant copies: Read each pair with tuple(map(int, var_5().rstrip().split())) instead of list(func_3()) where func_3 already builds a list, eliminating an extra list construction per line and favoring tuples for immutable records.
Faster sort key: Replace key=lambda var_8: var_8[1] with key=itemgetter(1) to cut per-element key-function overhead during sorting.
Early termination: Inside the loop, print 'No' and return immediately when var_6 > var_4, avoiding unnecessary work on the remaining tasks once infeasibility is detected.
Control structure cleanup: Wrap the main logic in func_1 and guard it with if __name__ == '__main__': for clearer execution entry and slightly reduced module-level side effects.

bottlenecks.detail: Multiple small wrapper functions (func_3, func_8, func_7, etc.) around sys.stdin.readline add Python-level call overhead for every line read. var_7.readline() is not locally bound to a shorter name for faster attribute access.
Each row is built as list(func_3()) while func_3 already returns a list; this creates an unnecessary second list object per input line.
Sorting uses key=lambda var_8: var_8[1], which invokes a Python lambda for each element; using operator.itemgetter(1) is slightly faster and more memory-friendly.
The loop continues even after detecting a violation (var_5 > var_4). This wastes time on remaining iterations when the answer is already known to be 'No'.
Several unused helpers (func_2, func_4, func_5, func_6, func_1) and sys.setrecursionlimit(10**7) are defined or called despite recursion not being used, adding minor startup overhead and noise.

complexity_delta.from: O(n log n) with high Python-level overhead in sort key, allocations, and IO wrappers
complexity_delta.to: O(n log n) with reduced per-element overhead and tighter inner loops
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotic complexity is unchanged, but performance improves significantly for large n due to fewer Python calls and allocations and a more efficient sort key.

trigger_signals: Time limit exceeded or near-timeout when n is at the upper constraints (e.g., ~2e5) despite an O(n log n) algorithm.
Profiling or reasoning shows most time spent in `list.sort` with a Python lambda key and in allocation-heavy parsing.
Recognizing common competitive-programming patterns: replacing lambda sort keys with `itemgetter`, minimizing per-line parsing overhead, and avoiding unnecessary data copies.
Awareness that /online-judge Python solutions for scheduling/sorting problems often require these micro-optimizations to pass.
transformation_steps: Replace the generic stdin handle and wrapper stack (`var_7`, `func_7`, `func_3`, etc.) with a single fast alias `var_5 = sys.stdin.readline`, minimizing function-call overhead for each input line.

Avoid redundant list-to-tuple copying: in fast_code, parse each line directly into a list of ints using `list(map(int, var_5().rstrip().split()))` and keep it as a list; there is no extra tuple creation.
Switch the sort key from a Python lambda (`lambda var_8: var_8[1]`) to `operator.itemgetter(1)`, which is implemented in C and significantly faster when called for each element during sorting.
Move the main logic into a function (`func_1`) and use local variables heavily, allowing CPython to use faster local variable access instead of repeated global lookups.

Simplify the success/failure logic: fast_code performs an early `print("No")` and `return` as soon as a violation is detected, which can save work on negative instances; slow_code uses a flag and breaks, then prints once at the end.

bottlenecks.detail: slow_code uses `var_1.sort(key=lambda var_8: var_8[1])`. The lambda is a Python-level function invoked n times to compute keys, adding noticeable per-element overhead compared to a C-implemented key like `operator.itemgetter(1)`.

Each job line is read via `func_3()` which returns a list of ints, and then immediately wrapped in `tuple(func_3())`, copying the list to a tuple for every line. This doubles allocation and element copying for all n lines.

Input is accessed through multiple small wrapper functions (`func_3`, `func_7`, etc.) that each call `var_7.readline()` and then process it. The extra Python call layers add overhead in tight loops for large n.

All logic runs at import time (top-level code) instead of inside a dedicated function. This mildly increases global lookups and may hurt performance versus local-variable-optimized function scope, though this effect is smaller than the sort and allocation costs.

complexity_delta.note: Both versions implement the same greedy scheduling algorithm with identical asymptotic complexity. Differences are purely in constant factors and code structure.

trigger_signals: Presence of a sort over all tasks indicates the unavoidable O(n log n) core cost.
Use of small wrapper functions around every readline() suggests potential per-iteration overhead compared to inlined parsing.
Storage of all input records in memory at once signals O(n) space consumption and lack of streaming processing.
transformation_steps: Replaced direct input() reading with sys.stdin.readline-based helpers (func_3, func_7), wrapped in small functions.
Switched from building a list of lists via an explicit for-loop with append to building a list of tuples via a list comprehension.
Replaced an immediate print-and-exit pattern with a flag-based approach and a single final print.
Introduced several generic input helper functions that are unused in this solution and set a high recursion limit, which is irrelevant to this problem.

bottlenecks.detail: Use of sort on all n jobs by deadline gives O(n log n); this is optimal for this straightforward greedy solution and is the true complexity driver.

Per-line parsing goes through a small wrapper function func_3(), adding an extra Python function call per input line on top of readline() and map(int, ...). For very large n this can add measurable overhead versus inlining parsing.

All jobs are stored as tuples in a list before processing. This is standard but requires O(n) memory and can impact cache locality for very large n. There is no streaming/online processing to reduce the footprint.

Multiple unused helper functions (func_1, func_2, func_4, func_5, func_6, func_8) and an unnecessary sys.setrecursionlimit call introduce a small one-time overhead and make the code heavier, although they do not change asymptotic complexity.

complexity_delta.from: O(N) time, O(N) space with higher Python-level overhead (per-element input helper calls, branches, and unused imports/functions).
complexity_delta.to: O(N) time, O(N) space with reduced constant factors via bulk input, C-level iteration, and a tighter loop.
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotic complexity is unchanged; the improvements mainly speed up large inputs by reducing Python overhead and simplifying the hot path.

trigger_signals: Presence of generic competitive-programming template code (many imports and helper functions) while only a small subset is actually needed.
Input values being read via a helper function called repeatedly inside loops (N calls to sys.stdin.readline) instead of using bulk reading patterns.
A tight loop that performs simple arithmetic but includes Python-level branching and list mutations that could be expressed more compactly.
Need to pass large test cases close to time limits where Python interpreter overhead dominates over the arithmetic itself.
transformation_steps: Replace repeated calls to func_1() (sys.stdin.readline + int) with a single bulk read: var_2, *var_1 = list(map(int, open(0))). This moves iteration and conversion into C-level code and removes custom input helpers.
Inline the core logic into the top level instead of wrapping in func_7(), eliminating extra Python function call overhead in the hot path.
Iterate over adjacent elements using zip(var_1, var_1[1:]) rather than manual index-based loops, simplifying the loop and pushing iteration into C-level constructs.

Use divmod(var_3 - var_6, 2) to compute both quotient (number of within-pile pairs, adjusted by previous carry) and remainder (leftover) in one operation, reducing Python instructions and keeping the loop branch-light.
Track cross-pair potential with a single carry variable (var_6) rather than modifying the next element in the list, avoiding extra loads/stores and making memory access more linear.
Drop unused imports and helper functions in the solution path, reducing module import time and code size, even though this does not materially affect big-O behavior.

bottlenecks.detail: Input is read via func_1() repeatedly inside Python loops (N calls to sys.stdin.readline + int conversion), instead of a single bulk read + C-level map. This increases constant factors for large N.
The core logic is wrapped in func_7(), and reading each integer calls func_1(); repeated Python-level function calls in tight loops add noticeable overhead compared to inlined or C-level iteration.

Multiple unused functions (func_3, func_4, func_5, func_6, func_8–func_11) and heavy imports (bisect, itertools, heapq, math, random, deepcopy, Counter, defaultdict, deque) are present. They cost some import/definition time and memory, though they do not affect asymptotic complexity.

The pair-count loop uses an if condition for each element to decide whether to form a cross-pair with the next element. This branch may be slightly more expensive than a branch-light formulation; still O(1) per element.

complexity_delta.from: Time: O(T), Space: O(1) with high constant factors due to input() and helper calls.
complexity_delta.to: Time: O(T), Space: O(1) with reduced constant factors via sys.stdin.readline() and inline logic.
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotic complexity remains linear in the number of lines, but performance improves substantially in CP judges because IO dominates runtime.

trigger_signals: Problem is dominated by simple per-line processing with potentially large input size.
Use of input() inside a tight loop in Python, a known performance bottleneck for up to 1e5+ lines.
Unnecessary helper function calls in a very small, frequently executed code path.
Online judge context where Python solutions using input() often TLE or run close to time limits on IO-heavy tasks.
transformation_steps: Replace input() with sys.stdin.readline() to speed up line reading for many iterations.
Inline the swap logic by directly comparing var_1 and var_2 and printing them in the correct order, removing func_1 and its call overhead.
Use map(int, ...) to convert both integers in one expression, simplifying parsing code.
Adopt an explicit infinite loop with a sentinel check (break when '0 0') for clearer control flow and slightly less overhead.
Avoid extra temporary variables for swapping and avoid re-reading/parsing before and inside the loop separately.

bottlenecks.detail: Uses built-in input(), which is significantly slower than sys.stdin.readline() for large numbers of lines due to additional safety and decoding overhead.
Each pair is passed through func_1, adding Python function call overhead in the tight loop for a trivial conditional swap.
Manual swap with a temporary variable var_4 instead of a direct comparison-and-print, plus repeated re-parsing logic inside the loop (int conversion duplicated around the loop condition).

Loop condition uses while (0 != var_1) or (0 != var_2), which is slightly less direct than a sentinel check inside the loop; also does an extra read before entering the loop, leading to more scattered logic.

complexity_delta.from: Time: O(N log N), Space: O(N)
complexity_delta.to: Time: O(N log N), Space: O(N)
complexity_delta.note: The asymptotic bounds are identical; improvements come from reduced Python-level overhead (fewer function calls, direct readline alias, early exit) and a slightly more specific sort key, which may help with worst-case constant factors but does not change big-O.

trigger_signals: Problem constraints suggest N can be large (≈1e5–2e5), making Python implementations sensitive to constant factors around sorting and I/O.
The original solution already uses the correct O(N log N) algorithm but may be close to time limits on heavy test data, prompting micro-optimizations rather than algorithm changes.
Profiling or intuition for similar tasks reveals input reading and sort-key application as dominant costs, so reducing function call overhead and early termination in failing cases are natural steps.
transformation_steps: Replaced multiple small input helper functions with a direct alias to sys.stdin.readline (var_4 = sys.stdin.readline) to avoid repeated Python function call overhead for simple operations.

Simplified reading of N: var_6 = func_7() became var_5 = int(eval(var_4())). This removes a wrapper call but introduces eval; using int(var_4()) would be simpler and safer while preserving the benefit of the readline alias.
Constructed the job list in a single expression using the readline alias and map(int, ...): var_1 = [list(map(int, var_4().split())) for _ in range(var_5)]. This removes the func_3 indirection.

Adjusted the sort key from sorting by deadline only (key=lambda var_8: var_8[1]) to sorting by (deadline, duration) (key=lambda var_7: (var_7[1], var_7[0])). This adds a deterministic tie-breaker but keeps the same optimal greedy structure and asymptotic cost.

Changed the loop logic to perform the violation check before updating the cumulative time fully in the stored variable, and to exit immediately on the first violation: if var_6 + var_2 > var_3: print('No'); exit(). This avoids unnecessary iterations in failing instances and removes the need for a boolean flag.
Simplified the final output to unconditional print('Yes') at the end of the loop when no early exit has occurred, dropping the ternary print and boolean state.

bottlenecks.detail: Sorting N jobs with a Python key function is inherently O(N log N); for large N (≈2e5), the sort dominates total runtime. This is necessary for the greedy approach, so it cannot be removed but defines the performance envelope.

Multiple tiny wrapper functions (func_1–func_8) create small overhead via extra Python function calls, though only func_3 and func_7 are actually used. This adds call overhead for every read and parse.

Using var_7 = sys.stdin and then var_7.readline() is fine, but not aliasing readline directly (e.g., var_4 = sys.stdin.readline) misses a micro-optimization where the function lookup is slightly cheaper.

The final decision logic keeps a flag (var_3) for the entire loop and prints once at the end instead of exiting early when a violation is found, which can waste some iterations in negative cases (though still O(N)).

complexity_delta.from: O(N log N) time, O(N) space
complexity_delta.to: O(N log N) time, O(N) space
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotic complexity is unchanged. Early termination can reduce the linear scan cost in failing cases, but sorting still dominates. Use of eval(input()) may actually increase constant factors.

trigger_signals: Observation that once any prefix sum of durations exceeds the current deadline, the final answer must be "No"; continuing the loop is unnecessary.
Desire to simplify and shorten the code by removing unused helper I/O functions and flags.
Refactoring towards more idiomatic Python constructs (for-else, sorted) while preserving the greedy scheduling logic.
transformation_steps: Replaced custom stdin wrappers (sys.stdin + helper functions) with direct input() calls and map(int, ...).
Read the number of jobs using eval(input()) instead of manually parsing via sys.stdin.readline.
Constructed the job list directly with a list comprehension over input().
Used sorted(var_2, key=lambda var_6: var_6[1]) instead of in-place list.sort with the same key; functionality is equivalent.
Changed the verification loop to print "No" and break immediately when var_5 > var_3 (deadline missed), instead of setting a flag and finishing the loop.
Used a for-else construct to print "Yes" only if the loop completes without encountering a deadline violation.

bottlenecks.detail: After detecting a violation (var_5 > var_4), the loop continues through the remaining jobs instead of terminating early. This wastes up to O(N) extra work in failing cases, though sorting still dominates asymptotically.
Multiple tiny wrapper functions around sys.stdin.readline add some overhead and obscurity, though they are not asymptotic bottlenecks.
Input is already using sys.stdin.readline, which is efficient. No real I/O bottleneck beyond the unavoidable O(N) reads.

complexity_delta.from: O(N log N) time, O(N) space (with heavy constant factors due to imports, wrappers, eval)
complexity_delta.to: O(N log N) time, O(N) space (lean implementation with smaller constants)
complexity_delta.note: Both solutions use the same greedy + sort algorithm; improvements are primarily in constant factors and code clarity, not in asymptotic complexity.

trigger_signals: Solution includes many unused generic utilities and imports typical of a personal template but unnecessary for the specific problem.
Use of eval() for simple integer input parsing.
Multiple thin wrapper functions around input() and list(map(int, ...)) used only once or twice.
Variable names reused for different purposes (function names reused as lists or loop counters).
Tight time limits where Python startup and I/O overheads matter, especially in online judges with many small tests.
transformation_steps: Remove unused imports (math, itertools, bisect, copy, collections, decimal) to reduce startup time and memory usage.
Inline input parsing into a list comprehension: var_2 = [list(map(int, input().split())) for _ in range(var_4)] instead of per-line helper functions and append in a loop.
Avoid reassigning function names (func_2, func_4) to local variables to prevent confusion and potential bugs.
Sort directly on the appropriate key (deadline) using sorted(var_2, key=lambda x: x[1]) instead of reordering pair structure and sorting by default.
Replace exit()-style early termination with a for-else construct that is idiomatic and avoids abruptly exiting the interpreter (though both are similar performance-wise).
Drop unused helper functions (func_3, func_5, func_6, func_7) to minimize definition overhead and improve readability.
Optionally (not shown in fast_code but recommended), replace int(eval(input())) with int(input()) to avoid eval overhead and security risk.

bottlenecks.detail: Each line of input is read via a wrapper function (func_1) and then processed, adding a function-call overhead per line compared to a direct comprehension.

Multiple heavy modules (math, itertools, bisect, collections, decimal, copy) are imported but unused. This increases startup time and memory footprint, especially noticeable in short-running programs.
Several helper functions (func_1–func_7, func_6) are defined but unused in the core logic, adding minor but unnecessary overhead at module load time and cognitive overhead for maintainers.
func_4 uses int(eval(input())), which is slower and less safe than int(input()). While used only once, it still introduces unnecessary overhead and potential security risk.

Variable names are reused for different purposes (e.g., func_4 reused as loop index, func_2 first as function then as list), which can confuse readers and increase the risk of subtle bugs, though it does not change asymptotic complexity.

complexity_delta.from: O(N log N) time, O(N) space
complexity_delta.to: O(N log N) time, O(N) space (with potential early-exit savings in practice)
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotic complexity is unchanged; only constant factors and best-case behavior differ.

trigger_signals: Desire to terminate quickly when an infeasible schedule is detected, improving best-case performance.
Attempt to simplify logic by printing answers directly in the loop instead of using a separate boolean state variable.
Possibly a misconception that adding a tie-break in the sort key materially improves complexity; in reality, it only affects constant factors and ordering of equal-deadline jobs.
transformation_steps: Replaced sys.stdin.readline()-based input helpers with direct input() calls and a single int(eval(input())) for N.

Changed sort key from key=lambda var_8: var_8[1] to key=lambda var_6: (var_6[1], var_6[0]) to use duration as a tie-breaker on equal deadlines. This does not affect worst-case complexity and is not required for correctness in this standard problem.

Reworked the verification loop to check if var_5 + var_2 > var_3 then print("No") and exit() immediately, enabling early termination on failure instead of completing the full loop and tracking a boolean flag.
Removed unused utility functions and sys.setrecursionlimit, slightly reducing overhead and code noise.

bottlenecks.detail: Full sort of all jobs by deadline is unavoidable for this greedy strategy; this is the theoretical lower bound for comparison-based ordering.
Per-element lambda allocation during sort (key=lambda var_8: var_8[1]) adds minor overhead but is standard; not a big issue vs Timsort cost.
Although sys.stdin.readline() is already efficient, repeated conversions via helper wrappers add small function-call overhead for each line.

complexity_delta.from: O(n log n) time, O(n) space
complexity_delta.to: O(n log n) time, O(n) space
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotic complexity is unchanged; all modifications affect constant factors, safety, and style rather than big-O. In fact, some changes (eval, input) may worsen constants compared to sys.stdin.

trigger_signals: Need to implement a known greedy scheduling solution using sorting by deadlines (already present in the slow code).
Desire to simplify control flow and avoid post-loop state checks by using early exit when a constraint is violated.
Attempt to write a shorter, more direct script-style solution without utility I/O functions.
transformation_steps: Input handling changed from sys.stdin.readline wrappers to direct input() calls, with n parsed via int(eval(input())).
Job storage changed from list of tuples to list of lists, which has negligible impact on performance here.

Sort key changed from key=lambda x: x[1] (deadline only) to key=lambda x: (x[1], x[0]) (deadline, then processing time). This only affects tie-breaking; feasibility is unaffected because jobs with equal deadlines can be in any order for this check.
Loop logic changed from maintaining a boolean flag and breaking, then printing based on the flag, to an early exit() on the first violation and an unconditional 'Yes' at the end otherwise.
Removed unused helper functions and recursion-limit setting, simplifying the code but not affecting runtime meaningfully.

bottlenecks.detail: Sorting the n jobs by deadline with list.sort is the primary cost at O(n log n); this is inherent to the greedy strategy.
Multiple small helper wrappers around sys.stdin.readline add minor function-call overhead, but sys.stdin itself is efficient and usually faster than bare input().
Building tuples for each job and using a lambda key on sort incur per-element overhead, but they do not change the overall complexity.

complexity_delta.from: O(N log N) time, O(N) space
complexity_delta.to: O(N log N) time, O(N) space
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotic complexity is unchanged, but constants are likely worse in the modified code due to using eval(input()) and plain input() instead of buffered sys.stdin, plus an unnecessary deque-to-list conversion.

trigger_signals: Desire to avoid an explicit key=lambda var_8: var_8[1] in sort, possibly under the misconception that this is slower than sorting by default order of transformed pairs.
Attempt to simplify the control flow by using early exit instead of carrying a boolean flag.
Refactoring towards more straightforward-looking code using input() rather than low-level sys.stdin.readline.
transformation_steps: Replaced sys.stdin.readline-based input helpers with direct input() calls and eval(input()) for the first integer.
Switched representation of each job from [duration, deadline] to [deadline, duration] to rely on default sort order instead of a key function.
Introduced a deque to collect jobs, then converted it back to a list before sorting.
Changed the validation loop from using a boolean flag and printing at the end to early exit on failure via print('No'); exit().

bottlenecks.detail: Although using sys.stdin.readline is already fast, the numerous unused helper wrappers add tiny overhead in readability but negligible runtime cost. Core I/O is appropriate for large N.
Sorting with key=lambda x: x[1] introduces a key-function call per comparison; this is still efficient and idiomatic in Python and not a real bottleneck at this scale.
No algorithmic bottleneck beyond the necessary O(N log N) sort; the algorithm is already optimal for this greedy scheduling check.

delta_type: Constant-factor optimization via faster I/O and streamlined implementation (same asymptotic complexity).

complexity_delta.from: O(n log n) time, O(n) space
complexity_delta.to: O(n log n) time, O(n) space
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotic complexity is unchanged; improvements are in constant factors and practical runtime: faster I/O, less overhead, and cleaner control flow.

trigger_signals: Use of eval(input()) in a competitive-programming style problem suggests both safety and performance issues.
Reliance on input() for potentially large N where sys.stdin.readline() is typically recommended.
Presence of many unused heavy imports and generic helper functions that are not relevant to this specific task.
Name shadowing (functions reused as variables) indicating auto-generated or template-based code rather than a minimal, tuned solution.
Typical constraints for scheduling/deadline problems (N up to around 1e5–2e5) make I/O and function-call overhead significant compared to core O(n log n) logic.
transformation_steps: Replaced eval(input()) and input() with sys.stdin.readline() and direct int / map(int, ...) parsing to reduce I/O overhead.
Removed unnecessary helper functions wrapping input(), avoiding extra Python function-call overhead inside tight input loops.
Eliminated unused imports (math, itertools, bisect, copy, deque, Counter, Decimal), reducing startup and namespace clutter.
Stopped shadowing function names with variables (e.g., func_2 as both function and list, func_4 reused as loop index), improving clarity and avoiding potential bugs.
Used list comprehension to read all (duration, deadline) pairs directly into a list in a compact, efficient manner.
Used list.sort(key=itemgetter(1)) on (time, deadline) pairs instead of swapping the order manually, keeping semantics clear and avoiding extra indexing.
Replaced sys.exit() inside the loop with a simple return from the main function, making control flow more explicit and slightly reducing overhead.

bottlenecks.detail: Uses input() for every read and eval(input()) for the first integer; eval adds parsing overhead and is significantly slower and unsafe compared to int(sys.stdin.readline()).
Multiple thin wrapper functions (func_1, func_2, func_4, etc.) around input()/eval() add extra Python-level calls for each line of input.
Imports many heavy modules (math, itertools, bisect, copy, collections, decimal) that are never used; while not asymptotically costly, it adds startup overhead and noise.
Using eval(input()) to parse an integer is not only slow but also insecure; it forces Python to parse arbitrary expressions rather than simple numeric strings.
Reusing function names as variables (func_2 list, func_4 loop variable) makes the code harder to reason about and maintain, increasing risk of subtle bugs and making performance tuning harder.

top_delta_type: Replace per-query incremental trial-division prime generation and primality testing with a one-time precomputation of all primes up to a fixed maximum using a sieve-like method
algorithm_change (sieve + Legendre exponent summation -> per-number trial factorization with aggregation)
algorithmic reformulation with a different cost profile (fewer factorizations but on a vastly larger integer
top_complexity_delta: Approx. O(N^(3/2)) time with precomputed primes and dictionary-based aggregation; O(N) space.->O(N^2) time with naive nested trial division and array-based aggregation; O(N) space.

Conceptually about O(n log n) trial divisions and dict operations in Python for factorizing each k in [2, n], plus small fixed sieve cost.->O(n log log n) primes and divisions mostly handled in optimized C: a single math.factorial call and O(π(n)) loops in Python over primes. Asymptotic bit-level complexity is worse due to big-int operations, but Python-level operation count is far smaller for n ≤ 1000.

O(L log log L + n * pi(sqrt(n))) Python-level operations with many nested loops and function calls (slow_code).->Mostly C-level big-integer work: factorial computation and a single factorization of n! by trial division; effective Python-level work is closer to O(n log log n) iterations (fast_code), though the underlying arithmetic complexity in terms of bit operations is roughly O(n^2 log n).

O(N * sqrt N / log N) factoring using primes + O(M log log M) sieve, dominated by many Python dict creations and updates->O(N * sqrt N) trial division using integers, but with aggregation pushed into C via Counter and far fewer Python objects
O(N log log N + N) time, O(N) space (sieve + exponent summation)->O(N^(3/2)) time, O(N log log N) space (trial factorization for each k in [2, N])
O(N log log N) time, O(N) space (sieve-based computation of exponents of primes in N!)->O(N^2) time, O(N log log N) space (per-integer full trial division and factor accumulation)

O(N^2) time with heavy constants due to repeated O(N)-sized vector operations plus an O(N^{3/2}) primality preprocessing; O(N) space.->O(N^2) time with much smaller constant factor from a single nested loop and no large per-iteration allocations; O(N) space.
O(N^2) time, O(N) space->O(N * sqrt(N)) time, O(N log N) space
O(N^2) time, O(N) space->O(N^2 / log^2 N) worst-case time (and roughly O(N log log N) successful factorizations), O(N) space

O(N^2) trial division with a single shared integer array (var_2) and only primitive arithmetic and list indexing in the hot loop.->Asymptotically better O(N^{3/2} / log N) via pre-sieved primes and per-number prime factorizations, but implemented using Python dicts, list constructions, and function calls.
top_triggers: A fixed-size array (length 10000) indexed up to a variable n indicates either a hidden correctness issue or an artificial cap; both are red flags when n may be large.
A function (func_2) that is called n times and itself contains a loop of length k with an inner sqrt(k) loop.
A function computing primality information (func_2) called inside an O(n) loop over k, with func_2 itself containing nested loops, indicates repeated heavy work.
A list of items (here, prime factors) is built only to be immediately aggregated (counted) with collections.Counter, indicating that counting during generation could remove an intermediate structure.
A main loop from 1 to n where each iteration calls a function (func_1) that itself loops up to the current index and may perform repeated division.
A primality check executed for every integer up to n using trial division, instead of a single sieve or direct use of known primes.
A primality-detection routine `func_2` that recomputes primes up to k for every k instead of using a global sieve; strong smell of avoidable recomputation.
Absence of any reuse of previously computed factorial or partial products, suggesting duplicated work.
An accumulation pattern that merges full-length arrays inside an outer loop, implying O(N^2) behavior.
Assumption that n may not be extremely large, making a simpler trial-division based solution appear acceptable despite worse asymptotic complexity.
Attempt to collapse multiple passes (factorization and aggregation) into a single nested loop structure.
Awareness that Python-level trial division and hash-map operations are comparatively slow, while math.factorial and big-int arithmetic are heavily optimized in C.
Branches involving math.sqrt that do not meaningfully reduce the work (e.g., math.sqrt(x) == 0 checks), hinting at a missed optimization opportunity.
Building a list of all prime factors, then applying collections.Counter solely to recover exponents, when exponents could be counted directly during factorization.
Code review reveals nested loops where the inner loop bounds depend linearly on the outer variable (sum of 1..n pattern), a common sign of O(n^2) behavior.
Construction of a large list of all prime factors and then passing it through collections.Counter, indicating unnecessary memory traffic and hashing overhead.
top_steps: Accept some theoretical complexity regression in the factorization loop (dropping sqrt cutoff) in exchange for simpler implementation and fewer intermediate objects.
Accumulate exponents for each prime globally in a single map/dictionary over the entire range 2..N, instead of per-k vectors and quadratic merges.
Accumulate prime exponents directly during factorization (using defaultdict(int)) instead of appending each prime factor to a list and later converting to a Counter.
Accumulate the answer as ans = ans * (e_p + 1) mod MOD across all primes, removing the need to store all individual prime factors or to count them with a Counter.
Accumulate the divisor count using modular multiplication at each step instead of building and summing length-n exponent arrays.
Accumulate the product of multiplicities (var_2 *= var_3) as a proxy for multiplying (exponent + 1) for each prime and apply the modulo only once at the end.
Accumulate the product of these counts (e_p + 1) in var_2 (and the final var_3) while keeping values within the modulo 1e9+7 at the end.
Accumulate the result as ans = ∏ (e_p + 1) mod 1_000_000_007, taking modulo after each multiplication to keep numbers small.
Accumulated all prime factors from 2..N into a single list and used `collections.Counter` to aggregate exponents per prime.
Accumulated all prime factors from 2..N into a single list var_8 and then used collections.Counter to compute exponents, instead of computing exponents via Legendre-like summations floor(N / p^k).
Added func_3 to iterate i from 2 to N, factor each i via func_2, and accumulate prime exponents into a global dict var_10, converting each per-i dict to list(dict.items()) for iteration.
Adjusted the final computation in func_1 to iterate over var_10.values(), computing the product of (exponent + 1) modulo 10^9+7, instead of iterating directly over a dense array var_2.

Adjusted the sieve marking strategy: fast_code marks multiples starting from 2*p instead of p*p, increasing the number of composite-marking assignments per prime compared with a standard optimized sieve.
After accumulating exponents, iterate over var_2, and for each exponent e > 0, multiply an accumulator by (e + 1) to obtain the total number of divisors.
After all factors are accumulated, compute the product over primes of (exponent+1) using a single pass over var_2, applying modulo 1e9+7.
After collecting all exponents e_p, compute the final answer as the product of (e_p + 1) modulo 1e9+7 in a single pass.

complexity_delta.from: O(N^{2.5}) (plus O(N^2)) time, O(N) space
complexity_delta.to: O(N log log N)–O(N log N) time, O(N) space
complexity_delta.note: The new approach replaces repeated trial-division-based primality tests and repeated factorizations with a single sieve and amortized-efficient factorizations using shared prime data.

trigger_signals: Nested loops where a primality test or trial division up to sqrt(i) is run for many values of i and again for growing prefixes.
Reconstruction of a sieve-like structure inside another loop over k (sieve inside an outer loop).
An accumulation pattern that merges full-length arrays inside an outer loop, implying O(N^2) behavior.
Total operation count grows super-quadratically when estimating Σ over loops (e.g., Σ k * sqrt(k)).
Use of general trial division for prime testing/factorization in a problem that clearly involves many factorizations up to the same upper bound N.
transformation_steps: Replace per-k primality computation (func_2 called for every k) with a single sieve of Eratosthenes up to N to get all primes once.

Avoid factoring each k from scratch via trial division up to sqrt(k); instead, factor k using the precomputed prime list, dividing by each prime as long as it divides k and updating the remaining value.
Accumulate exponents for each prime globally in a single map/dictionary over the entire range 2..N, instead of per-k vectors and quadratic merges.
After collecting all exponents e_p, compute the final answer as the product of (e_p + 1) modulo 1e9+7 in a single pass.
Eliminate the O(N) per-iteration list-comprehension aggregation; only maintain one global exponent structure.

bottlenecks.detail: func_2 recomputes primality information from scratch for every k (1..N), instead of computing primes once up to N and reusing them.
Primality is determined by trial division up to sqrt(i) for each candidate i, leading to Θ(k * sqrt(k)) per func_2(k).
Work for k includes all i ≤ k; summing this over k gives super-quadratic behavior (Σ k * sqrt(k) = Θ(N^{2.5})).
var_9 is updated via a full-length list comprehension of size N in each outer iteration over k, adding Θ(N^2) cost.
Each k is factorized independently without leveraging structure of N! (e.g., using p-adic valuations or reusing prime exponents).

complexity_delta.from: O(L log log L + n * pi(sqrt(n))) Python-level operations with many nested loops and function calls (slow_code).
complexity_delta.to: Mostly C-level big-integer work: factorial computation and a single factorization of n! by trial division; effective Python-level work is closer to O(n log log n) iterations (fast_code), though the underlying arithmetic complexity in terms of bit operations is roughly O(n^2 log n).
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotically, factoring n! directly is not superior to aggregating exponents over 2..n. However, in Python for n up to about 10**3, pushing work into math.factorial and big-int divisions in C can be empirically faster than many small Python loops and function calls.

trigger_signals: Presence of nested loops: outer loop over v in range(2, n+1) and inner loops over primes plus repeated division.
Function calls (func_3, func_2) in tight loops, which are costly in Python for small units of work.
Repeated trial-division factorization for each integer up to n instead of aggregating exponents more directly.
Use of defaultdict and list(...) constructions in hot loops, indicating extra overhead for hashing and object management.
transformation_steps: Removed the explicit sieve and per-integer factorization (func_4, func_2, func_3) that loop over 2..n and primes repeatedly.
Introduced math.factorial to compute n! in a single call, leveraging C-level optimized big-integer arithmetic.
Replaced accumulation of prime exponents across 2..n with a direct factorization of n! itself using a single while loop and trial division from 2 upwards.
Used collections.Counter to aggregate prime multiplicities from the factor list, then computed the product of (exponent + 1) modulo 1e9+7.
Eliminated defaultdict and most hash/dict updates in the inner loops, reducing Python dynamic-structure overhead.

bottlenecks.detail: func_3 iterates v from 2 to n and calls func_2(v) for each, creating many Python-level loops and function calls.
For each v, func_2 loops over primes up to sqrt(v) and performs repeated modulus and division operations; this nested structure is expensive in interpreted Python.
Instead of aggregating exponents via a direct formula for n! (e.g., Legendre’s formula), the code factorizes each integer 2..n separately, leading to redundant work.
Use of defaultdict(int) in tight loops adds hashing and dynamic dictionary operations to the critical path.

complexity_delta.from: Slow_code: sieve O(M log log M) with fixed M=10^3 (constant), plus factorization O(N * π(√N)) ≈ O(N^{3/2}/log N) but with heavy Python overhead from N calls to func_2 and per-call dict allocations.
complexity_delta.to: Fast_code: sieve O(N log log N) and naive multi-factorization O(N * π(N)) ~ O(N^2/log N) in theory, but implemented in a single tight nested loop with shared data structures and minimal allocations; for N≈10^3 this is significantly faster in practice.
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotically, the arithmetic work in slow_code is not worse and could even look better on paper, but the implementation introduces substantial Python-level overhead. Fast_code trades a slightly heavier theoretical bound for a much cheaper constant factor, which dominates at the small N of this problem.

trigger_signals: Profiling or timing shows most runtime in func_2 and in defaultdict/dict construction, not in the numerical operations themselves.
Observed slowdown or TLE at upper bound even though algorithmic complexity looks acceptable on paper, indicating dominant constant factors.
High call counts to small helper functions (func_2, func_3) visible in a profiler, each doing small work but incurring substantial overhead.
Memory allocations (new dicts, list() calls) grow linearly with N despite the final result only needing aggregate counts.
transformation_steps: Eliminated per-number factorization helper func_2 and its repeated defaultdict returns; integrated factorization directly into the main loop over k in [2..N].
Switched from building a new temporary dict for each k and merging into a global dict to updating a single global defaultdict var_5 in-place as factors are found.
Removed list(...).items() wrapping: in fast_code, factor counts are iterated directly from a single dict at the end, avoiding per-iteration copies.
Unified the prime generation: a single sieve up to N produces var_3 once, reused for all factorizations, instead of using a fixed-prime set and a factorization helper with its own loop.

Reduced function-call depth and improved locality: all core work (sieve, factorization, combination) is in one script scope with simple loops, which executes more efficiently in CPython for these constraints.

bottlenecks.detail: func_2 is called for every k in [2..N], creating a new defaultdict and returning it. func_3 then immediately materializes list(func_2(k).items()). This repeated allocation and function-call overhead dwarfs the actual arithmetic for small N.
For each k, a temporary factorization dict is constructed and then iterated, even though the counts could be accumulated directly into a single global dict without per-k temporaries.
Using list(func_2(var_4).items()) copies the items instead of iterating directly over func_2(var_4).items(), adding extra allocations.
Even though per-number trial division only goes up to sqrt(k), performing it as a separate function with its own structures for each k prevents caching or reusing results and keeps overhead high.

complexity_delta.from: O(n^2) scalar operations (plus O(n^(3/2)) naive primality tests) with heavy Python-level overhead on lists and repeated factorization of each k in [1..n].
complexity_delta.to: O(n) big-integer multiplications and divisions by trial division up to n, with a single factorization of n! and linear-time aggregation of exponents.
complexity_delta.note: Asymptotically in n, the new approach reduces the combinatorial work from quadratic to roughly linear in the bound n, but introduces big-integer arithmetic cost and a large intermediate n!. For n≈10^3 this is still significantly faster in Python than repeatedly factorizing all k=1..n.

trigger_signals: Nested loops over both the range 1..n and all candidate divisors for each k, indicating O(n^2) or worse complexity.
A primality check executed for every integer up to n using trial division, instead of a single sieve or direct use of known primes.
Reallocation of a full O(n) list (var_9) on each iteration of an outer loop that runs O(n) times.
Using a factorization routine (func_1) repeatedly on many small numbers when the mathematical goal is to understand the factorization of n! as a whole.
Float division (/) on integer-like values in tight loops, which suggests both correctness and performance concerns.
transformation_steps: Changed the perspective from 'sum prime exponents over all numbers 1..n' to 'factorize the single number n! and read exponents directly'.
Removed the custom naive prime-detection func_2 and per-k factorization func_1, which both used nested loops and repeated trial division.
Introduced math.factorial to compute n! directly, avoiding the need to track exponents across many intermediate products.
Replaced manual exponent-tracking arrays of length n with a flat list of prime factors and used collections.Counter to aggregate multiplicities compactly.
Computed the number of divisors as a simple product over (exponent + 1) while maintaining the result modulo 1e9+7, eliminating repeated list rebuilding and large intermediate products.

bottlenecks.detail: func_2 tests each number up to n by trial dividing up to its square root, instead of using a proper sieve of Eratosthenes.
func_1 factorizes every k in [1..n] separately, scanning through all candidate bases up to k and dividing while possible, even though the goal is only to aggregate prime exponents for n!.
In the outer loop, var_9 = [var_9[i] + var_3[i] for i in range(n)] rebuilds an O(n) list on every iteration, adding an extra O(n^2) overhead in Python list operations.
Inside func_1, var_5 = var_5 / (var_2 + 1) uses floating-point division instead of integer division (//), which is slower and potentially unsafe for exact arithmetic on large integers.
Exponent arrays have length n and are maintained for all indices, even though only prime indices are meaningful, wasting memory traffic and cache.

complexity_delta.from: Original: Θ(n^{5/2}) elementary operations, Θ(n) space for arrays; heavy repeated primality testing and factorization across all i ≤ n.
complexity_delta.to: Modified: Θ(n) loop iterations (ignoring big-int cost) after one Θ(n) factorial build; Θ(n log n) bits of space to hold n!; much lower asymptotic count of high-level steps, but each step is a big-int operation.
complexity_delta.note: The modified approach removes the repeated sieving and per-i factorization, instead doing a single global factorization of n!. This dramatically reduces operation count in terms of n, though actual performance depends on how large n! is allowed to grow in Python.

trigger_signals: Nested loops where an inner computation (primality test) is repeated from scratch for every outer iteration, resulting in super-quadratic patterns like sum_{i=1..n} i^{3/2}.
Recomputation of the same prime information for many prefix ranges 1..i instead of computing primes up to n once.
Factorization of many consecutive integers individually rather than factorizing the aggregate structure (here n!) in a single pass or using known formulas for exponents in n!.
Observed timeouts or very slow behavior as n grows (e.g., original code will quickly degrade past a few thousand).
transformation_steps: Eliminate per-i prime sieve and factorization: remove func_2 and func_1 and the outer loop over i = 1..n that accumulated prime exponents across all i.
Directly construct n! as a single big integer using math.factorial, encapsulating the product 1·2·...·n in one object.
Factor n! by trial division from 2 upward: repeatedly divide by current divisor while divisible, counting how many times it divides (the exponent of that prime or composite factor).

Convert exponent counts into the divisor-count product: whenever the current divisor changes, multiply the running result by the accumulated exponent var_3 (serving as (e+1) in a slightly different encoding) and reset the counter.
Apply modulus only at the end (on var_2 * var_3) rather than during accumulation of exponents, since only the final divisor-count needs to be taken modulo 1e9+7.
Avoid large intermediate arrays: use O(1) scalars instead of O(n)-length arrays to track exponents and accumulated exponents across all primes.

bottlenecks.detail: func_2 performs naive primality checks for every k ≤ i via trial division up to sqrt(k), and func_2 is called separately for every i = 1..n. This is roughly Θ(n^{5/2}) total, far too slow for n in the thousands or higher.
The primality / sieve information is recomputed from scratch for each i. Primes up to i are recomputed for every i instead of computing primes once up to n and reusing them.
Each i is factored independently, instead of using a formula for exponents of primes in n! (e_p(n!) = sum floor(n/p^k)) or reusing factorization results incrementally.
func_1 allocates an array of length n for every i, even though meaningful exponents exist only up to i. This increases per-iteration memory traffic.

complexity_delta.from: Sieve-based approach: O(n log log n) Python-level operations plus O(n) integer ops for exponent summation, modest memory O(n).
complexity_delta.to: Factorial+trial-division approach: O(n) Python-level iterations, with most heavy work (factorial and big-int arithmetic) offloaded to optimized C; theoretical bit complexity is higher but practical performance is better for n up to around 10^4.
complexity_delta.note: The modified (slow) code is algorithmically more elegant for n! (Legendre exponents + sieve) and asymptotically better in terms of basic arithmetic operations, but it performs those operations in Python with nested loops. The original (fast) code pushes almost all heavy work into math.factorial and big-int division implemented in C, drastically reducing Python interpreter overhead and winning in practice for the given constraints.

trigger_signals: Nested Python for-loops whose bounds scale with n (e.g., i up to n and j over multiples of i) are a strong sign of potential slowness for n beyond about 10^5.
Manual sieve implementations in Python that touch most entries many times often become bottlenecks compared to C-implemented library functions.
A fixed-size array (length 10000) indexed up to a variable n indicates either a hidden correctness issue or an artificial cap; both are red flags when n may be large.
Repeated integer division and multiplication in a Python loop for every prime (even if only O(log n) per prime) can accumulate significant overhead when π(n) is large.
transformation_steps: Eliminate the explicit Sieve of Eratosthenes and the per-prime exponent summation loops that run in Python.
Use math.factorial(n) to compute n! directly in C, avoiding a Python loop over multiplications.
